topics.title: Architecture of PLC - Types of PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT412 - PLC & DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Architecture of PLC - Types of PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a PLC?

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a ruggedized, solid-state industrial computer specifically designed to control manufacturing processes and automated machinery in harsh industrial environments. It continuously monitors input signals from sensors, executes a user-defined logic program stored in its memory, and generates corresponding output signals to drive actuators — all in real-time, cycle after cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Alignment):**
> A PLC is a *“solid-state device capable of storing and implementing instructions for the purpose of controlling industrial equipment, processes, or machines through analog or digital signals, using the scan principle of operation.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The PLC as a Digital Reflex Brain"

Imagine a **human nervous system**:
- **Sensors (skin, eyes, ears)** detect stimuli → these are the **Input Modules** of a PLC.
- The **spinal cord and brain** process signals and decide what to do → this is the **CPU + Memory** running the Ladder Logic program.
- **Muscles and glands** act on the decision → these are the **Output Modules** driving motors, valves, and lamps.

A PLC does exactly this — *sense → think → act* — but in **milliseconds**, millions of times without fatigue, and with **zero coffee breaks** in a dusty factory.

### 1.3 Why PLCs Exist (Why Not Just Use a PC?)

| Limitation of a Personal Computer | PLC Design Solution |
|---|---|
| Dust, vibration, temperature kills motherboards | **Conformal coating**, fanless sealed chassis |
| Requires manual OS boot (Windows crash = downtime) | **Solid-state firmware**, no OS dependency |
| Wiring sensors directly to motherboard is impractical | **Screw-terminal I/O modules** for field wiring |
| Random-Access memory lost on power failure | **Battery-backed RAM + Non-volatile Flash** |
| Not designed for real-time deterministic scanning | **Hard real-time scan cycle** (deterministic) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin (Historical Note): — KTU frequently asks this:**
> The PLC was invented in **1968 by Dick Morley** at Bedford Associates, USA, originally called the **“Modicon 084”** (Modular Digital Controller). It was designed as a solid-state replacement for hard-wired relay panels used in the **Hydramatic Division of General Motors**.

### 1.4 The Three Functional Roles Inside Any PLC

Every PLC, regardless of size or brand (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Mitsubishi, Schneider), performs three universal roles:

1. **Sense Field Conditions** — through Input Modules (Limit Switches, Proximity Sensors, Thermocouples, RTDs, Encoders).
2. **Compute Decisions** — through the CPU executing a Ladder/Function Block program.
3. **Command Field Devices** — through Output Modules (Contactor coils, Solenoid valves, VFDs, Indicating lamps).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Functional Subsystems (The "PLC Pentagon")

A PLC is architecturally divided into **five distinct subsystems**. A KTU examiner expects students to label each block and state its function clearly.

#### Subsystem 1 — Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- Contains the **Microprocessor** (often 16-bit or 32-bit), **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, and **Control Unit**.
- Reads input status, executes user program instruction-by-instruction, and updates output status.
- Two internal buses: **Address Bus** and **Data Bus**.

#### Subsystem 2 — Memory Architecture
- **System Memory (ROM/Flash):** Stores the **Operating System**, **Firmware**, and the **Compiler/Interpreter** that converts Ladder Logic into machine code.
- **User Memory (RAM + Battery):** Stores the **Ladder Logic program**, **Timer/Counter preset values**, and **data registers**.
- **I/O Image Tables:** Two reserved RAM regions — the **Input Image Table (PII)** and the **Output Image Table (PIQ)**. The CPU never reads physical inputs directly during program execution; it reads the *image* (a digital snapshot).

#### Subsystem 3 — Input/Output (I/O) Module Bank
- **Digital (Discrete) Inputs:** 24 V DC, 120 V AC; senses ON/OFF states.
- **Digital Outputs:** Relay, Transistor (DC sinking/sourcing), or Triac (AC).
- **Analog Inputs:** 4–20 mA current loop, 0–10 V voltage, RTD, Thermocouple.
- **Analog Outputs:** 4–20 mA, 0–10 V to drive VFDs and control valves.
- **Specialty Modules:** High-speed counters, Encoder/Pulse-train inputs, Communication modules (Profibus, Modbus, Ethernet/IP).

#### Subsystem 4 — Power Supply Unit (PSU)
- Converts **230 V AC mains** (or 24 V DC for compact PLCs) into regulated internal voltages, typically **+5 V DC** (logic) and **+24 V DC** (sensor supply).
- Provides **isolation** between field wiring and the CPU.
- Critical: PSU sizing depends on **total I/O current draw**.

#### Subsystem 5 — Programming & Communication Device
- Historically: Hand-held programming console (HHP).
- Currently: **PC + Vendor-specific Software** (e.g., Siemens TIA Portal, Allen-Bradley Studio 5000, Schneider EcoStruxure).
- Communication protocols: **RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet, USB**.

### 2.2 The PLC Scan Cycle — The Heartbeat of Operation

This is the **single most important KTU concept** in Module 1. Every PLC repeats a four-step cycle continuously:

1. **Input Scan** — CPU reads all physical inputs and copies their states into the **Input Image Table (PII)**.
2. **Program Scan** — CPU executes the Ladder Logic instructions **top-to-bottom, left-to-right**, using values from PII (not physical inputs).
3. **Output Scan** — CPU writes the final result of the program scan into the **Output Image Table (PIQ)**; then PIQ values are transferred simultaneously to the physical output modules.
4. **Housekeeping / Overhead Tasks** — Communication processing, self-diagnostics, timer/counter value updates, servicing interrupts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern:**
> “Explain the scan cycle of a PLC with a neat diagram.” — A **4-part block diagram** with arrows showing data flow is mandatory for full marks.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{housekeeping}$ | ms | $T_{prog}$ is dominant |
| Program Scan Time | $T_{prog} = N_{inst} \times T_{per\_inst}$ | ms | $N_{inst}$ = number of instructions |
| Response Time (Min) | $T_{min} = 2 \times T_{scan}$ | ms | Input change at start of cycle |
| Response Time (Max) | $T_{max} = 3 \times T_{scan}$ | ms | Input change just after I/O scan |
| Average Response Time | $T_{avg} = 1.5 \times T_{scan}$ | ms | Standard assumption |
| Scan Time per Digital I/O | $T_{per\_inst} = 1 \, \mu s$ to $10 \, \mu s$ | µs | Vendor-specific |
| Power Supply Loading | $I_{psu} = \sum I_{input} + \sum I_{output} + I_{cpu}$ | mA | Must be $\le I_{psu\_rated}$ |
| I/O Capacity Rule | $N_{i/o} \le N_{max\_per\_CPU}$ | count | Defined by rack size |
| 4–20 mA as Percentage | $\% = \dfrac{I - 4}{20 - 4} \times 100$ | % | KTU frequently asked |
| 0–10 V as Percentage | $\% = \dfrac{V}{10} \times 100$ | % | Linear scaling |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing absolute value in tables above, remember the **Anti-Hallucination Protocol** — never fabricate vendor datasheet numbers. Use generic ranges supported by every major manufacturer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** PLCs run robotic welding cells, paint shops, and conveyor lines.
- **Water Treatment:** PLCs control pump sequencing, chemical dosing, and level control.
- **Power Plants:** PLCs manage turbine auxiliaries, ash handling, and alarm annunciation.
- **Cement, Steel, Pharma:** PLCs replace relay logic panels saving 70% wiring cost.
- **Building Management:** HVAC, fire alarm, lift control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Response Time of a PLC

The response time of a PLC to an input change is **not constant** — it depends on **when** during the scan the input transitions. This is why KTU examiners ask for a derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the assumption.**
Let $T_{scan}$ be the total time for one complete scan cycle. Assume the input transition occurs at a **uniformly random instant** $t$ during the cycle.

**Step 2 — Define the wait time before recognition.**
The CPU will next sample the input only during the **next Input Scan phase**. The maximum possible wait time is $T_{scan}$ (if the change occurs just after the input scan), and the minimum is $0$ (if it occurs just before the next input scan).

**Step 3 — Express wait time as an average.**
Since the transition is equally likely anywhere in the cycle:

$$
T_{wait} = \dfrac{0 + T_{scan}}{2} = 0.5 \times T_{scan}
$$

This is the average time the PLC takes to **recognize** the input change.

**Step 4 — Add the processing time.**
After recognition, the CPU needs one full **program scan** to process and update the output. Then the output module is updated in the next output scan. Conservatively, we add **one full scan cycle** for processing and output update.

**Step 5 — Combine to get average response time.**

$$
T_{avg} = T_{wait} + T_{scan} = 0.5 \cdot T_{scan} + T_{scan}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{avg} = 1.5 \times T_{scan}}
$$

**Step 6 — Express extreme cases.**

For the **best-case** (input changes just before input scan), the response is one full cycle:

$$
T_{min} = T_{scan} \quad \text{(input \to next output, but conservatively counted as 2 cycles in many texts: } T_{min} = 2 \, T_{scan}\text{)}
$$

For the **worst-case** (input changes just after input scan), the response is two full cycles:

$$
T_{max} = 2 \times T_{scan}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Preferred Notation (Karpagam & Suganya Textbook Style):**
> $T_{min} = 2 \, T_{scan}$ and $T_{max} = 3 \, T_{scan}$ — this is the **Karpagam convention** widely used in KTU board answers.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (For Numerical 14-mark problems)

**Problem:** A PLC has an input scan time of 2 ms, an output scan time of 3 ms, a housekeeping time of 1 ms, and executes a ladder program with 1500 instructions at $8 \, \mu s$ per instruction. Find: (a) Total scan time (b) Average response time (c) Maximum response time (using Karpagam convention).

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Total scan time:**

$$
T_{in} = 2 \, ms, \quad T_{out} = 3 \, ms, \quad T_{house} = 1 \, ms
$$

$$
T_{prog} = 1500 \times 8 \, \mu s = 12000 \, \mu s = 12 \, ms
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 2 + 12 + 3 + 1 = 18 \, ms
$$

**Part (b) — Average response time:**

$$
T_{avg} = 1.5 \times 18 = 27 \, ms
$$

**Part (c) — Maximum response time (Karpagam convention):**

$$
T_{max} = 3 \times 18 = 54 \, ms
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of I/O Scan vs Program Scan

| Aspect | Input Scan | Program Scan | Output Scan |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reads / Writes** | Reads physical inputs | Reads PII, writes PIQ | Writes physical outputs |
| **Speed Determinant** | Number of input modules | Number of instructions | Number of output modules |
| **Isolation** | Opto-isolator barrier | None (internal logic) | Opto-isolator + driver |
| **User Influence** | None | **Direct (user-written logic)** | None |
| **Can be disabled?** | No | No | No |
| **Typical Time** | 0.5 – 2 ms | 1 – 20 ms | 0.5 – 2 ms |

### 3.4 Conversion Logic — Why 4–20 mA and Not 0–20 mA?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks: *“Why is 4–20 mA preferred over 0–20 mA for industrial current loops?”*

- **Zero discrimination:** A reading of $0$ mA is ambiguous — is the sensor at zero output, or is the wire broken? With $4$ mA as live-zero, **any reading $\le 4$ mA indicates a fault** (open loop).
- **Two-wire powering:** The 4 mA “offset” powers the field transmitter electronics (e.g., pressure transmitter) over the same two wires — no separate power needed.
- **Noise immunity:** Current is unaffected by lead resistance over long cable runs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a PLC

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **internal architecture** of a generic PLC and the data flow between subsystems. This is the standard 5-block diagram expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    psU["POWER SUPPLY UNIT 230V AC to 5V DC 24V DC"]
    cpu["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT Microprocessor ALU Control Unit"]
    rom["SYSTEM MEMORY ROM OR Flash Operating System Firmware"]
    ram["USER MEMORY RAM with Battery Backup Ladder Program Data Registers"]
    pii["INPUT IMAGE TABLE PII Snapshot of all input states"]
    piq["OUTPUT IMAGE TABLE PIQ Snapshot of all output states"]
    inp["INPUT MODULES Digital 24V DC 110V AC Analog 4 to 20 mA RTD Thermocouple"]
    outp["OUTPUT MODULES Relay Transistor Triac Analog 4 to 20 mA"]
    fieldI["FIELD INPUTS Sensors Switches Transmitters"]
    fieldO["FIELD OUTPUTS Contactors Solenoids Motors Valves Lamps"]
    prog["PROGRAMMING DEVICE PC with TIA Portal or Studio 5000"]
    comm["COMMUNICATION PORT RS232 RS485 Ethernet USB"]

    psU --- cpu
    psU --- inp
    psU --- outp
    cpu --- rom
    cpu --- ram
    cpu --- pii
    cpu --- piq
    inp --- pii
    piq --- outp
    fieldI --> inp
    outp --> fieldO
    prog --> comm
    comm --> cpu
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The CPU is the **central node**. Power supply feeds CPU, I/O modules. Programming device talks to CPU via communication port. Inputs are read into PII; outputs come from PIQ. The CPU never touches field devices directly — it always works through the image tables.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The PLC Scan Cycle Flow

This diagram is **mandatory** for the 14-mark question *“Explain the scan cycle of a PLC.”*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["START OF SCAN"]
    stepB["INPUT SCAN Read all physical inputs Store states in PII"]
    stepC["PROGRAM SCAN Execute ladder logic top to bottom Use PII values not physical inputs Update PIQ values"]
    stepD["OUTPUT SCAN Transfer PIQ values to physical output modules All outputs updated simultaneously"]
    stepE["HOUSEKEEPING Communication diagnostics timer counter update internal checks"]
    stepF["END OF CYCLE Return to START"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepA
```

### 4.3 Classification Tree — Types of PLC

The following flowchart shows the **taxonomy of PLC types** as per KTU 2024 syllabus. KTU expects students to be able to draw or describe this classification in 3-mark short questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["TYPES OF PLC"]
    root --> t1["BY CONSTRUCTION"]
    root --> t2["BY SIZE AND APPLICATION"]
    root --> t3["BY OUTPUT SWITCHING DEVICE"]

    t1 --> t1a["COMPACT OR INTEGRATED PLC Fixed I O count Non expandable Single unit"]
    t1 --> t1b["MODULAR OR RACK MOUNTED PLC Plug in modules expandable I O Scalable Higher cost"]

    t2 --> t2a["NANO PLC less than 32 I O relay only"]
    t2 --> t2b["MICRO PLC 32 to 128 I O small machines"]
    t2 --> t2c["SMALL PLC 128 to 256 I O standalone machines"]
    t2 --> t2d["MEDIUM PLC 256 to 1024 I O plant level"]
    t2 --> t2e["LARGE PLC more than 1024 I O SCADA integrated"]

    t3 --> t3a["RELAY OUTPUT Suitable for AC and DC Slow 10 ms Mechanical wear"]
    t3 --> t3b["TRANSISTOR OUTPUT DC only Fast 0.1 ms Long life Sinking or Sourcing"]
    t3 --> t3c["TRIAC OUTPUT AC only Quiet operation Optoisolated"]
```

### 4.4 Functional Comparison — Compact vs Modular PLC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cmp["COMPACT PLC"]
    mod["MODULAR PLC"]
    cmp --> c1["Single integrated unit"]
    cmp --> c2["Fixed I O cannot expand"]
    cmp --> c3["Lower cost"]
    cmp --> c4["Used in small machines"]
    mod --> m1["Rack with slots"]
    mod --> m2["Plug in modules for I O expansion"]
    mod --> m3["Higher cost and flexibility"]
    mod --> m4["Used in large plants SCADA level"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define a PLC. List any four advantages of PLCs over conventional relay-based control systems.

**Model Answer:**
A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, microprocessor-based industrial controller that uses programmed instructions stored in memory to implement logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic functions for controlling industrial machinery and processes.

**Advantages of PLCs over relay logic:**
1. **Flexibility:** Control logic can be changed by editing the program — no rewiring required.
2. **Reliability:** Solid-state components with no moving parts (except relays) result in Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of 100,000+ hours.
3. **Compactness:** A single PLC replaces hundreds of relays, timers, and counters, reducing panel size by 70–80%.
4. **Diagnostics & Communication:** Built-in fault indicators, communication ports, and remote monitoring (SCADA/HMI) — features absent in relay panels.
5. **Cost-effective for complex logic:** As complexity grows, PLC cost grows linearly while relay cost grows exponentially.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between the Input Image Table (PII) and the Output Image Table (PIQ) of a PLC.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Input Image Table (PII) | Output Image Table (PIQ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | CPU **reads** from physical inputs and stores here | CPU **writes** results here; later sent to physical outputs |
| **Update timing** | Updated during **Input Scan** phase | Updated during **Program Scan**; physically transferred during **Output Scan** |
| **Stored bit value** | Represents the **current ON/OFF state** of the field input | Represents the **commanded state** to be sent to the field output |
| **Program interaction** | Program reads from PII (NEVER from physical input directly) | Program writes to PIQ (NEVER to physical output directly) |
| **Purpose** | Provides a **stable snapshot** so that all logic decisions in one scan see a consistent set of inputs | Prevents outputs from flickering mid-scan as different rungs update |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly write “CPU reads from sensors directly during program scan.” This is **wrong** — the CPU reads the **Input Image Table**, not the physical terminal. Mentioning this distinction can earn 1–2 bonus marks.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q3 (A).** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of a PLC**. Describe the function of each block. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Explain the **scan cycle of a PLC** with a flowchart. Derive the expression for **average response time**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a): Architecture**

A PLC consists of five major functional blocks:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU):** Heart of the PLC. Contains the microprocessor (typically 16-bit or 32-bit), ALU for arithmetic/logic operations, and the control unit for instruction sequencing. Executes the user program stored in RAM.

2. **Memory Unit:** Divided into System Memory (ROM/Flash — contains OS, compilers, firmware) and User Memory (RAM with lithium battery — contains ladder program, preset values, data registers).

3. **Input/Output Modules:** Interface between CPU and field. Input modules use **opto-isolators** for electrical isolation; accept 24 V DC, 110 V AC, 4–20 mA, RTD, Thermocouple. Output modules use **Relays, Transistors, or Triacs** to switch field loads.

4. **Power Supply:** Converts 230 V AC mains (or 24 V DC for compact PLCs) into regulated +5 V DC and +24 V DC. Provides isolation and short-circuit protection.

5. **Programming Device & Communication Port:** PC loaded with vendor software (TIA Portal, RSLogix, EcoStruxure) connected via RS-232, RS-485, USB, or Ethernet. Used to write, upload, download, monitor, and debug ladder programs.

*Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 above for the complete schematic.*

**Model Solution — Part (b): Scan Cycle**

The PLC operates on a **repetitive four-step cycle** called the **Scan Cycle**:

1. **Input Scan (T_in):** All physical input terminals are read sequentially and their ON/OFF states are stored in the **Input Image Table (PII)**. This takes 0.5–2 ms typically.

2. **Program Scan (T_prog):** The CPU executes the ladder logic instructions **one by one**, from top to bottom and left to right. It reads input conditions from the PII (never from physical inputs), evaluates logic, and writes intermediate results to the PIQ.

3. **Output Scan (T_out):** The contents of the **Output Image Table (PIQ)** are transferred simultaneously to the physical output modules to energize/de-energize field devices. This takes 0.5–2 ms.

4. **Housekeeping (T_house):** Performs self-diagnostics, updates timer/counter elapsed values, services communication requests, and handles interrupt routines.

**Total Scan Time:**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{housekeeping}
$$

**Derivation of Average Response Time** *(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation)*:

$$
T_{avg} = 1.5 \times T_{scan}
$$

**Numerical illustration (if asked):** For a PLC with 2000 instructions at 5 µs/instruction, $T_{scan} = 1 + 10 + 1 + 1 = 13$ ms, giving $T_{avg} = 19.5$ ms.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q3 (B).** *(a)* Classify the **types of PLCs** based on construction, size, and output switching device. Explain each type in detail. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Compare **Compact (Integrated) PLC** with **Modular (Rack-Mounted) PLC** in a 10-row table. Justify which type is preferred for a large cement plant. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — Part (a): Classification of PLCs**

**Type 1 — By Construction:**
- **Compact (Integrated) PLC:** CPU, PSU, I/O, and communication are housed in a **single sealed unit**. The I/O count is fixed (typically 10–40 I/O). Cannot be expanded. Examples: Siemens LOGO!, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1000, Mitsubishi MELSEC-FX0N.
- **Modular (Rack-Mounted) PLC:** Each subsystem (CPU, PSU, I/O) is a **separate plug-in module** mounted on a common **rack/backplane**. I/O can be expanded by adding more modules. Examples: Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Schneider Modicon M340.

**Type 2 — By Size/Application:**
- **Nano PLC:** < 32 I/O, relay output only, used in domestic/small applications.
- **Micro PLC:** 32–128 I/O, used in small standalone machines.
- **Small PLC:** 128–256 I/O, used in skid-mounted equipment.
- **Medium PLC:** 256–1024 I/O, plant-floor controllers.
- **Large PLC:** > 1024 I/O, SCADA-integrated, used in power plants, refineries.

**Type 3 — By Output Switching Device:**
- **Relay Output:** Works on both AC and DC. Slow (10 ms switching), limited mechanical life (~1 million operations), no leakage current. Used for heavy loads.
- **Transistor Output:** DC only, very fast (0.1 ms), long life (no moving parts), available as **sinking (NPN)** or **sourcing (PNP)**. Used for high-speed solenoids.
- **Triac Output:** AC only, silent operation, optoisolated, used for AC solenoids and small motors.

**Model Solution — Part (b): Comparison Table & Justification**

| # | Feature | Compact PLC | Modular PLC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Physical form | Single unit | Multiple plug-in modules on rack |
| 2 | I/O count | Fixed (10–40) | Expandable up to thousands |
| 3 | Expansion | Not possible | Possible by adding modules |
| 4 | Cost | Low | High |
| 5 | Flexibility | Low | High |
| 6 | Maintenance | Replace whole unit | Replace faulty module only |
| 7 | Spare parts stocking | Simple | Complex (many module variants) |
| 8 | Hot-swap support | No | Yes (in most modern racks) |
| 9 | Typical applications | Small machines, lifts, pumps | Large process plants, SCADA nodes |
| 10 | Examples | LOGO!, MicroLogix 1000 | S7-1500, ControlLogix, M340 |

**Justification for a Cement Plant:** A cement plant has **distributed control requirements** — raw mill, kiln, cooler, cement mill, packing plant, water treatment — each with hundreds of I/O signals. A **Modular PLC** is the only realistic choice because (i) it can handle thousands of I/O points, (ii) supports **redundancy** (duplicate CPU/Power Supply for critical kiln control), (iii) interfaces easily with **SCADA/HMI** and **fieldbus networks** (Profibus/Modbus), and (iv) allows **module-level hot-swap** maintenance without plant shutdown.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks in ESE:**
> 1. **Drawing the scan cycle as 3 steps instead of 4.** Housekeeping is a separate phase — do not skip it.
> 2. **Writing “$T_{avg} = 1.5 \times T_{scan}$” without derivation.** Marks are split: 2 for formula, 2 for derivation, 1 for numerical, 2 for conclusion.
> 3. **Confusing PII and PIQ directions.** PII = input snapshot, PIQ = output snapshot. The CPU reads PII, writes PIQ.
> 4. **Claiming Relay outputs are faster than Transistor outputs.** The opposite is true: Relay $\approx$ 10 ms, Transistor $\approx$ 0.1 ms.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention opto-isolation** in the I/O module description — it is a 1-mark “must-mention” keyword.
> 6. **Writing scan time in seconds instead of milliseconds.** Always check units — $1 \mu s \times 1000$ instructions $= 1$ ms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
> - PLC = **P**rogrammable **L**ogic **C**ontroller; invented **1968 by Dick Morley**; first product = **Modicon 084**.
> - **Five Blocks:** CPU, Memory, I/O Modules, Power Supply, Programming Device.
> - **Memory split:** **System Memory (ROM/Flash)** = OS + Firmware; **User Memory (RAM)** = Ladder Program + Data.
> - **Two Image Tables:** **PII** (input snapshot) and **PIQ** (output snapshot) — CPU always works with the image, not the field.
> - **Scan Cycle = 4 steps:** Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping.
> - **Total Scan Time:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$.
> - **Average Response Time:** $T_{avg} = 1.5 \times T_{scan}$.
> - **Karpagam convention:** $T_{min} = 2 \, T_{scan}$, $T_{max} = 3 \, T_{scan}$.
> - **Types by construction:** **Compact (Fixed I/O, low cost, non-expandable)** vs **Modular (Rack-mounted, expandable, expensive)**.
> - **Types by size:** Nano < Micro < Small < Medium < Large (based on I/O count).
> - **Output types:** **Relay** (universal, slow, mechanical wear) vs **Transistor** (DC, fast, no wear) vs **Triac** (AC, silent, optoisolated).
> - **Industrial current loop:** **4–20 mA** is preferred over 0–20 mA because $4$ mA is the **live-zero** that doubles as a **fault indicator** (open loop).
> - **Opto-isolation** in I/O modules is the primary defense against **voltage spikes** and **ground loops**.
> - **Programming languages per IEC 61131-3:** Ladder Diagram (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), Structured Text (ST), Instruction List (IL), Sequential Function Chart (SFC).
> - **Always mention “solid-state, ruggedized, real-time, deterministic”** when defining a PLC — these are examiner-favorite keywords.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Modules, Input and Output Modules
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 — PLC Modules, Input and Output Modules

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** A solid-state, industrial-grade digital computer designed for the real-time control of manufacturing processes and automated machinery. It operates using a stored program executing logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic functions, and interfaces discrete and continuous field devices through modular **Input (I)** and **Output (O)** sections.

A **PLC Module** is a self-contained, plug-in hardware sub-assembly of the PLC rack/system that performs a specific function—processing, memory, power conversion, input signal conditioning, or output signal switching. The PLC architecture is fundamentally **modular** so that field wiring, signal levels, and I/O counts can be tailored to the controlled process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Nervous System"

Think of a PLC as a **human reflex arc**:

| Human Body | PLC Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Skin (Sensors) | **Input Module** | Detects external stimuli (push-button, sensor, limit switch) |
| Spinal Cord | **CPU + Memory Module** | Processes the sensation and decides a response (logic program) |
| Muscles (Actuators) | **Output Module** | Executes the action (turn ON motor, solenoid, lamp) |
| Heart & Blood | **Power Supply Module** | Pumps energy to every organ |
| Communication/Backbone | **Backplane / Chassis** | The nervous-system wiring carrying signals between organs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The Input and Output modules together constitute the **I/O Subsystem**, often called the *interface between the "brain" (CPU) and the "muscles/senses" (field devices)*. Roughly 60 % of any PLC's physical volume and cost is I/O.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standards (Bolded)

- **Standard Supply Voltage to PLC rack:** **24 V DC** (logic), **120/230 V AC** (mains).
- **Discrete (Digital) Voltage Thresholds:** **ON ≥ 15 V DC**, **OFF ≤ 5 V DC** for 24 V DC inputs.
- **Analog Standard Signals:** **4–20 mA** (current loop, industrial standard) and **0–10 V DC** (voltage standard).
- **Isolation Voltage:** Typically **1500 V AC** (opto-isolation barrier between field and logic).
- **Update (Scan) Time per I/O point:** **< 10 ms** for fast discrete, **< 1 µs** for high-speed counters.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a PLC — The Five Core Modules

A modern PLC (Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Siemens S7-1500, Schneider Modicon M340) is built from five functional modules. The KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus places primary emphasis on **I/O Modules**, but the student must understand every module's role.

#### Module ① — Processor / CPU Module

- Contains the **Microprocessor / Microcontroller**, arithmetic logic unit (ALU), and accumulator.
- Executes the **ladder logic / structured text program** stored in RAM.
- Reads inputs, solves the user program, and updates outputs — the famous **Scan Cycle**:

  $$T_{\text{scan}} = T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{program}} + T_{\text{output}} + T_{\text{houskeeping}}$$

- Performs self-diagnostics (watchdog timer, memory parity, power-fail detection).

#### Module ② — Memory Module

| Memory Type | Function | Volatility | Technology |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RAM** (Random Access Memory) | Active user program & I/O image table | **Volatile** (battery-backed) | CMOS |
| **ROM / EPROM** | Operating system, firmware boot loader | **Non-volatile** | Mask ROM / UV-erasable |
| **EEPROM / Flash** | Permanent user program backup | **Non-volatile** | Electrically erasable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why battery-backed RAM?** During a power failure, the CMOS RAM holds the user program and the **Process Image (PI)** of inputs/outputs so that on power restoration, the PLC resumes exactly where it left off — a key **KTU 2024 expected answer point**.

#### Module ③ — Power Supply Module (PSU)

- Converts **120/230 V AC** mains (or 24 V DC) into the regulated DC voltages required by the CPU, memory, and I/O modules: typically **+5 V DC** (logic), **±15 V DC** (analog), and **+24 V DC** (sensor supply).
- Provides **isolation** and **over-voltage / over-current protection**.
- Supplies the *sensor power* to the field — this is why we say "sourcing vs. sinking" in I/O wiring.

#### Module ④ — Input Module (Detailed in §2.2)

#### Module ⑤ — Output Module (Detailed in §2.3)

#### The Backplane / Chassis

A **printed-circuit motherboard** containing parallel data, address, and control buses plus power rails. Modern PLCs use **backplane-based serial communication** (e.g., SERCOS, EtherCAT, Profibus) instead of parallel buses for higher speed and noise immunity.

### 2.2 Input Module — Theory of Operation

#### 2.2.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Discrete Input Module

A typical 24 V DC discrete input module executes four sequential stages:

1. **Terminal Block & Protection** — Screw/spring terminals with **TVS diodes**, **fuses**, and **reverse-polarity protection**.
2. **Signal Conditioning & Filtering** — **RC low-pass filter** to reject contact bounce and EMI (typical cutoff **f_c = 1/(2πRC)** between 100 Hz – 1 kHz).
3. **Optical Isolation** — **Optocoupler (phototransistor + LED)** provides galvanic isolation; the field side and logic side share no common conductor.
4. **Threshold Detection / Schmitt Trigger** — Compares filtered analog voltage against **V_IL** and **V_IH** thresholds and produces a clean **logic-0/1** to the **Input Image Table (IIT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Key Term:** The **Input Image Table (IIT)** is an area of CPU memory (one bit per physical input) updated at the start of every scan. The user program *never* reads the field directly during execution — it reads the IIT, guaranteeing **deterministic, repeatable** scan time.

#### 2.2.2 Types of Input Modules

| Type | Signal Range | Typical Devices Connected |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete DC (sinking/sourcing)** | 5 V, 12 V, 24 V, 48 V, 60 V DC | Push-buttons, limit switches, proximity sensors, photo-sensors |
| **Discrete AC** | 110 V, 120 V, 230 V AC | Motor contactor auxiliaries, selector switches |
| **Analog Input (AI)** | 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, ±10 V, RTD, Thermocouple (mV) | Level transmitters, temperature probes, flow meters |
| **Special / High-Speed** | 5–24 V DC; up to 200 kHz | Encoders, SSI absolute encoders, interrupts |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** "Can a 24 V DC sourcing sensor be wired to a sinking input?" The answer is *no* — current direction must match. A mismatch is the #1 wiring bug in KTU lab exams.

#### 2.2.3 Sinking vs. Sourcing — The Critical Distinction

- **Sinking Input (NPN):** Current **flows *into* the input terminal** from the field device. Field device acts as a *current sink* to ground.
- **Sourcing Input (PNP):** Current **flows *out of* the input terminal** to the field device. Field device acts as a *current source* from V+.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Always draw a small arrow indicating current flow during your exam diagrams. Examiners *mark for direction* — many students lose 2–3 marks by writing "PNP = sourcing" without the current direction.

### 2.3 Output Module — Theory of Operation

#### 2.3.1 Functional Stages of a Discrete Output Module

1. **Logic-to-Output Latch (Output Image Table – OIT):** The CPU writes the solved logic to OIT; at the end of the scan, the OIT is transferred to the output latches.
2. **Driver Stage:** The latch drives a **transistor (DC)**, **TRIAC (AC)**, or **relay contact (electromechanical)**.
3. **Isolation:** Same opto-isolator principle — and often **relay isolation** for AC outputs.
4. **Surge Suppression:** **Snubber networks (R-C)**, **MOVs (Metal-Oxide Varistors)**, and **freewheeling diodes** across inductive loads.

#### 2.3.2 Types of Output Modules

| Output Element | Switches | Switching Speed | Lifetime | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Relay (Electromechanical)** | AC or DC | 10 ms (slow) | 10⁵ – 10⁶ operations | General purpose, motor starters |
| **Transistor (BJT/MOSFET)** | DC only | < 1 µs (fast) | Unlimited (no contacts) | Solenoids, DC valves, indicators |
| **TRIAC** | AC only | < 10 µs | >10⁸ operations | Heaters, AC solenoids, lamps |
| **Analog Output (AO)** | 4–20 mA, 0–10 V | n/a | n/a | Variable-speed drives, control valves |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Standard Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scan Cycle Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{diag}$ | ms | Determinism guarantee |
| I/O Wiring Voltage Drop | $V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{wire}$ | V | Maximum cable length |
| Maximum Cable Length | $L_{max} = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{IL}}{2 \cdot R_{wire\_per\_m} \cdot I_{leak}}$ | m | Sourcing sensor limit |
| RC Filter Cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz | Debounce / EMI filter |
| Digital Resolution (Analog) | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^{n}}$ | V or mA / bit | 12-bit AI = 4.88 µA/bit @ 4–20 mA |
| Sampling Rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max\_signal}$ | Hz | Shannon's theorem |
| Power Dissipation in Output | $P_{out} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{load}$ | W | Thermal design |
| Opto-isolation Voltage | $V_{iso} \geq 1500\,V_{AC}$ | V | Safety barrier |
| Contact Bounce Time | $t_b = 0.5\!-\!5$ ms | ms | Why we need debounce |
| Inductive Kick Voltage | $V_{kick} = -L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V | Why we need flyback diode |

> **Real-world utility:** These formulas govern cable-plant design (EIA/TIA 422, IEC 61131-2), EMC compliance, and Mean Time Between Failure (MTBF) calculations of the I/O subsystem.

### 2.5 Why a Separate I/O Module? (The "How" and the "Why")

- **Voltage Translation:** Field devices run on 230 V AC, but the CPU runs on 5 V DC. The I/O module translates between worlds.
- **Isolation:** A 1500 V opto-isolator prevents a single field-side short circuit from destroying the entire CPU.
- **Modularity:** A user can swap an 8-point 24 V DC input for a 16-point 120 V AC input without touching the CPU.
- **Diagnostics:** Modern I/O modules include **per-point LED indication**, **short-circuit detection**, **wire-break detection**, and **hot-swap capability**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Computing I/O Scan Time

A Siemens S7-1200 PLC has:
- 32 discrete inputs, each with 1 µs read time
- 16 discrete outputs, each with 1.5 µs write time
- User program: 1.8 ms execution
- Diagnostics/housekeeping: 0.4 ms

**Find the total scan time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in}  &= 32 \times 1\,\mu s = 32\,\mu s \\[2pt]
T_{out} &= 16 \times 1.5\,\mu s = 24\,\mu s \\[2pt]
T_{scan} &= T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{diag} \\[2pt]
T_{scan} &= 32\,\mu s + 1800\,\mu s + 24\,\mu s + 400\,\mu s \\[2pt]
T_{scan} &= 2256\,\mu s = 2.256\,ms
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The PLC performs roughly **443 scans per second**, well above the threshold of 10 scans/s required for most discrete manufacturing control.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Designing an Input Debounce Filter

A push-button with **contact-bounce time $t_b$ = 3 ms** is wired to a 24 V DC input. We must design an RC low-pass filter that filters above $f_c = 1/t_b$ but does not delay the legitimate press ($>$ 50 ms).

**Step 1 — Choose cutoff frequency.**

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{t_b} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 333\,Hz
$$

**Step 2 — Pick a standard capacitor.** Choose $C = 0.1\,\mu F$.

**Step 3 — Solve for R.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} \\[2pt]
R &= \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 333 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \\[2pt]
R &= \frac{1}{2.092 \times 10^{-4}} \\[2pt]
R &\approx 4.78\,k\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use the nearest standard value: $R = 4.7\,k\Omega$** (E12 series).

> **Real-world sanity check:** The time constant $\tau = RC = 4.7 k\Omega \times 0.1 \mu F = 0.47\,ms$ — this is much less than 50 ms, so legitimate presses pass through clean.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Analog Input Resolution

A 12-bit analog input module reads a **4–20 mA** signal. What is the **least-significant bit (LSB)** resolution?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FSR} &= 20\,mA - 4\,mA = 16\,mA \\[2pt]
\text{Resolution per bit} &= \frac{16\,mA}{2^{12}} = \frac{16}{4096}\,mA \\[2pt]
&= 0.00390625\,mA \\[2pt]
&= 3.91\,\mu A / \text{bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 0–10 V signal at 14 bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Resolution} &= \frac{10\,V}{2^{14}} = \frac{10}{16384} \\[2pt]
&= 0.610\,mV / \text{bit}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Maximum Cable Length for a 24 V DC Sourcing Sensor

A sensor requires a **minimum ON voltage $V_{IL}$ = 15 V**. Wire resistance is **50 mΩ / meter** and the input draws **15 mA**. The supply is 24 V.

**Step 1 — Allowed voltage drop across the wire:**

$$
V_{drop} = 24\,V - 15\,V = 9\,V
$$

**Step 2 — Resistance budget for the loop (out + return):**

$$
R_{loop} = \frac{V_{drop}}{I} = \frac{9}{0.015} = 600\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — One-way resistance = 300 Ω. Length:**

$$
L = \frac{300}{0.05} = 6000\,m
$$

> **Conclusion:** Practically, **voltage drop and shielding capacitance** limit cables to < 100 m. The 6000 m is a theoretical maximum — real installations use **4–20 mA current loops** precisely to be immune to cable resistance.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which I/O

| Decision Criterion | Relay Output | Transistor Output | TRIAC Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load is **AC** | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ (best) |
| Load is **DC** | ✅ | ✅ (best) | ❌ |
| **High switching frequency** (> 1 Hz) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Inrush current** (motors) | ✅ (high surge rating) | ⚠️ | ⚠️ |
| **Galvanic isolation** required | ✅ (inherent) | ❌ (needs opto) | ❌ (needs opto) |
| **Cost per point** | High | Low | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Answer (7-mark sub-part):** Compare relay, transistor, and TRIAC outputs on at least **three parameters** (switching speed, voltage class, life, leakage current). The above table is the model answer.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a PLC Input Module. List any two types of input modules.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An **Input Module** is the interface section of a PLC that receives electrical signals from field sensing devices (push-buttons, limit switches, sensors) and converts them into **logic-level signals** suitable for processing by the CPU. It performs **signal conditioning**, **isolation** (typically optical), and **threshold detection**.
>
> **Two types (1.5 marks each):**
> 1. **Discrete (Digital) Input Module** — handles ON/OFF signals at 24 V DC, 120 V AC, 230 V AC, etc.
> 2. **Analog Input Module** — handles continuously variable signals such as 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, RTD, or thermocouple.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between sinking and sourcing input with a neat sketch.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Sourcing input (PNP):** The field device **sources current *into* the PLC input terminal** from the positive supply. The PLC input "draws" current from the sensor.
> - **Sinking input (NPN):** The field device **sinks current *from* the PLC input terminal** to the common/ground. The PLC input "pushes" current to the sensor.
>
> **Sketch (1 mark):** Draw two simple circuits showing a 24 V DC source, a sensor switch, and a PLC input terminal with an arrow showing current direction (in for sinking, out for sourcing).

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Answer **any one full question** out of two. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Mark distribution is shown against each step.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of a PLC and explain the function of each module.

**Model Solution:**

A PLC consists of the following five modules (1 mark for naming all, 4 marks for explanation, 2 marks for diagram):

1. **Power Supply Module (PSU):** Converts 120/230 V AC mains into regulated DC voltages (5 V, ±15 V, 24 V) for the CPU, memory, and I/O. Also supplies sensor power. (1 mark)

2. **CPU / Processor Module:** Contains the microprocessor, ALU, accumulator, and control unit. Executes the user program in a repetitive **scan cycle** — *read inputs → solve logic → write outputs*. Performs self-diagnostics using a **watchdog timer**. (1 mark)

3. **Memory Module:** Stores the operating system (ROM), user program (RAM, battery-backed), and backup (EEPROM/Flash). The **Input Image Table (IIT)** and **Output Image Table (OIT)** reside here. (1 mark)

4. **Input Module:** Receives signals from field devices, conditions (filters, debounces), optically isolates, and converts to logic levels. (1 mark)

5. **Output Module:** Receives logic-level commands from the CPU, switches power to field actuators through relays, transistors, or TRIACs, with isolation and surge protection. (1 mark)

6. **Backplane / Chassis:** A motherboard carrying data, address, and control buses plus power rails between all modules. (1 mark)

7. **Diagram (2 marks):** Standard block diagram (refer to §4.1 above).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the functional block diagram of a discrete DC input module. State the role of the **optocoupler** and **Schmitt trigger**.

**Model Solution:**

A discrete DC input module consists of four stages (5 marks for stages, 2 marks for opto + Schmitt):

- **Stage 1 – Terminal Block & Protection:** Screw/spring terminals with **TVS diodes**, **fuses**, and **reverse-polarity protection diodes**. (1 mark)

- **Stage 2 – Signal Conditioning (RC Filter):** Low-pass RC network to **reject contact bounce** and **electromagnetic interference (EMI)**. Typical cutoff $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ in the range 100 Hz – 1 kHz. (1 mark)

- **Stage 3 – Optical Isolation (Optocoupler):** An LED + phototransistor pair inside a single IC. When the field-side LED is energized, light crosses a 1500 V isolation gap and switches the logic-side phototransistor. Provides **galvanic isolation** — a short circuit on the field side cannot damage the CPU. (1.5 marks)

- **Stage 4 – Threshold Detection (Schmitt Trigger):** A comparator with **hysteresis** converts the slowly-rising filtered signal into a clean digital 0/1 with two thresholds: $V_{IL}$ (max low) and $V_{IH}$ (min high). Hysteresis prevents chattering near the threshold. (1.5 marks)

- **Output of stage 4 → Input Image Table (IIT)** in CPU RAM. (1 mark)

- **Role summary:** The **optocoupler** provides **galvanic isolation and noise immunity**; the **Schmitt trigger** provides **clean, noise-free digital logic levels with hysteresis**. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **relay**, **transistor**, and **TRIAC** output modules on the basis of switching speed, voltage class, lifetime, leakage current, and typical applications.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Relay Output | Transistor Output | TRIAC Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switches** | AC or DC | DC only | AC only |
| **Switching speed** | Slow (5–15 ms) | Very fast (< 1 µs) | Fast (< 10 µs) |
| **Lifetime** | 10⁵–10⁶ operations | Unlimited (solid-state) | > 10⁸ operations |
| **Leakage current** | Zero (open contact) | µA range | mA range (off-state) |
| **Galvanic isolation** | Inherent | Needs optocoupler | Needs optocoupler |
| **Cost per point** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Typical load** | Motor contactors, mixed-voltage panels | DC solenoids, indicators | AC heaters, lamps, AC solenoids |

(5 marks for table + 2 marks for application: e.g., "Relays are used in mixed-voltage control panels; transistors in fast DC applications like stepper drivers; TRIACs in zero-cross-switched AC heating.")

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Input Image Table (IIT)** and **Output Image Table (OIT)** concept. Why does the PLC never read inputs during program execution?

**Model Solution:**

- **IIT (Input Image Table):** A reserved area in **CPU RAM** containing one bit per physical input. It is updated *once* at the start of every scan by reading the input modules. (2 marks)

- **OIT (Output Image Table):** A reserved area in **CPU RAM** containing one bit per physical output. The user program writes to the OIT during execution; the OIT is transferred to the output modules *only* at the end of the scan. (2 marks)

- **Diagram (2 marks):** Show CPU with IIT and OIT blocks, arrows showing "IIT ← Input Module at start of scan" and "OIT → Output Module at end of scan."

- **Why the PLC never reads inputs during execution (1 mark):** To guarantee **deterministic scan time** and **consistent logic results**. If the input changes mid-scan, the program could see contradictory states. Reading the IIT snapshot eliminates this hazard.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Do **not** write "PLC reads inputs at the end of scan" — that is wrong; inputs are read at the **start** of the scan.
> 2. Do **not** say "IIT is in ROM" — IIT is in **volatile RAM** (battery-backed), not ROM.
> 3. Always mention **deterministic scan time** when explaining IIT/OIT — examiners award the full 1 mark for that phrase.

### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A PLC has **5 modules**: PSU, CPU, Memory, Input, Output — connected by a **backplane**.
- ✅ **Scan time** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{diag}$ — must be **deterministic**.
- ✅ **IIT (Input Image Table)** is updated at the **start** of the scan; **OIT (Output Image Table)** is written at the **end**.
- ✅ **RAM** is volatile but **battery-backed**; **ROM/Flash** holds the OS and permanent backup.
- ✅ Input module stages: **Terminal → RC Filter → Optocoupler → Schmitt Trigger → IIT**.
- ✅ Output module stages: **OIT Latch → Driver → Isolation → Snubber → Terminal → Load**.
- ✅ **Optocoupler** = galvanic isolation at **1500 V AC** between field and CPU.
- ✅ **Sinking (NPN) vs Sourcing (PNP)** is decided by **direction of current flow**, not the device type.
- ✅ **Relay** = universal, slow, contacts wear. **Transistor** = DC only, fast, solid-state. **TRIAC** = AC only, fast, has off-state leakage.
- ✅ **Analog standard** = **4–20 mA** (current loop, noise-immune) or **0–10 V** (voltage).
- ✅ **Resolution of n-bit AI** = FSR / 2ⁿ — 12-bit on 4–20 mA ⇒ **3.91 µA/bit**.
- ✅ **Snubber / flyback diode** mandatory on inductive DC loads; **MOV** mandatory on AC loads.
- ✅ **Debounce filter cutoff** should be roughly $f_c = 1/t_{bounce}$.
- ✅ IEC standard governing PLC design: **IEC 61131-3** (programming languages) and **IEC 61131-2** (hardware / I/O).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PLC Modules, Input and Output Modules**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcomes, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L2–L4).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
