> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT412 - PLC & DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital and Analog Input/Output - Examples of Digital and Analog Inputs/Outputs in PLC - AC-DC Power Supplies in PLC - Isolators - PLC Configuration - Scan Cycle - Capabilities of PLC - Selection Criteria for PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Digital Inputs and Outputs (Discrete I/O)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
A **Digital Input (DI)** is a discrete, two-state signal received by the PLC that can exist in only one of two possible conditions: **ON (Logic 1)** or **OFF (Logic 0)**. Typical voltage levels are **24 V DC** (industry standard) or **120/230 V AC**.

A **Digital Output (DO)** is a discrete switching signal generated by the PLC to control actuators that operate in only two states (energize/de-energize, start/stop).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** Digital I/O is *binary*, *noise-immune*, and uses *threshold voltages* (typically < 5 V = OFF, > 15 V = ON for 24 V DC systems, with a dead-band between).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **light switch in your home**. It has only two positions — ON or OFF. You can dim a light with a dimmer, but the wall switch itself is digital. PLC digital inputs behave exactly like reading the position of that switch, and PLC digital outputs behave like that switch being controlled remotely by the PLC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of DIGITAL INPUTS:**
> - Push buttons (Start/Stop)
> - Limit switches (valve open/close feedback)
> - Proximity sensors (object detected/not detected)
> - Photoelectric sensors
> - Selector switches (Auto/Manual)
> - Pressure switches (high/low trip)
> - Float switches (tank level high/low)
> - Relay contacts from other equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of DIGITAL OUTPUTS:**
> - Solenoid valves
> - Motor contactors (Start/Stop relay)
> - Indicator lamps (Run, Trip, Healthy)
> - Buzzers and alarms
> - Relay coils
> - Heating element contactors
> - Pneumatic/Hydraulic cylinder actuators

---

## 1.2 Analog Inputs and Outputs (Continuous I/O)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
An **Analog Input (AI)** is a continuously variable signal received by the PLC that can take any value within a defined range. Standard industrial signal ranges are:
- **Voltage:** 0–10 V, ±10 V, 0–5 V
- **Current:** 4–20 mA (most common, current loop)
- **Resistance/Temperature:** RTD (Pt100, Pt1000), Thermocouple (J, K, T types)

An **Analog Output (AO)** is a continuously variable control signal sent from the PLC to proportional actuators to modulate their behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** Analog signals are *continuous*, *susceptible to noise*, and require *A/D and D/A conversion* inside the PLC. The 4–20 mA current loop is preferred over voltage because it is *immune to voltage drop over long distances* and can detect a *broken wire* (0 mA = fault).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the **volume knob on a TV remote**. Unlike a light switch, the volume can be set to any value from 0 to 100. PLC analog inputs work exactly like that — instead of just "object there / not there," they answer "how much?" and "how fast?". A 4–20 mA signal is like measuring the volume not by position, but by how strongly the knob is being pushed (current strength).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of ANALOG INPUTS:**
> - Temperature transmitters (RTD/TC-based, output 4–20 mA)
> - Pressure transmitters
> - Flow meters (magnetic, vortex, Coriolis)
> - Level transmitters (ultrasonic, capacitive, hydrostatic)
> - Load cells (weight)
> - Humidity sensors
> - pH meters
> - Vibration sensors

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples of ANALOG OUTPUTS:**
> - Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for motor speed control
> - Control valve positioners (pneumatic/hydraulic)
> - Damper actuators for HVAC
> - Chart recorders
> - PID controller set-points
> - Thyristor power controllers for heaters

---

## 1.3 Power Supplies in PLC

### Formal Definition
A PLC requires a **regulated DC power supply** to operate its internal logic circuits, I/O modules, and communication interfaces. The supply converts incoming mains (AC) or battery (DC) into stable DC rails such as **+5 V, +12 V, +24 V, and −12 V**.

| PLC Type | Input Supply | Internal Rails Generated |
|---|---|---|
| AC-powered PLC | 120/230 V AC, 50/60 Hz | +5 V, ±12 V, +24 V (isolated) |
| DC-powered PLC | 24 V DC (battery/solar) | +5 V, ±12 V (via DC–DC converter) |

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the power supply as the **heart and lungs** of the PLC. Just as your body needs clean, oxygenated blood, the PLC needs clean, regulated DC voltage. A noisy or fluctuating power supply is like polluted blood — the brain (CPU) malfunctions.

---

## 1.4 Isolators

### Formal Definition
**Isolators** (also called **galvanic isolators** or **signal isolators**) are devices that **transfer a signal between two circuits while breaking any direct electrical (galvanic) connection** between them. Common types are:
- **Opto-isolators** (LED + phototransistor)
- **Transformer isolators** (magnetic coupling)
- **Capacitive isolators** (digital isolators)

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **glass window between two rooms**. People in Room A can see people in Room B and signal to them, but they cannot touch or harm each other. Air, germs, and noise are blocked — only the *information* passes through. That is exactly what an isolator does: information passes, electricity does not.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why isolation is mandatory in PLC systems:**
> 1. Protects the sensitive CPU from **voltage transients** and **spikes** on field side.
> 2. Eliminates **ground-loop currents** that cause measurement errors.
> 3. Provides **operator safety** (up to 1500 V AC isolation barrier).
> 4. Reduces **common-mode noise** in analog signals.

---

## 1.5 PLC Configuration

### Formal Definition
**PLC Configuration** refers to the physical and logical arrangement of PLC components — including the **CPU**, **power supply**, **I/O modules**, **communication modules**, and **backplane/rack** — that defines the system’s hardware architecture.

### Common Configurations (KTU 2024)

| Configuration Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Compact (Slim-line)** | CPU, PS, I/O in a single housing | Small machines, OEM skid |
| **Modular (Rack-mounted)** | Separate plug-in modules on a chassis | Medium/large plants |
| **Distributed I/O (Remote I/O)** | I/O stations placed near field devices, networked back to CPU | Large plants, long cable runs |
| **Hot-swappable** | Modules can be replaced online without powering down | Critical processes |

---

## 1.6 Scan Cycle

### Formal Definition
The **Scan Cycle** is the **continuous, cyclic sequence of operations** a PLC CPU performs repeatedly to read inputs, execute the user program, update outputs, and perform housekeeping. The total time for one complete cycle is called the **Scan Time**, usually measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four phases of the scan cycle (in order):**
> 1. **Input Scan** — read status of all inputs into the *Input Image Table (IIT)*.
> 2. **Program Execution (Logic Solve)** — execute the user program using IIT.
> 3. **Output Scan** — write results of logic to *Output Image Table (OIT)* and physical outputs.
> 4. **Housekeeping (Overhead)** — communication, diagnostics, memory checks, timer updates.

### Conceptual Analogy
A PLC scan cycle is like a **factory supervisor doing rounds every 5 minutes**: (1) walks around and checks all gauges (Input Scan) → (2) decides what to do based on the readings (Program Execution) → (3) pushes buttons to start/stop machines (Output Scan) → (4) writes a logbook entry and checks his phone messages (Housekeeping). Then he repeats.

---

## 1.7 Capabilities of a PLC

A modern PLC can:
- Execute **relay logic, timers, counters, math, sequencers, and PID control**
- Communicate over **Ethernet/IP, PROFINET, Modbus TCP, DeviceNet, PROFIBUS**
- Handle **thousands of I/O points** (distributed)
- Provide **redundancy** (CPU, power, network) for critical processes
- Store and run **recipes and batch logic**
- Integrate **motion control, safety (SIL), and HMI/SCADA** connectivity
- Perform **data logging** and **alarms management**
- Offer **IEC 61131-3 programming** (Ladder, FBD, ST, SFC, IL)

---

## 1.8 Selection Criteria for a PLC

> [!NOTE]
> **Key parameters to evaluate before selecting a PLC:**
> 1. **Number and type of I/O** (Digital vs Analog, input vs output, voltages, current ratings)
> 2. **Memory size** (program memory, data memory, retentive memory)
> 3. **Scan time / Speed** (must be faster than the fastest process event)
> 4. **Communication protocols** required (Modbus, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA)
> 5. **Environmental conditions** (temperature, humidity, vibration, ingress protection)
> 6. **Special functions** (PID, motion, high-speed counters, interrupts, safety)
> 7. **Expandability** (extra slots, remote I/O, future I/O addition)
> 8. **Programming software availability and ease**
> 9. **Reliability, MTBF, redundancy options**
> 10. **Cost, brand support, local service availability**

### Conceptual Analogy
Selecting a PLC is like **buying a vehicle**. You wouldn't buy a sports car to haul bricks, nor a truck for a daily office commute. You match the vehicle to the job: load (I/O), terrain (environment), speed (scan time), and future trips (expandability).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: Digital I/O Operation

### Operational Logic
- **Threshold-based sensing:** The PLC module has a defined **ON threshold** (V_IH) and **OFF threshold** (V_IL) with a hysteresis band to prevent chattering.
- **Filtering:** Input modules include **RC filters** (typical 1–10 ms) to debounce mechanical contacts.
- **Sinking vs Sourcing:**
  - **Sinking input (NPN):** device *sinks* current *into* its terminal.
  - **Sourcing input (PNP):** device *sources* current *out* of its terminal.
  - The PLC and the field device *must match* (PNP ↔ PNP, NPN ↔ NPN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** "Differentiate between sourcing and sinking I/O and explain the compatibility rule." — worth 3 to 7 marks every cycle.

### Output Types
- **Relay output (Dry contact):** voltage-free, can switch AC or DC, slow (~10 ms), limited life (~100,000 operations).
- **Transistor output (Sink/Source):** DC only, fast (~0.1 ms), long life, requires flyback diode for inductive loads.
- **TRIAC output:** AC only, for solenoid valves and small AC loads.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Analog I/O Operation

### Operational Steps
1. **Transducer** (RTD, TC, pressure sensor) generates a low-level signal (e.g., mV, Ω).
2. **Transmitter** conditions the signal to **4–20 mA** (or 0–10 V) proportional to the measured variable.
3. **Analog input module** terminates the current loop across a **250 Ω sense resistor**, producing **1–5 V** internally, then a **multiplexer** routes to an **A/D converter**.
4. **A/D converter** produces a **binary count** (n bits) representing the signal.
5. The CPU's program scales the raw count to **engineering units** (e.g., 0–100 °C).

### Why 4–20 mA and not 0–20 mA?
- **4 mA = 0 %** → preserves a "live zero" to detect a **broken wire** (0 mA = fault condition).
- Current signals are **immune to line resistance** and **noise pickup** (they remain constant over kilometers of wire).

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive: PLC Power Supply Architecture

### Block-Level Understanding
1. **Input filter** (line filter, surge arrestor, EMI choke)
2. **Bridge rectifier** (for AC input) or **DC bus** (for DC input)
3. **Bulk capacitor** (energy storage, ripple smoothing)
4. **Switching regulator / Flyback converter** (high-frequency SMPS)
5. **Isolation transformer** (galvanic isolation between mains and low-voltage side)
6. **Secondary rectifiers and LDO regulators** → +5 V, +12 V, +24 V rails
7. **Supervisory circuit (UVLO/OVP)** to protect against under/over-voltage

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Modern PLCs use **Switched Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** because they are lighter, cooler, and more efficient (typically 80–90 % efficiency) than linear supplies (30–50 %).

---

## 2.4 Deep Dive: Isolator Function

- **Opto-isolator:** LED on input side emits photons → phototransistor on output side conducts. Isolation barrier: **5000 V RMS** typical.
- **Bandwidth:** Opto-isolators are fast enough for digital I/O and low-frequency analog. For high-speed analog (> 10 kHz), use transformer or capacitive isolators.
- **Common-mode rejection:** Isolators reject voltages appearing simultaneously on both signal lines — critical in long cable runs in plants.

---

## 2.5 Deep Dive: Scan Cycle Timing

### Total Scan Time
The total scan time is composed of:

$$
T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}
$$

Where:
- $T_{input}$ = time to scan all input points (typically 8 µs per point)
- $T_{program}$ = time to execute one full pass of user logic
- $T_{output}$ = time to update all output points
- $T_{housekeeping}$ = communication servicing, diagnostics

### Program Execution Time
For a typical ladder-logic rung with one contact and one coil:

$$
T_{program} \approx N_{rungs} \times T_{rung}
$$

For a Boolean instruction cycle time of $T_{inst} \approx 0.1$ to $0.5$ µs:

$$
T_{program} \approx N_{instructions} \times T_{inst}
$$

### Scan Time Estimation Example
For 256 digital I/O scanned at 8 µs each, plus 5 ms program execution and 2 ms overhead:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{input} &= 256 \times 8 \, \mu s = 2.048 \, ms \\
T_{output} &= 256 \times 8 \, \mu s = 2.048 \, ms \\
T_{program} &= 5 \, ms \\
T_{housekeeping} &= 2 \, ms \\
T_{scan} &= 2.048 + 5 + 2.048 + 2 = 11.096 \, ms
\end{aligned}
$$

This translates to:

$$
f_{scan} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{11.096 \, ms} \approx 90.12 \, Hz
$$

---

## 2.6 Deep Dive: Capabilities — Modern PLC Functional Stack

| Layer | Capability | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Bit-level | Relay logic, latches, timers, counters | Motor start/stop logic |
| 2. Word-level | Math, comparison, data move, scaling | Flow totalization, PID |
| 3. Communication | Industrial networks | Modbus, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP |
| 4. Motion | High-speed counters, pulse train, servo | Stepper control |
| 5. Safety | SIL-rated I/O, redundant processors | E-stop, light curtain |
| 6. Redundancy | Hot-standby CPUs | Power plant DCS layer |
| 7. Integration | OPC UA, MQTT, SQL | IIoT / Industry 4.0 |

---

## 2.7 Deep Dive: Selection Criteria — Decision Tree

```
Start → How many I/O?
   ├── < 32 → Compact PLC
   ├── 32–256 → Modular slim PLC
   └── > 256 → Modular rack PLC + Distributed I/O
       │
       └── Scan time requirement?
            ├── > 50 ms OK → Mid-range PLC
            └── < 10 ms → High-performance PLC (e.g., Allen-Bradley ControlLogix)
                │
                └── Analog signals?
                     ├── Few → Built-in AI/AO modules
                     └── Many → Dedicated analog rack with signal conditioning
                          │
                          └── Safety required?
                               ├── No → Standard
                               └── Yes → Safety PLC (e.g., Siemens F-CPU, Pilz)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 70 % rule (KTU standard):** Always select a PLC with at least **30 % spare I/O capacity** above the calculated maximum. This is a common viva question.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ in formulas (not the pipe symbol) so markdown tables render correctly.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resolution $= \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage, $n$ = A/D bits | V, bits |
| 2 | LSB $= \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | Smallest detectable change | V |
| 3 | Quantization error $= \pm \dfrac{LSB}{2}$ | Max $\pm$ 0.5 LSB | V |
| 4 | Engineering units $= \left( \dfrac{Raw - Raw_{min}}{Raw_{max} - Raw_{min}} \right) \times (EU_{max} - EU_{min}) + EU_{min}$ | Linear scaling | EU |
| 5 | $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$ | Total scan time | ms |
| 6 | $f_{scan} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Scan frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $T_{program} \approx N_{inst} \times t_{inst}$ | Program execution time | ms |
| 8 | Power $P = V \times I$ | Electrical power | W |
| 9 | Efficiency $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | PSU efficiency | % |
| 10 | Loop voltage $V_{loop} = V_{psu} - (I \times R_{loop})$ | 4–20 mA loop budget | V |
| 11 | Voltage from current: $V = I \times R_{sense}$ | With $R_{sense} = 250 \, \Omega$ typical | V |
| 12 | Spare I/O rule: $I/O_{PLC} \geq 1.3 \times I/O_{calculated}$ | 30 % spare margin | points |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: A/D Resolution and Quantization

A 12-bit A/D converter in a PLC analog input module accepts a 0–10 V signal.

### Step 1: Identify parameters
- Full-scale voltage $V_{FS} = 10 \, V$
- Number of bits $n = 12$

### Step 2: Compute total discrete levels

$$
N_{levels} = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

There are 4096 distinct digital codes, from 0 to 4095.

### Step 3: Compute the resolution (1 LSB)

$$
Resolution = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{10}{4096 - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3} \, V
$$

### Step 4: Convert to engineering units (0–100 °C)
Each LSB corresponds to:

$$
EU_{per\_LSB} = \frac{EU_{max} - EU_{min}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{100 - 0}{4095} = 0.0244 \, ^\circ C
$$

### Step 5: Quantization error bound

$$
Q_{error} = \pm \frac{LSB}{2} = \pm \frac{2.442 \, mV}{2} = \pm 1.221 \, mV
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** A 12-bit A/D on a 0–10 V / 0–100 °C transmitter gives a theoretical temperature resolution of **~0.024 °C per count** with a maximum quantization uncertainty of **±1.22 mV**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: 4–20 mA Current Loop to Engineering Units

A pressure transmitter outputs 4–20 mA for 0–10 bar. The PLC reads the raw count 12288 on a 16-bit A/D (0–65535).

### Step 1: Identify linear scaling relationship
The general linear transfer function:

$$
EU = \left( \frac{Raw - Raw_{4mA}}{Raw_{20mA} - Raw_{4mA}} \right) \times (EU_{max} - EU_{min}) + EU_{min}
$$

### Step 2: Compute raw counts at endpoints
For a 16-bit A/D, full range 0–65535:
- $Raw_{4mA} = \dfrac{4}{20} \times 65535 = 13107$
- $Raw_{20mA} = \dfrac{20}{20} \times 65535 = 65535$

### Step 3: Plug in observed value

$$
EU = \left( \frac{12288 - 13107}{65535 - 13107} \right) \times (10 - 0) + 0
$$

### Step 4: Compute numerator and denominator

$$
EU = \left( \frac{-819}{52428} \right) \times 10
$$

### Step 5: Final value

$$
EU = -0.156 \, bar
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative result interpretation:** A raw count of 12288 corresponds to **3.75 mA**, which is *below* the 4 mA live-zero. This indicates a **broken wire, failed sensor, or out-of-range undervalue** — the PLC should flag a "below range" fault.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Scan Time for a Modular PLC

A modular PLC has the following scan composition:
- 16 digital input modules × 16 channels each = **256 DI**, scanned at 1 µs/channel
- 8 digital output modules × 16 channels each = **128 DO**, scanned at 1 µs/channel
- 4 analog input modules × 8 channels each = **32 AI**, scanned at 50 µs/channel
- Program: **4000 Boolean instructions** at 0.2 µs/instruction
- Communication overhead: 3 ms

### Step 1: Input scan time

$$
T_{input} = (256 \times 1) + (32 \times 50) = 256 + 1600 = 1856 \, \mu s = 1.856 \, ms
$$

### Step 2: Output scan time

$$
T_{output} = 128 \times 1 = 128 \, \mu s = 0.128 \, ms
$$

### Step 3: Program execution time

$$
T_{program} = 4000 \times 0.2 = 800 \, \mu s = 0.8 \, ms
$$

### Step 4: Housekeeping

$$
T_{housekeeping} = 3 \, ms
$$

### Step 5: Total scan time

$$
T_{scan} = 1.856 + 0.128 + 0.8 + 3 = 5.784 \, ms
$$

### Step 6: Scan frequency

$$
f_{scan} = \frac{1}{5.784 \, ms} = 172.9 \, Hz
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering judgment:** For a packaging line operating at 100 cycles/min (1.67 Hz), 172.9 Hz scan is **massively over-specified**. This means the PLC has plenty of bandwidth for future expansion — confirming the 30 % spare margin principle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Loop Voltage Compliance

A 4–20 mA transmitter is powered by a 24 V DC PLC analog input card. The cable run is 200 m of 18 AWG copper (resistance ≈ 21 Ω/km loop).

### Step 1: Loop wire resistance

$$
R_{loop} = 21 \, \frac{\Omega}{km} \times 0.2 \, km \times 2 = 8.4 \, \Omega
$$

(Factor of 2 for the return path.)

### Step 2: Voltage drop at 20 mA maximum

$$
V_{drop} = I_{max} \times R_{loop} = 0.020 \times 8.4 = 0.168 \, V
$$

### Step 3: Voltage available at transmitter

$$
V_{tx} = 24 - 0.168 = 23.832 \, V
$$

### Step 4: Verify transmitter compliance
If the transmitter requires a minimum of **12 V** to operate, then **23.83 V >> 12 V** → the loop is healthy. If we ever extended to 2 km, the drop would be 1.68 V → still OK. Beyond ~3 km, we would need a higher-voltage supply or a 4-wire (separately excited) transmitter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The 4–20 mA current loop is the **most robust industrial signal standard** because:
> - The current is **independent of loop resistance** (Ohm's law on the transmitter side regulates it).
> - It tolerates **long cable runs, contact resistance, and EMI** without losing accuracy.
> - A 4 mA live-zero enables **fault detection** (0 mA = broken wire).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Power Supply Sizing

A PLC has the following internal loads:
- CPU: 1.5 A at +5 V
- 2 I/O modules: 0.4 A each at +5 V
- 1 analog module: 0.3 A at +24 V
- 1 communication module: 0.6 A at +5 V
- Field-side sensor load: 1.0 A at +24 V

### Step 1: +5 V rail

$$
I_{+5V} = 1.5 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.6 = 2.9 \, A
$$

### Step 2: +24 V rail

$$
I_{+24V} = 0.3 + 1.0 = 1.3 \, A
$$

### Step 3: Power on each rail

$$
P_{+5V} = 2.9 \times 5 = 14.5 \, W
$$

$$
P_{+24V} = 1.3 \times 24 = 31.2 \, W
$$

### Step 4: Total output power

$$
P_{out} = 14.5 + 31.2 = 45.7 \, W
$$

### Step 5: Required input power (assuming 85 % efficiency)

$$
P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{45.7}{0.85} = 53.76 \, W
$$

### Step 6: AC input current at 230 V

$$
I_{AC} = \frac{P_{in}}{V_{AC}} = \frac{53.76}{230} = 0.234 \, A
$$

The PLC should be protected by a **0.5 A or 1 A MCB** on the AC mains side.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Digital vs Analog I/O

| Parameter | Digital I/O | Analog I/O |
|---|---|---|
| Signal type | Binary (ON/OFF) | Continuous (4–20 mA, 0–10 V) |
| States | 2 | Infinite (within resolution) |
| Conversion | None (or debounce) | A/D or D/A |
| Typical modules | 8, 16, 32 channel | 4, 8 channel |
| Speed | Fast (µs) | Slower (ms) |
| Wiring | Multi-core | Twisted pair, shielded |
| Noise immunity | High | Low (needs shielding) |
| Cost per point | Low | High |
| Failure mode | Stuck-at-0 or stuck-at-1 | Out-of-range, drift |
| Programming | Boolean | Integer / Float |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: PLC Selection Decision Table

| Criterion | Compact PLC | Modular PLC | Distributed PLC | Safety PLC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I/O count | < 64 | 64–2000 | > 2000 | Variable |
| Expandability | Limited | High | Very High | Application-specific |
| Scan time | 5–20 ms | 1–10 ms | < 1 ms | 5–20 ms |
| Cost per point | High | Medium | Low | Very High |
| Programming | Ladder only | IEC 61131-3 | IEC 61131-3 | Certified languages |
| Best suited for | OEM machines | Process plants | Large plants | Safety loops |
| Example brands | Siemens LOGO!, Schneider Zelio | Siemens S7-1500, AB CompactLogix | Siemens ET 200, AB POINT I/O | Pilz PNOZ, Siemens F-CPU |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PLC Scan Cycle — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([PLC Powered ON]) --> initA[Initialize Hardware and Memory]
    initA --> phase1[/Phase 1: Input Scan/]
    phase1 --> readDI[Read all Digital Inputs into IIT]
    readDI --> readAI[Read all Analog Inputs via MUX and A/D]
    readAI --> phase2[/Phase 2: Program Execution/]
    phase2 --> fetchI[Fetch Instruction from Memory]
    fetchI --> decodeI[Decode and Execute Logic]
    decodeI --> branchI{End of Program?}
    branchI -- No --> fetchI
    branchI -- Yes --> phase3[/Phase 3: Output Scan/]
    phase3 --> writeDO[Update Digital Outputs from OIT]
    writeDO --> writeAO[Update Analog Outputs via D/A]
    writeAO --> phase4[/Phase 4: Housekeeping/]
    phase4 --> commH[Service Communication Requests]
    commH --> diagH[Run Self-Diagnostics]
    diagH --> timerH[Update Internal Timers and Counters]
    timerH --> backA([Repeat Cycle Continuously])
    backA --> phase1
```

---

## 4.2 PLC Configuration — Modular Rack Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    psu1[AC-DC Power Supply 230V AC to 24V DC] --> backplane1[Rack Backplane with Parallel Bus]
    backplane1 --> cpu1[CPU Module with Processor and Memory]
    backplane1 --> di1[Digital Input Module 24V DC 32Ch]
    backplane1 --> do1[Digital Output Module Relay 16Ch]
    backplane1 --> ai1[Analog Input Module 4-20mA 8Ch]
    backplane1 --> ao1[Analog Output Module 0-10V 4Ch]
    backplane1 --> comm1[Communication Module PROFINET or Modbus]
    cpu1 --> comm1
    cpu1 --> psu1
    comm1 --> external1[External HMI SCADA and Engineering Station]
    comm1 --> external2[Distributed I/O Stations over Fieldbus]
    di1 --> field1[Field Sensors Pushbuttons Limit Switches]
    do1 --> field2[Field Actuators Contactors Solenoids Lamps]
    ai1 --> field3[Field Transmitters Temperature Pressure Flow]
    ao1 --> field4[Control Elements Valves VFDs Dampers]
```

---

## 4.3 Isolator Function — Optical Isolation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fieldSide1[Field Side Circuit 120V AC or 24V DC] --> led1[LED Infrared Emitter]
    led1 -.Photon Coupling.-> photo1[Phototransistor Receiver]
    photo1 --> plcsafe[PLC Internal Logic 5V DC Logic]
    note1[Barrier up to 5000V RMS]
    led1 --- note1
    photo1 --- note1
    ground1[Field Ground] -.- fieldSide1
    ground2[Logic Ground] -.- plcsafe
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed line represents the **galvanic isolation barrier** — there is **no direct electrical connection** between the field side and the PLC logic side. Signal transfer occurs only via light.

---

## 4.4 AC-DC Power Supply Functional Blocks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains1[AC Mains 230V 50Hz] --> emi1[EMI Filter and Surge Protection]
    emi1 --> rect1[Bridge Rectifier to Pulsating DC]
    rect1 --> pfc1[Power Factor Correction Stage]
    pfc1 --> bulk1[Bulk Capacitor DC Link ~400V]
    bulk1 --> switch1[High-Frequency Switching MOSFET]
    switch1 --> xformer1[HF Isolation Transformer]
    xformer1 --> sec1[Secondary Side Rectifier and Filter]
    sec1 --> reg1[Linear or LDO Regulators]
    reg1 --> rail1[Output +5V DC to CPU and Logic]
    reg1 --> rail2[Output +12V DC to Analog Circuits]
    reg1 --> rail3[Output +24V DC to Field and Sensors]
    ctrl1[Feedback and PWM Control Loop] -.-> switch1
    sec1 -.Feedback.-> ctrl1
```

---

## 4.5 Digital I/O — Sinking vs Sourcing Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOURCING_PNP
        ps1[24V DC Positive] --> sw1[Field Sensor PNP]
        sw1 --> di1[PLC Digital Input Terminal]
        di1 --> gnd1[24V DC Common Negative]
    end
    subgraph SINKING_NPN
        ps2[24V DC Positive] --> di2[PLC Digital Input Terminal]
        di2 --> sw2[Field Sensor NPN]
        sw2 --> gnd2[24V DC Common Negative]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** In **PNP (sourcing) systems**, current *flows out* of the sensor to the input. In **NPN (sinking) systems**, current *flows into* the sensor. Europe uses PNP dominantly; Japan uses NPN dominantly.

---

## 4.6 Analog Input Module — Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fld1[Field Transmitter 4-20mA] --> term1[Input Terminal Block with Surge Protection]
    term1 --> fuse1[Self-resetting PTC Fuse]
    fuse1 --> rsen1[Sense Resistor 250 ohm]
    rsen1 --> mux1[Analog Multiplexer 8 or 16 Channel]
    mux1 --> amp1[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    amp1 --> filter1[Anti-aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    filter1 --> adc1[16-bit A/D Converter]
    adc1 --> iso1[Digital Isolator Optocoupler]
    iso1 --> cpubus1[CPU Backplane Data Bus]
    cpubus1 --> cpu1[CPU reads scaled value]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions are designed for KTU continuous assessment / internal tests and short-answer viva. Each carries **3 marks**, with the model answer covering the expected **1 page** of writing.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**Differentiate between digital and analog inputs in a PLC with one industrial example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Digital inputs** are two-state (ON/OFF, Logic 1/0) signals connected to devices such as **push buttons, limit switches, proximity sensors**. The PLC detects whether the input voltage is above (ON) or below (OFF) a fixed threshold (e.g., 15 V threshold for 24 V DC systems).

**Analog inputs** are continuously variable signals connected to **transmitters** that output a standard range like **4–20 mA or 0–10 V**, representing variables such as temperature, pressure, flow, or level. The PLC's A/D converter digitizes these into a binary count and the program scales them to engineering units.

**Example:** A **limit switch** is a digital input (valve open/closed). A **RTD temperature transmitter** is an analog input (0–200 °C represented as 4–20 mA).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]
**What is the function of an opto-isolator in a PLC I/O module?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
An **opto-isolator** (or optocoupler) provides **galvanic isolation** between the field-side circuitry and the PLC's internal logic. It consists of an **LED on the input side** and a **phototransistor on the output side**, with no electrical connection between them — only light couples the signal.

The **functions** are:
1. **Protects** the sensitive CPU from voltage transients, surges, and field-side faults (up to **5000 V isolation**).
2. **Eliminates ground loops** that cause measurement errors.
3. **Enhances operator safety** by preventing hazardous voltages from reaching the logic side.

---

## 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module contains either **one full 14-mark question** or **two 7-mark sub-questions** with internal choice. Below are **TWO alternative 14-mark questions** (Choice A and Choice B) with **part (a) at 7 marks and part (b) at 7 marks** and complete model solutions.

---

### 📕 QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice A)

#### [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **PLC scan cycle** in detail. Draw a labeled block diagram showing all four phases. Why is the **scan time** an important parameter in high-speed control applications?

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A 4–20 mA pressure transmitter is connected to a 12-bit analog input module of a PLC. The transmitter is calibrated for 0–10 bar. When the process pressure is 7.5 bar, calculate the expected raw count. Also calculate the resolution in bar.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — PLC Scan Cycle (7 marks)**

The **PLC scan cycle** is the continuous, sequential set of operations the CPU performs repeatedly to interface with the process. The four phases are:

1. **Input Scan (Read Phase):** The CPU reads the current ON/OFF state of every digital input and the instantaneous value of every analog input, storing the values in the **Input Image Table (IIT)** in RAM. The actual input terminals are not accessed again during program execution — the IIT snapshot is used.

2. **Program Execution (Logic Solve Phase):** The CPU executes the user program (typically stored in ladder logic) **rung-by-rung, top to bottom, left to right**. It uses the IIT as inputs, evaluates the boolean/logic expressions, and updates intermediate results. Output values are not yet sent to the field — they are written to the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.

3. **Output Scan (Write Phase):** The CPU copies the values from the OIT to the physical output modules, energizing or de-energizing relays, transistors, or analog output signals.

4. **Housekeeping (Overhead Phase):** The CPU services communication requests, updates internal timers and counters, performs self-diagnostics on memory and I/O, and prepares for the next cycle.

**Block Diagram (already shown in SECTION 4.1).**

**Importance of Scan Time:**
- For high-speed events (e.g., a bottle on a conveyor at 600 bottles/min, or a safety light curtain), the PLC **must detect the event and respond within the time interval between consecutive events**.
- If scan time > event interval, the PLC **misses events** — leading to defective products or unsafe operation.
- For an event interval of 10 ms, scan time must be < 10 ms; ideally scan time should be **less than 50 %** of the event interval for safe operation.

---

**Part (b) — Analog Scaling Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Input: 4–20 mA for 0–10 bar
- A/D resolution: 12 bits → $2^{12} = 4096$ levels (0 to 4095)
- Measured pressure: 7.5 bar

**Step 1:** Compute current at 7.5 bar
Using linear interpolation between 4 mA (= 0 bar) and 20 mA (= 10 bar):

$$
I = 4 + \left( \frac{7.5 - 0}{10 - 0} \right) \times (20 - 4) = 4 + 0.75 \times 16 = 4 + 12 = 16 \, mA
$$

**Step 2:** Convert current to raw count
Assuming 0 mA = 0 counts and 20 mA = 4095 counts:

$$
Raw = \frac{I}{20} \times 4095 = \frac{16}{20} \times 4095 = 0.8 \times 4095 = 3276
$$

**Step 3:** Compute resolution in bar
Full-scale engineering range = 10 bar, total counts = 4095:

$$
Resolution = \frac{10 \, bar}{4095} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3} \, bar = 2.44 \, mbar
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Expected raw count = **3276**
- Resolution = **2.44 mbar per count**

---

### 📙 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice B)

#### [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **selection criteria for a PLC** for an industrial automation project. List any **six technical parameters** and justify each in one sentence.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A PLC is to be installed in a cement plant area where the ambient temperature reaches 55 °C and dust concentration is high. The system must monitor **40 digital inputs, 24 digital outputs, 8 analog inputs (4–20 mA) and 4 analog outputs (0–10 V)**. It must communicate with an HMI over **Modbus TCP** and have a **redundant CPU** for the kiln control loop.
Recommend a suitable PLC configuration (compact/modular/distributed) and justify with reference to the **70 % spare I/O rule**, **environmental specs**, and **redundancy requirement**.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — Selection Criteria for a PLC (7 marks)**

The selection of a PLC for an industrial application must consider the following technical parameters:

1. **Number and Type of I/O:** Total DI, DO, AI, AO required today and projected for the next 5 years. The PLC must provide at least 30 % spare capacity.

2. **Memory Size:** Program memory (typically 8 KB to several MB) and data memory for recipes, logs, retentive tags.

3. **Scan Time:** Must be faster than the fastest process event. For servo motion, scan times < 1 ms are needed.

4. **Communication Protocols:** Must support fieldbus networks such as **Modbus, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet** for HMI, SCADA, and distributed I/O integration.

5. **Environmental Specifications:** Operating temperature (typically 0–55 °C), humidity, vibration, IP rating (e.g., IP65 for washdown areas), and conformal coating for dust and corrosive atmospheres.

6. **Special Functions:** Built-in PID, high-speed counters, motion control, real-time clock, interrupts, and safety (SIL) capabilities.

7. **Redundancy:** Hot-standby CPUs, redundant power supplies, and redundant communication for critical processes.

8. **Programming Software Support:** IEC 61131-3 language support (Ladder, FBD, ST), simulation, online editing, and library availability.

9. **Reliability and Brand Support:** MTBF > 100,000 hours, local service, spare availability, training, and documentation.

10. **Cost:** Total cost of ownership (TCO) including hardware, software licenses, training, and maintenance over 10 years.

---

**Part (b) — PLC Recommendation (7 marks)**

**Given System Requirements:**
- 40 DI, 24 DO, 8 AI, 4 AO
- Modbus TCP communication with HMI
- Redundant CPU for kiln control
- 55 °C ambient, high dust
- Industrial environment

**Step 1: Total I/O Count**

$$
I/O_{total} = 40 + 24 + 8 + 4 = 76 \, points
$$

**Step 2: Apply 70 % Spare Rule**

$$
I/O_{PLC} = \frac{76}{0.7} = 108.6 \rightarrow 128 \, points \, (minimum)
$$

**Step 3: Recommended Configuration — Modular Rack PLC with Redundant CPU**

Justification:

| Requirement | Recommended Solution |
|---|---|
| 76 I/O + 30 % spare | **Modular PLC with at least 128 I/O capacity** (e.g., Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Schneider M580). Compact PLCs are too small for this. |
| Modbus TCP | Choose a CPU with built-in **Ethernet port supporting Modbus TCP**, or add a dedicated communication module. |
| Redundant CPU | Select a **hot-standby redundant CPU pair** (e.g., Siemens S7-1500R/H, AB ControlLogix Hot Backup, or Schneider M580 Hot Standby). |
| 55 °C + dust | Specify **conformal-coated modules**, **IP20 minimum**, and **extended temperature range (–25 to +70 °C)**. Install in a **sealed air-conditioned panel with IP54 enclosure and dust filters**. |
| Future expandability | Modular rack allows easy addition of I/O cards as the plant grows. |
| 4 AI (4–20 mA) + 4 AO (0–10 V) | Use a dedicated **8-channel AI module** (4–20 mA) and a **4-channel AO module** (0–10 V) — separate from digital modules to avoid noise coupling. |

**Step 4: Final Configuration**

> A **Modular Redundant-CPU PLC** (e.g., Siemens S7-1517H or equivalent) with the following modules is recommended:
> - 1 × Redundant CPU pair (Hot-Standby)
> - 2 × 24 V DC, 5 A redundant power supply
> - 3 × 16-channel DI module (48 DI → 8 spare)
> - 2 × 16-channel DO relay module (32 DO → 8 spare)
> - 1 × 8-channel AI module (4–20 mA)
> - 1 × 4-channel AO module (0–10 V)
> - 1 × Ethernet communication module (Modbus TCP / PROFINET)

**Justification of 70 % rule:** The calculated I/O is 76. The chosen PLC provides 48 + 32 + 8 + 4 = **92 directly wired points**, but the rack supports **128 points**, leaving ~30 % spare for future expansion — satisfying the KTU-recommended 70 % utilization rule.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "I/O type" with "I/O direction":** Students write "analog input" when they mean "4–20 mA output" — always state direction (in/out) and signal type (current/voltage).
> 2. **Confusing scan time with program execution time:** Scan time includes input + program + output + housekeeping — not just program time. Marks lost if only program time is given.
> 3. **Forgetting the 4 mA live-zero in 4–20 mA calculations:** When raw count is below the 4 mA equivalent, students compute a *negative* engineering unit and forget to flag it as a **fault condition**.
> 4. **Mixing up isolation types:** Students write "transformer" for digital I/O isolation — the correct answer is **opto-isolator** for digital, transformer for analog power isolation.
> 5. **Skipping justification in selection-criteria questions:** The KTU rubric requires *justification* of each criterion. Writing a one-line list without explanation loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Wrong formula for resolution:** Using $2^{n}$ instead of $2^{n} - 1$ in the denominator is a common 1-mark error.
> 7. **Not drawing the scan cycle diagram when asked:** A neat labeled block diagram is worth **3 of the 7 marks** in Part (a) of the scan cycle question.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final quick-glance summary — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Digital I/O:** Two-state (ON/OFF). Threshold-based. Examples: push buttons, limit switches (DI); contactors, solenoids, lamps (DO). Voltages: 24 V DC, 120/230 V AC.
- ✅ **Analog I/O:** Continuously variable. Examples: RTD, thermocouple, pressure, flow, level (AI); VFDs, control valves, dampers (AO). Signal ranges: 0–10 V, 4–20 mA.
- ✅ **4–20 mA preferred over 0–20 mA:** Live-zero at 4 mA enables **broken-wire fault detection** (0 mA = fault).
- ✅ **Sinking (NPN) vs Sourcing (PNP):** Match the sensor type to the PLC input type. Europe = PNP dominant.
- ✅ **Output types:** Relay (AC/DC, slow), Transistor (DC only, fast), TRIAC (AC only).
- ✅ **PLC Power Supply:** AC mains (230 V) or DC (24 V) → SMPS → +5 V, +12 V, +24 V isolated rails. Modern PLCs use **SMPS** for efficiency.
- ✅ **Isolators:** Opto-isolators (LED + phototransistor) provide up to 5000 V galvanic isolation. Prevent ground loops, protect CPU, ensure safety.
- ✅ **PLC Configurations:** Compact (small), Modular (rack), Distributed (remote I/O), Safety (SIL), Redundant (hot-standby).
- ✅ **Scan Cycle 4 Phases:** Input Scan → Program Execution → Output Scan → Housekeeping. Total scan time = sum of all four phases.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$
- ✅ **Resolution formula:** $Resolution = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n} - 1}$
- ✅ **Quantization error:** $\pm \dfrac{LSB}{2}$
- ✅ **Linear scaling:** $EU = \left(\dfrac{Raw - Raw_{4mA}}{Raw_{20mA} - Raw_{4mA}}\right) \times (EU_{max} - EU_{min}) + EU_{min}$
- ✅ **70 % Spare I/O Rule:** Always size the PLC for $1.3 \times$ the calculated I/O count.
- ✅ **Selection Criteria:** I/O count & type, memory, scan time, communication, environment, special functions, redundancy, cost, brand support.
- ✅ **Scan Time Rule of Thumb:** Scan time should be **less than 50 %** of the fastest process event interval.
- ✅ **4–20 mA Loop Compliance:** Verify $V_{loop} \geq V_{transmitter(min)}$ at maximum loop resistance.
- ✅ **PLC vs DCS:** PLCs are for **discrete/fast logic and machine control**; DCS for **continuous, large-scale process control**. (This comparison is likely to appear in Module 2/3.)
- ✅ **IEC 61131-3 Languages:** Ladder Diagram (LD), Function Block Diagram (FBD), Structured Text (ST), Instruction List (IL), Sequential Function Chart (SFC).
- ✅ **Industrial Buses to remember:** Modbus RTU/TCP, PROFINET, PROFIBUS, EtherNet/IP, DeviceNet, OPC UA.
- ✅ **Safety:** SIL-1, SIL-2, SIL-3 levels — safety PLCs needed for E-stop, light curtain, interlock circuits.
- ✅ **Always draw a labeled block diagram** when asked for "explain the scan cycle / power supply / isolator" — 2 to 3 marks depend on the diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Communication with PC and Software - PLC Wiring - Installation of PLC and its Modules. PLC Programming Languages: Ladder Logic, Functional Block Diagram (FBD), Sequential Flow Chart (SFC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT412 - PLC & DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PLC Communication with PC and Software - PLC Wiring - Installation of PLC and its Modules. PLC Programming Languages: Ladder Logic, Functional Block Diagram (FBD), Sequential Flow Chart (SFC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 PLC Communication with PC and Software

### Formal Definition
**PLC Communication with PC** refers to the bidirectional data exchange interface established between a Programmable Logic Controller and a personal computer (or engineering workstation) using proprietary or standardized industrial communication protocols. This communication is essential for **programming**, **monitoring**, **supervisory control**, **data logging**, and **firmware updates**.

The PC side typically runs a **vendor-specific PLC programming software** (e.g., Siemens TIA Portal, Allen-Bradley RSLogix/Studio 5000, Schneider EcoStruxure, Mitsubishi GX Works) which acts as the HMI for the engineer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For PERAT412 Module 1, you must understand *both* the physical layer (cables, connectors, ports) and the software layer (programming environment, project structure, online/offline modes).

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a PLC as a **sealed industrial brain** mounted on a factory wall. It has no screen and no keyboard of its own (just indicator LEDs). To "talk" to it, you need:
- A **translator** (the programming software on the PC), and
- A **telephone line** (the communication cable and protocol).

The PC asks, *"What does Input I:0/0 show right now?"* — the PLC replies, *"TRUE."* Conversely, the engineer says, *"When I:0/0 is TRUE, energize O:0/1"* — the PLC stores this in its logic.

### Common Communication Interfaces
| Interface | Standard | Typical Use | Max Distance |
|-----------|----------|-------------|--------------|
| **RS-232** | Point-to-point | PC-to-PLC programming port | **15 m** |
| **RS-422** | Multi-drop (1 driver, 10 receivers) | Long-distance programming | **1200 m** |
| **RS-485** | Multi-drop (32 nodes) | Fieldbus for PLC networks | **1200 m** |
| **Ethernet/IP** | IEEE 802.3 | Modern high-speed communication | **100 m** per segment |
| **USB** | USB 2.0/3.0 | Direct programming (modern PLCs) | **5 m** |
| **Profibus-DP** | IEC 61158 | Siemens/European fieldbus | **1200 m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Communication Parameters (must match on both sides):** Baud rate (e.g., 9600, 19200, 115200 bps), Parity (None/Even/Odd), Data bits (7 or 8), Stop bits (1 or 2). Mismatch in **even one** of these parameters results in a communication failure — a classic KTU exam trap.

---

## 1.2 PLC Wiring

### Formal Definition
**PLC Wiring** is the systematic interconnection of input devices, output devices, power supplies, communication cables, and field sensors/actuators to the PLC's terminal blocks, following **IEC 61131** standards, **NFPA 79** (electrical standard for industrial machinery), and the manufacturer's installation guidelines.

### Types of Wiring Categories
1. **Power Supply Wiring** — Main AC (110/230 V) or DC (24 V) to the PLC's power terminals.
2. **Input Wiring** — Connecting field input devices (sensors, switches, pushbuttons) to the input module.
3. **Output Wiring** — Connecting output loads (relays, solenoids, contactors, lamps) to the output module.
4. **Communication Wiring** — Network cables (shielded twisted pair, Cat5e/Cat6, Profibus cable) to comm ports.
5. **Grounding/Earthing Wiring** — Protective Earth (PE) and Signal Ground connections.

### Conceptual Analogy (Wiring = The Nervous System)
If the **CPU is the brain** and the **software is the mind**, then the wiring is the **nervous system**. The brain cannot function without nerves carrying signals. Similarly, every sensor signal must travel through a properly shielded, properly terminated wire to the input module — otherwise noise, voltage drops, and crosstalk will corrupt the signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sinking vs. Sourcing — A KTU Hot Topic:**
> - **Sourcing (PNP) device:** Provides current **into** the input terminal (current flows from device → PLC input). Common in **European (Siemens)** systems.
> - **Sinking (NPN) device:** Pulls current **from** the input terminal (current flows from PLC input → device). Common in **Asian (Mitsubishi, Omron)** systems.
> - You must verify whether the PLC input module is **sinking or sourcing compatible** before wiring — otherwise the device will never be detected.

---

## 1.3 Installation of PLC and its Modules

### Formal Definition
**PLC Installation** is the structured mechanical and electrical procedure of mounting the PLC chassis (rack), inserting power supplies, CPUs, I/O modules, and communication modules, wiring them correctly, and configuring them in software to create a fully operational control system.

### Typical Installation Sequence
1. **Site Survey & Environmental Check** — Temperature (0–55 °C), humidity, dust, vibration, EMI sources.
2. **DIN-Rail / Panel Mounting** — Mount the chassis onto a standard **35 mm DIN rail** or panel.
3. **Module Insertion** — Power Supply → CPU → I/O Modules → Communication Modules.
4. **Wiring & Termination** — Torque the screw terminals to spec (typically **0.5–0.8 N·m**).
5. **Grounding** — Connect protective earth using **≥ 2 mm²** copper conductor.
6. **Software Configuration** — Define hardware in the programming software (rack/slot assignment).
7. **Power-Up & Diagnostic Test** — Verify LED status, run I/O check routine.
8. **Backup & Documentation** — Save project to server, label every wire.

### Conceptual Analogy
Installing a PLC is like **assembling a high-end desktop computer**, but in an industrial environment. The motherboard is the **CPU**, the RAM sticks are **I/O modules**, the PSU is the **power supply**, and the network card is the **communication module**. You wouldn't install a graphics card in a RAM slot, would you? Similarly, **each module must go in the correct slot** in the PLC rack.

---

## 1.4 PLC Programming Languages — Overview

The **IEC 61131-3** standard defines **5 official PLC programming languages**:

| # | Language | Acronym | Type | Common Use |
|---|----------|---------|------|------------|
| 1 | **Ladder Diagram** | LD | Graphical | Boolean logic, interlocks (most common) |
| 2 | **Function Block Diagram** | FBD | Graphical | Process control, math, PID loops |
| 3 | **Sequential Function Chart** | SFC | Graphical | Sequential/batch processes |
| 4 | **Structured Text** | ST | Textual | Complex math, loops, conditional logic |
| 5 | **Instruction List** | IL | Textual | Legacy, low-level (deprecated) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 focuses on the three graphical languages: Ladder, FBD, and SFC.**

### 1.4.1 Ladder Logic (LD)
**Ladder Diagram (LAD)** is the most widely used PLC programming language. It visually mimics electrical relay logic schematics, using two vertical **rails** (L1 and L2) and horizontal **rungs** containing contacts, coils, and function blocks.

**Core Symbols:**
- `| |` — **Normally Open (NO) contact** — passes power when its referenced bit is TRUE.
- `|/|` — **Normally Closed (NC) contact** — passes power when its referenced bit is FALSE.
- `( )` — **Output coil** — energized when rung logic is TRUE.
- `[ ]` — **Latching/Retentive coil**

### Conceptual Analogy (Ladder = Electrical Relay Schematic)
Imagine the original **relay-based control panels** of the 1960s, where motors were controlled by a maze of physical relays, timers, and contactors. Electricians had to read the schematic *left-to-right, top-to-bottom*, like reading a page. PLC manufacturers kept this **visual mental model** so that electricians could adapt to PLCs without retraining. That is exactly what a ladder diagram is — a software reproduction of a relay schematic.

---

### 1.4.2 Function Block Diagram (FBD)
**FBD** is a graphical language where the program is built by connecting **function blocks** (AND, OR, ADD, TIMER, PID, etc.) with signal lines. It is highly suited for **continuous process control** and **signal processing**.

**Key Concept:** Each function block has **inputs on the left** and **outputs on the right**, and data flows strictly left → right.

### Conceptual Analogy (FBD = Flowchart with Math)
Think of an **analog signal flow diagram** in a textbook (e.g., control systems or DSP). Each block transforms the signal: a summing junction adds, an integrator integrates, a comparator compares. FBD is the PLC equivalent — boxes transform signals, and the lines are the wires that carry them.

---

### 1.4.3 Sequential Function Chart (SFC)
**SFC** is a **state-transition-based** graphical language ideal for **sequential and batch processes** (e.g., a bottling plant, an elevator, a traffic light). It decomposes a process into:
- **Steps** (states/operations) — represented as rectangles.
- **Transitions** (conditions) — represented as horizontal lines with a boolean condition.
- **Actions** (outputs) — actions associated with each step.
- **Directed Links** — arrows connecting steps and transitions.

### Conceptual Analogy (SFC = A Flowchart for a Recipe)
Imagine you are writing a **cooking recipe**: *"If the water is boiling AND the pasta is added, wait 10 minutes. Then, if 10 minutes have passed, drain the water. Then, if the sauce is ready, mix them."* Each action is a **step**, each "if" is a **transition**. SFC is the formal, graphical version of this recipe — perfect for processes that move through distinct stages.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 PLC ↔ PC Communication — Architecture

### 2.1.1 Communication Modes
1. **Point-to-Point (RS-232):** Only one device on the link. Used for **direct programming**.
2. **Multipoint / Multi-drop (RS-485):** Up to **32 drivers and 32 receivers** on a single bus. Used for **PLC networks (DH-485, Modbus, Profibus)**.
3. **Hierarchical Networks:** PC (Supervisor) → Master PLC → Slave PLCs → Field devices. This is the foundation of **SCADA** and **DCS** (covered later in the course).

### 2.1.2 Handshaking & Data Framing
Communication uses **asynchronous serial transmission**. A standard frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Frame} &= \underbrace{1\ \text{Start bit}}_{\text{always 0}} + \underbrace{D\ \text{Data bits}}_{7\ \text{or}\ 8} + \underbrace{P\ \text{Parity bit}}_{\text{optional}} + \underbrace{S\ \text{Stop bits}}_{1\ \text{or}\ 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Total bits per character** $= 1 + D + P + S$. Example: 1 start + 8 data + 0 parity + 1 stop = **10 bits/character**.

### 2.1.3 Protocol Stack Layers (Simplified OSI for PLC)

| OSI Layer | PLC Equivalent | Example |
|-----------|----------------|---------|
| 7 — Application | Function codes (Read Holding Registers) | Modbus FC 03 |
| 2 — Data Link | Master/Slave polling | Modbus RTU |
| 1 — Physical | Voltage levels, connectors | RS-485, Ethernet |

### 2.1.4 Why Handshaking?
Handshaking ensures the **receiver is ready** before the sender transmits. Two main methods:
- **Hardware handshaking (RTS/CTS):** Uses dedicated control lines.
- **Software handshaking (XON/XOFF):** Uses special control characters (ASCII 17 / 19).

---

## 2.2 PLC Wiring — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Input Wiring Configurations

**A. Sink (NPN) Input Module — Current flows OUT of input terminal**
- Requires **PNP (sourcing) field devices**.
- Signal return path: PLC input → device → 0 V (common).
- Typical in **Asian PLCs**.

**B. Source (PNP) Input Module — Current flows INTO input terminal**
- Requires **NPN (sinking) field devices**.
- Signal return path: 24 V (common) → device → PLC input.
- Typical in **European PLCs (Siemens S7)**.

**C. Universal / Bidirectional Input Module**
- Auto-detects sink/source; modern PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7-1200) have this.

### 2.2.2 Output Wiring Configurations
1. **Relay Output (R):** Dry contacts, **2 A @ 250 V AC / 30 V DC**. Handles both AC and DC, but slower (10 ms).
2. **Transistor Output (T):** DC only, **0.5 A @ 24 V DC**. Fast (0.1 ms), no surge. NPN or PNP.
3. **Triac Output (S):** AC only, **0.5 A @ 250 V AC**. Used for dimmer/solenoid control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU exams:**
> - Inductive loads (solenoids, contactors, motors) → always use a **flyback diode (DC)** or **MOV/snubber (AC)** in parallel with the coil.
> - Relay outputs are the most flexible but have a finite mechanical life (**~100,000 operations**).

### 2.2.3 Signal Conditioning
To protect against industrial noise, PLC wiring uses:
- **Shielded twisted pair (STP)** cables for analog signals.
- **Separation of power and signal cables** (≥ 200 mm in cable trays).
- **Galvanic isolation** between field side and logic side.
- **Surge protection** for outdoor/field wiring.

### 2.2.4 Cable Color Code (IEC 60446 / NFPA 79)
- **Black** — AC line (L)
- **Red** — AC line 2 (L2 / L3 in 3-phase)
- **Blue** — Neutral (N)
- **Green/Yellow** — Protective Earth (PE)
- **Orange** — 24 V DC control wiring
- **Light Blue** — 0 V DC / Signal return

---

## 2.3 Installation of PLC Modules — Procedure

### 2.3.1 Hardware Inventory Checklist
- [ ] PLC chassis/rack (4, 7, 12, or 16 slots)
- [ ] Power supply module (verify voltage: **120/230 V AC** or **24 V DC**)
- [ ] CPU module (with memory card/battery backup)
- [ ] Discrete I/O modules (DI, DO, DI/DO mix)
- [ ] Analog I/O modules (AI, AO, RTD, Thermocouple)
- [ ] Communication modules (Ethernet, Profibus, RS-485)
- [ ] Special function modules (High-speed counter, positioning)
- [ ] Terminal blocks, DIN rail, end brackets
- [ ] Spare fuses and relays

### 2.3.2 Mechanical Installation
- **DIN rail (35 mm × 7.5 mm)** per EN 50022.
- Mount in a **ventilated enclosure** (IP54 or higher for dust/moisture).
- Maintain clearance: **≥ 50 mm** above/below for airflow.
- Ambient temperature: **0 to 55 °C** (operation), **−25 to +70 °C** (storage).

### 2.3.3 Electrical Installation
1. Verify **lockout/tagout (LOTO)** is in place.
2. Connect **PE first**, then **N/L** to the power supply.
3. Wire inputs to the input modules (use **bootlace ferrules**).
4. Wire outputs from the output modules to the field loads.
5. Connect communication cables to the comm ports.
6. Power up the PLC and check LEDs (Power, Run, Error, I/O status).

### 2.3.4 Software Configuration (After Hardware Install)
1. Launch the programming software.
2. Create a **new project** and select the correct **PLC model and firmware version**.
3. **Add modules** in the project tree to match the physical rack.
4. Assign **I/O addresses** (e.g., I:0.0/0, O:0.0/0).
5. **Download the empty project** to the PLC.
6. Verify communication by going **Online** with the PLC.

---

## 2.4 PLC Programming Languages — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 Ladder Logic (LD) — Building Blocks

**A. Contacts**
- **Normally Open (NO)** `| |` — `Contact is TRUE ↔ referenced bit = 1`
- **Normally Closed (NC)** `|/|` — `Contact is TRUE ↔ referenced bit = 0`
- **Positive Edge (P)** `|P|` — `TRUE for ONE scan on rising edge`
- **Negative Edge (N)** `|N|` — `TRUE for ONE scan on falling edge`

**B. Coils**
- **Standard Coil** `( )` — energize output when rung is TRUE.
- **Negated Coil** `(/)` — de-energize output when rung is TRUE.
- **Set (Latch) Coil** `(S)` — sets bit to 1 and **holds** it.
- **Reset Coil** `(R)` — resets bit to 0 and **holds** it.
- **Retentive Coil** `(M)` — maintains state across power cycles.

**C. Boolean Logic — The Foundation**
The rung evaluates **left-to-right, top-to-bottom**, like reading a sentence. Series = AND, Parallel = OR.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Output} &= (\text{Input}_1 \land \overline{\text{Input}_2}) \lor (\text{Input}_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Scan Cycle:** The CPU executes the program **top-to-bottom, left-to-right**, then updates all outputs. This **cyclic scan** is what makes PLC deterministic. A typical scan time is **5–20 ms** for small programs.

### 2.4.2 Function Block Diagram (FBD) — Building Blocks

**A. Standard Function Blocks (per IEC 61131-3)**
- **Bitwise:** AND, OR, NOT, XOR, SR (Set-Reset), RS (Reset-Set)
- **Timers:** TP (Pulse), TON (On-Delay), TOF (Off-Delay)
- **Counters:** CTU (Count Up), CTD (Count Down), CTUD (Count Up/Down)
- **Math:** ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, ABS, SQRT
- **Comparison:** GT, GE, EQ, LE, LT, NE
- **Selection:** SEL (Selector), MUX (Multiplexer)
- **Control:** PID, LIMIT, INTEGRAL, DERIVATIVE

**B. Block Anatomy**

$$
\boxed{\text{BLOCK NAME}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Input}_1, \text{Input}_2, \ldots, \text{Input}_n \rightarrow \text{Output}
$$

Each block has:
- **Instance name** (e.g., `Timer_1`)
- **Inputs** (left side)
- **Outputs** (right side)
- **Instance DB** (memory storage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FBD vs. LD:** FBD is **data-flow oriented** (signal flows left-to-right, can be reused multiple times), while LD is **execution-flow oriented** (rungs evaluated sequentially). FBD is preferred for **analog loops, math-heavy processes, and reusability** of function blocks.

### 2.4.3 Sequential Function Chart (SFC) — Building Blocks

**A. Elements**
- **Initial Step** — A double-bordered rectangle, marks the entry point. Every SFC must have at least one.
- **Step** — A rectangle representing a stable state of the process. Has a unique number.
- **Transition** — A short horizontal bar with a boolean condition. The "gate" between steps.
- **Action** — Text inside or attached to a step, describing what to do when active.
- **Directed Link** — Arrow (downward by default) connecting step → transition → step.
- **Selection Divergence** — "OR" branching: a single transition → multiple alternative paths.
- **Selection Convergence** — "OR" merging: multiple paths → single transition.
- **Simultaneous Divergence** — "AND" branching: one transition → multiple parallel branches.
- **Simultaneous Convergence** — "AND" merging: multiple parallel branches → single transition.
- **Jump** — Loop back to a previous step (e.g., for batch repeats).

**B. SFC Execution Rules**
1. **Initial step** is active at startup.
2. A step's **actions execute** while it is active.
3. A step's **successor transition is evaluated** continuously.
4. When the transition condition is TRUE → step becomes **inactive**, next step becomes **active**.
5. Only **one step in an OR branch** is active at a time; **all steps in an AND branch** can be active simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Action Qualifiers (IEC 61131-3):**
> - `N` — Non-stored (active while step is active)
> - `S` — Set (latched on, never reset by step deactivation)
> - `R` — Reset
> - `P` — Pulse (active for one scan on entry)
> - `L` — Time-limited (active for `T` time)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

### Table 2.1: Communication Protocol Quick Reference

| Protocol | Physical Layer | Topology | Max Nodes | Speed |
|----------|----------------|----------|-----------|-------|
| **RS-232** | Single-ended | Point-to-point | 2 | 115.2 kbps |
| **RS-422** | Differential | Multi-drop | 10 | 10 Mbps |
| **RS-485** | Differential | Multi-drop | 32 | 10 Mbps |
| **Modbus RTU** | RS-485 | Master-Slave | 247 | 115.2 kbps |
| **Profibus-DP** | RS-485 | Master-Slave | 126 | 12 Mbps |
| **EtherNet/IP** | Ethernet | Star/Ring | Many | 1 Gbps |
| **Profinet** | Ethernet | Line/Star | Many | 1 Gbps |

### Table 2.2: Wiring Best Practices

| Best Practice | Reason |
|---------------|--------|
| Use **bootlace ferrules** on stranded wires | Prevents stray strands, improves torque retention |
| Maintain **separation ≥ 200 mm** between power and signal cables | Reduces EMI coupling |
| **Twist signal pairs** at 1 twist per 30–50 mm | Cancels common-mode noise |
| Connect **PE at one point only** (star grounding) | Prevents ground loops |
| Use **shielded cables** for analog signals | Blocks capacitive pickup |
| Label every wire with **ferrules or tags** | Faster maintenance |

### Table 2.3: LD vs. FBD vs. SFC — When to Use

| Criterion | Ladder Logic (LD) | FBD | SFC |
|-----------|-------------------|-----|-----|
| **Best for** | Boolean logic, interlocks | Process control, math | Sequential/batch processes |
| **Learning curve** | Easy (for electricians) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Reusable blocks** | Limited | Excellent (FB instances) | N/A |
| **Debugging** | Easy (visual scan) | Moderate | Easy (state view) |
| **KTU exam weight** | Highest | Medium | Medium |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains **fully worked-out analytical problems** typical of KTU University Exam questions. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Boolean Logic Translation

### Problem Statement
A PLC rung has the following ladder structure:
- **Rung 1:** `Start_PB` (NO) `— Stop_PB` (NC) `— Motor_OL` (NC) — all in series — driving `Motor_Run` coil.
- **Rung 2:** `Motor_Run` (NO) in parallel with a **latch** branch around Rung 1, so once started, the motor keeps running until `Stop_PB` is pressed.

Write the boolean expression for `Motor_Run` and trace its behavior for a typical start-stop sequence.

### Step 1: Identify the Boolean Variables
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \text{Start\_PB (NO)} \\
T &= \text{Stop\_PB (NC)} \\
O &= \text{Motor\_OL (NC, overload contact)} \\
M &= \text{Motor\_Run (feedback NO contact)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Translate the Rungs to Boolean Algebra
- **Rung 1 (Start branch):** $X_1 = S \land T \land O$
- **Rung 2 (Seal-in branch):** $X_2 = M \land T \land O$ (NO contact $M$ in series with same Stop and OL)

### Step 3: Combine the Parallel Branches (OR logic)
$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= X_1 \lor X_2 \\
M &= (S \land T \land O) \lor (M \land T \land O)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Trace the Sequence (Truth Table Analysis)

| Step | $S$ | $T$ | $O$ | $M_{\text{prev}}$ | $M_{\text{new}}$ | Comments |
|------|-----|-----|-----|------------------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **0** | Motor OFF, initial state |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **1** | Start pressed → Motor starts |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **1** | Start released → Sealed by $M$ |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **0** | Stop pressed → Motor stops |
| 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **0** | Overload trips → Motor stops (fail-safe) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The overload contact (NC) is a **fail-safe** element — if the contact wire breaks, the motor stops. This is the **de-energize-to-trip** philosophy of industrial safety.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Designing a 3-Motor Sequential Starter in Ladder

### Problem Statement
Design a ladder program to start **three motors (M1, M2, M3) in sequence** with a **5-second time delay** between each. A single `START_PB` initiates the sequence; a single `STOP_PB` halts it. Each motor must remain ON once started (latch).

### Step 1: Identify Inputs and Outputs
- **Inputs:** `START_PB` (I:0.0/0), `STOP_PB` (I:0.0/1, NC)
- **Outputs:** `M1` (O:0.0/0), `M2` (O:0.0/1), `M3` (O:0.0/2)
- **Timer:** `T4:0` (5-second on-delay, preset 50 × 0.1 s = 5 s)

### Step 2: Ladder Rung-by-Rung Design

**Rung 1 — Start M1 with latching:**
```
|  START_PB  |  /STOP_PB  |  ( S )M1  |       ( L )M1 (latch confirmation) |
```
- Latch `M1` set when START pressed and STOP not pressed.

**Rung 2 — Start M2 after 5 s:**
```
|      M1     |  T4:0/DN  |  ( S )M2  |
```
- `T4:0/DN` is the "Done" bit of the timer; becomes TRUE when 5 s elapsed.

**Rung 3 — Timer logic:**
```
|      M1     |  /T4:0/DN |  TON  T4:0  |  Preset: 50  |  Acc: 0  |
```
- When M1 turns ON, the timer starts. After 5 s, `T4:0/DN` becomes TRUE.

**Rung 4 — Start M3 after another 5 s (need a second timer T4:1):**
```
|      M2     |  T4:1/DN  |  ( S )M3  |
|      M2     |  /T4:1/DN |  TON  T4:1  |  Preset: 50  |  Acc: 0  |
```

**Rung 5 — STOP halts everything:**
```
|  STOP_PB    |  ( R )M1  |
|             |  ( R )M2  |
|             |  ( R )M3  |
|             |  ( R )T4:0|
|             |  ( R )T4:1|
```

### Step 3: Timing Diagram

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_0 &= \text{START pressed} \\
t_1 &= t_0 + 0\ \text{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M1 \text{ ON} \\
t_2 &= t_0 + 5\ \text{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M2 \text{ ON} \\
t_3 &= t_0 + 10\ \text{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M3 \text{ ON} \\
t_{\text{stop}} &= \text{STOP pressed} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M1, M2, M3 \text{ OFF}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Mistake:** Students often place the timer on the **wrong side** of M1's seal-in contact. The timer should start **as soon as M1 energizes** (not after M1's done bit), otherwise the timing chain breaks.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — FBD Implementation of a PID Control Loop

### Problem Statement
Implement a temperature control loop in FBD. The setpoint (SP) is `100 °C`. The process variable (PV) comes from a thermocouple. The output should drive a heater through a 0–100 % control signal. Use a standard PID block with $K_p = 2.0$, $T_i = 10\ \text{s}$, $T_d = 0\ \text{s}$.

### Step 1: Identify the Blocks
1. **SP Input** — Constant or HMI tag
2. **PV Input** — Analog input from thermocouple module
3. **SUB block** — Computes Error = SP − PV
4. **PID block** — Outputs control signal 0–100 %
5. **LIMIT block** — Clamps output to 0–100 %
6. **AO Output** — Drives the heater via analog output module

### Step 2: FBD Structure (Textual Representation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error} &= \text{SP} - \text{PV} \quad (\text{SUB block}) \\
\text{Output} &= \text{PID}(\text{Error},\ K_p,\ T_i,\ T_d) \quad (\text{PID block}) \\
\text{Output}_{\text{clamped}} &= \text{LIMIT}(\text{Output},\ 0,\ 100) \quad (\text{LIMIT block}) \\
\text{Heater} &= \text{Output}_{\text{clamped}} \quad (\text{AO block})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: PID Equation (Standard Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(t) &= K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right] \\
u_n &= K_p \left[ e_n + \frac{T_s}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{T_d}{T_s} (e_n - e_{n-1}) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ — control output (0–100 %)
- $e(t)$ — error = SP − PV
- $K_p$ — proportional gain
- $T_i$ — integral time constant
- $T_d$ — derivative time constant
- $T_s$ — sampling time

### Step 4: Plug in Values (Steady-State Analysis)
With $T_d = 0$, the system becomes a **PI controller**. At steady state ($e \to 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{\text{ss}} &= K_p \cdot 0 + K_p \cdot \frac{1}{T_i} \int e\, dt \\
u_{\text{ss}} &= \frac{K_p}{T_i} \cdot E_{\text{integral}} = 50\% \quad (\text{assume saturation point})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — SFC for a Traffic Light Controller

### Problem Statement
Design an SFC program for a **2-way traffic light** with states:
1. **State S1:** North = Green (30 s), South = Red.
2. **State S2:** North = Yellow (5 s), South = Red.
3. **State S3:** North = Red, South = Green (30 s).
4. **State S4:** North = Red, South = Yellow (5 s).
Then loop back to S1.

### Step 1: List the Steps and Transitions

| Step | Action (N qualifier — active while step is active) | Transition Condition |
|------|--------------------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **S1** | N: `N_Green = 1, S_Red = 1` | T1: `T1_done` (after 30 s) |
| **S2** | N: `N_Yellow = 1, S_Red = 1` | T2: `T2_done` (after 5 s) |
| **S3** | N: `N_Red = 1, S_Green = 1` | T3: `T3_done` (after 30 s) |
| **S4** | N: `N_Red = 1, S_Yellow = 1` | T4: `T4_done` (after 5 s) |

### Step 2: SFC Cycle Time Calculation
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cycle}} &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 \\
T_{\text{cycle}} &= 30 + 5 + 30 + 5 \\
T_{\text{cycle}} &= 70\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: SFC Diagram Description (Mermaid)
The SFC is a simple **sequential loop** with no branches:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepS1[Step 1: N Green] -->|T1 timer 30s| stepS2[Step 2: N Yellow] -->|T2 timer 5s| stepS3[Step 3: S Green] -->|T3 timer 30s| stepS4[Step 4: S Yellow] -->|T4 timer 5s| stepS1
```

### Step 4: Boolean Equations for Outputs
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{Green}} &= \text{Step1\_Active} \\
N_{\text{Yellow}} &= \text{Step2\_Active} \\
N_{\text{Red}} &= \text{Step3\_Active} \lor \text{Step4\_Active} \\
S_{\text{Green}} &= \text{Step3\_Active} \\
S_{\text{Yellow}} &= \text{Step4\_Active} \\
S_{\text{Red}} &= \text{Step1\_Active} \lor \text{Step2\_Active}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Interlock (industry practice):** Always include a **red overlap** for 1 second in both directions (i.e., all red for 1 s before switching) to clear the intersection. This is a common KTU follow-up question.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Ladder Rung Translation Cheat Sheet

| Ladder Rung Element | Boolean Operator | Example |
|---------------------|------------------|---------|
| `| X |   | Y |` (series) | AND | $X \land Y$ |
| `| X |` over `| Y |` (parallel) | OR | $X \lor Y$ |
| `|/| X |` (NC contact) | NOT | $\overline{X}$ |
| `|P| X |` (positive edge) | $\uparrow X$ | $X_0 \land \overline{X_{-1}}$ |
| `|N| X |` (negative edge) | $\downarrow X$ | $\overline{X_0} \land X_{-1}$ |
| `(S) X` (Set coil) | $X \leftarrow 1$ (latch) | Persistent TRUE |
| `(R) X` (Reset coil) | $X \leftarrow 0$ (unlatch) | Persistent FALSE |

## 4.2 PLC Wiring — Sinking vs. Sourcing (Block Architecture)

flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Sinking Input Module NPN Style]

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram is an **abstract architecture flow**, illustrating the current-flow direction. The physical wiring must match the **module type** declared in the software configuration, or the input will never register a TRUE state.

## 4.3 PLC Installation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Step 0: Site Survey and LOTO])
    mount[Step 1: Mount DIN Rail and Chassis]
    psu[Step 2: Insert Power Supply Module]
    cpu[Step 3: Insert CPU Module]
    inputM[Step 4: Insert Input Module]
    outputM[Step 5: Insert Output Module]
    commM[Step 6: Insert Communication Module]
    wire1[Step 7: Wire Power Supply PE N L]
    wire2[Step 8: Wire Inputs to Input Module]
    wire3[Step 9: Wire Outputs to Output Module]
    wire4[Step 10: Wire Communication Cable]
    config[Step 11: Configure Hardware in Software]
    power[Step 12: Power Up and Check LEDs]
    test[Step 13: Run I/O Diagnostic Test]
    backup[Step 14: Save Project and Document]
    end1([System Ready for Programming])

    start --> mount
    mount --> psu
    psu --> cpu
    cpu --> inputM
    inputM --> outputM
    outputM --> commM
    commM --> wire1
    wire1 --> wire2
    wire2 --> wire3
    wire3 --> wire4
    wire4 --> config
    config --> power
    power --> test
    test --> backup
    backup --> end1
```

---

## 4.4 Ladder Logic Rung — Visual Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[L1 Power Rail] --> r1{Input 1 NO Contact}
    r1 --> r2{Input 2 NO Contact}
    r2 --> r3{Output Coil}
    r3 --> L2[L2 Power Rail]

    L1b[L1 Power Rail] --> r1b{Input 1 NO}
    r1b --> r2b{Input 2 NO}
    r2b --> coilb(Output Coil)
    coilb --> L2b[L2 Power Rail]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **parallel OR branch**, two rungs would be drawn simultaneously and merged into a single output coil. Mermaid represents this as parallel paths; in real ladder diagrams, it appears as **two horizontal rungs stacked vertically**, both feeding the same coil.

---

## 4.5 FBD Architecture — Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Input] --> SUBnode
    PV[Process Variable Input] --> SUBnode
    SUBnode[Subtraction Block SP minus PV] --> error
    error[Error Signal] --> PIDnode
    PIDnode[PID Block with Kp Ti Td] --> ctrl
    ctrl[Control Output 0 to 100] --> LIMnode
    LIMnode[Limit Block 0 to 100] --> finalout
    finalout[Final Control Signal to Heater] --> AO
    AO[Analog Output Module]
```

---

## 4.6 SFC Architecture — Two-Way Traffic Light

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    init[Initial Step: System Start]
    s1[Step 1: North Green, South Red, 30s]
    s2[Step 2: North Yellow, South Red, 5s]
    s3[Step 3: North Red, South Green, 30s]
    s4[Step 4: North Red, South Yellow, 5s]
    loop([Loop back to Step 1])

    init --> s1
    s1 -->|Transition T1 timer 30s done| s2
    s2 -->|Transition T2 timer 5s done| s3
    s3 -->|Transition T3 timer 30s done| s4
    s4 -->|Transition T4 timer 5s done| loop
    loop --> s1
```

---

## 4.7 PLC Program Scan Cycle Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A([Step 1: Read Inputs from Input Module])
    B([Step 2: Execute Ladder Logic Top to Bottom])
    C([Step 3: Compute Output Coils and Memory])
    D([Step 4: Write Outputs to Output Module])
    E([Step 5: Service Communication Requests])
    F([Step 6: Update Timers and Counters])
    G([Step 7: Return to Step 1])

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scan Time Formula:**
>
> $$
> T_{\text{scan}} = T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{program}} + T_{\text{output}} + T_{\text{housekeeping}}
> $$
>
> For a typical small-to-medium PLC, $T_{\text{scan}} \approx 5\text{–}20\ \text{ms}$.

---

## 4.8 PLC Network Architecture (Multi-Drop RS-485)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[PC with Programming Software] --> master
    master[Master PLC] --> slave1
    master --> slave2
    master --> slave3
    master --> terminatorA[120 ohm Terminator at end]
    slave1[Slave PLC 1]
    slave2[Slave PLC 2]
    slave3[Slave PLC 3]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
