# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 SFC Program Development – Step-by-Step Methodology (KTU Lab Method)

The IEC 61131-3 standard mandates a systematic development procedure for any SFC program. Below is the **algorithmic step-by-step method** that examiners look for:

**Step 1 — Identify Process States (Steps).**
List every distinct state the controlled system can occupy. Each state becomes a **Step**. Example: For a washing machine, states are `Idle`, `Fill`, `Wash`, `Drain`, `Spin`, `Done`.

**Step 2 — Identify Transition Conditions.**
For every change of state, identify the **boolean condition** that triggers it. Example: `WaterLevelReached`, `WashTimerDone`, `LidClosed`.

**Step 3 — Define Actions per Step.**
For each step, list what actuators / outputs are active. Use action qualifiers (`N`, `L`, `D`, `P`, etc.) where timing matters.

**Step 4 — Draw the SFC Diagram.**
Use a **flowchart with rectangular step boxes, transition bars, and directed links**. Initial step uses **double-line border**.

**Step 5 — Encode Steps with Boolean Flags.**
Assign a boolean variable per step. The PLC maintains these flags through its scan cycle. Activation rule:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step}_i(\text{Active}) &\iff \big( \text{Step}_{i-1} \text{ active} \big) \land \big( \text{Transition}_{i-1} \text{ TRUE} \big) \\
\text{Step}_i(\text{Inactive}) &\iff \big( \text{Step}_{i} \text{ active} \big) \land \big( \text{Transition}_{i} \text{ TRUE} \big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Implement Actions in the Action Block.**
Each step's action block executes continuously while the step is active.

**Step 7 — Test via Simulation.**
Use vendor software (e.g., **Codesys, TIA Portal, SoMachine**) to simulate the SFC and verify the sequence.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Example — Traffic Light Control using SFC

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

Design an SFC program for a **two-way traffic light** (North-South and East-West) with the sequence:
- **NS = Green, EW = Red** for 30 s
- **NS = Yellow, EW = Red** for 5 s
- **NS = Red, EW = Green** for 30 s
- **NS = Red, EW = Yellow** for 5 s
- Repeat forever.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify Steps (4 total):**

$$
S_1 = \text{NS\_Green}, \quad S_2 = \text{NS\_Yellow}, \quad S_3 = \text{EW\_Green}, \quad S_4 = \text{EW\_Yellow}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Transitions:**

$$
T_1 = \text{Timer}_{30s} \text{ done}, \quad T_2 = \text{Timer}_{5s} \text{ done}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Actions (L1 = 30 s, L2 = 5 s timed actions):**

$$
\text{Action}(S_1) = \text{NS\_Green ON, EW\_Red ON (L1 = 30 s)}
$$

$$
\text{Action}(S_2) = \text{NS\_Yellow ON, EW\_Red ON (L2 = 5 s)}
$$

$$
\text{Action}(S_3) = \text{EW\_Green ON, NS\_Red ON (L1 = 30 s)}
$$

$$
\text{Action}(S_4) = \text{EW\_Yellow ON, NS\_Red ON (L2 = 5 s)}
$$

**Step 4 — SFC Diagram (Logic Flow):**

$$
S_1 \xrightarrow{T_1} S_2 \xrightarrow{T_2} S_3 \xrightarrow{T_1} S_4 \xrightarrow{T_2} S_1
$$

**Step 5 — Boolean Flag Equations (Sample for $S_1$ activation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1(\text{NEXT}) &= S_4 \land T_2 \\
S_4(\text{NEXT}) &= S_3 \land T_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cycle}} &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 \\
&= 30\,\text{s} + 5\,\text{s} + 30\,\text{s} + 5\,\text{s} \\
&= \mathbf{70\,\text{seconds}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This exact pattern is the basis for **ladder-free PLC programming in cement kilns, water-treatment SCADA batching, and traffic SCADA systems** — and is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Example — FBD: PID-Based Temperature Control

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

Design an FBD program to maintain a **process temperature (PV)** at a **setpoint (SP) = 100 °C** using a **PID block**, where the controller output (MV) drives a heater via an analog output.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e(t) &= SP - PV \\
&= 100 - PV(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PID Algorithm (Standard / Ideal Form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MV(t) &= K_p \, e(t) \;+\; K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_d \,\frac{de(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Discretize for PLC Scan (Velocity Algorithm, KTU-Standard):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MV_n &= MV_{n-1} \;+\; K_p \big( e_n - e_{n-1} \big) \;+\; \frac{K_p \, T_s}{T_i}\, e_n \;+\; \frac{K_p \, T_d}{T_s} \big( e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2} \big)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Output Limits (Anti-Windup):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MV_n &= \begin{cases}
MV_{\max}, & \text{if } MV_n \geq MV_{\max} \\
MV_{\min}, & \text{if } MV_n \leq MV_{\min} \\
MV_n, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — FBD Block Diagram (Logic Flow):**

$$
\text{SP} \rightarrow \big( \text{SUB} \big) \rightarrow e \rightarrow \big( \text{PID} \big) \rightarrow MV \rightarrow \big( \text{Limit} \big) \rightarrow \text{Heater AO}
$$

**Step 6 — Sample Calculation (Numerically Verifiable):**
Assume $K_p = 2$, $K_i = 0.5$, $K_d = 0.1$, $T_s = 1\,\text{s}$, $e_0 = 10$, $e_1 = 8$, $e_2 = 5$, $MV_0 = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= K_p \,(e_n - e_{n-1}) = 2 \times (5 - 8) = -6 \\
I_n &= \frac{K_p \, T_s}{T_i} \times e_n = 2 \times 1 \times 5 = 10 \quad (\text{with } T_i = 1) \\
D_n &= \frac{K_p \, T_d}{T_s} \times (e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) = 2 \times 1 \times (5 - 16 + 10) = -2 \\
MV_2 &= MV_1 + P_n + I_n + D_n = 0 + (-6) + 10 + (-2) = \mathbf{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 RS-232 Frame Calculation – Worked Example

### 3.4.1 Problem
A PLC sends **1 character (8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit)** at **9600 baud**. Calculate the **transmission time per character** and the **effective data rate**.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Count Total Bits per Frame:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{bits}} &= 1\,\text{start} + 8\,\text{data} + 0\,\text{parity} + 1\,\text{stop} = \mathbf{10\ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Time per Bit (Bit Duration):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{bit}} &= \frac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}} = \frac{1}{9600} = 1.0417 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = \mathbf{104.17\,\mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Time per Character:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{char}} &= N_{\text{bits}} \times T_{\text{bit}} = 10 \times 104.17\,\mu\text{s} = \mathbf{1.0417\,ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective Data Rate (excluding overhead):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{8\,\text{data bits}}{T_{\text{char}}} = \frac{8}{1.0417 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{7680\,\text{bps}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Overhead Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead \%} &= \frac{10 - 8}{10} \times 100 = \mathbf{20\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Frequently Asked:** This is a classic KTU numerical problem. Memorize the formula: **$T_{\text{frame}} = (\text{Start} + \text{Data} + \text{Parity} + \text{Stop}) / \text{Baud}$**.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix – IEC 61131-3 Languages

| Parameter | LD | FBD | SFC | ST | IL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Type | Graphical | Graphical | Graphical | Textual | Textual |
| Basis | Relay logic | Circuit diagram | State machine | Pascal | Assembly |
| Best For | Discrete logic | Continuous control | Sequential processes | Complex math | Low-level (legacy) |
| Learn Curve | Easy | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Hard |
| Readability | High | High | Very High | High | Low |
| Vendor Support | Universal | High | High | High | Deprecated |
| 2024 KTU Module 3 Focus | Yes | **Yes (Primary)** | **Yes (Primary)** | Yes (overview) | No |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SFC Program Architecture (Two-Parallel-Branch Conveyor System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initStep[Initial Step - System Idle]
    s1[Step 1 - Wait For Start Pushbutton]
    s2[Step 2 - Run Conveyor A Motor]
    s3[Step 3 - Run Conveyor B Motor]
    s4[Step 4 - Both Conveyors Stop For Box Eject]
    s5[Step 5 - Reset And Return To Idle]
    t1{Transition T1: StartPB = TRUE}
    t2{Transition T2: Sensor1 = TRUE}
    t3{Transition T3: Sensor2 = TRUE}
    t4{Transition T4: Timer3s Done}
    t5{Transition T5: ResetPB = TRUE}
    a1[Action N: Conveyor_A ON]
    a2[Action N: Conveyor_B ON]
    a3[Action P0: Eject Solenoid Pulse]
    a4[Action N: All Motors OFF]
    initStep --> t1
    t1 --> s1
    s1 --> t2
    t2 --> s2
    s2 --> a1
    s2 --> t3
    t3 --> s3
    s3 --> a2
    s3 --> t4
    t4 --> s4
    s4 --> a3
    s4 --> t5
    t5 --> s5
    s5 --> a4
    s5 --> initStep
    classDef stepStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef transStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef initStyle fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:3px
    class initStep initStyle
    class s1,s2,s3,s4,s5 stepStyle
    class t1,t2,t3,t4,t5 transStyle
```

**Interpretation of Diagram:**
- The system is **idle** at `Initial Step`.
- On pressing `StartPB` (T1), the system moves to `Step 1` (wait state).
- `Step 2` runs Conveyor A; when **Sensor 1** detects a box (T2), `Step 3` runs Conveyor B in parallel.
- After both boxes are detected, the timer (3 s) expires (T4), and `Step 4` ejects a defective box via a pneumatic pulse.
- A `ResetPB` (T5) returns the system to the initial step.

## 4.2 Function Block Diagram (FBD) – Motor Starter with Overload

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpStart[Input: Start_PB BOOL]
    inpStop[Input: Stop_PB BOOL]
    inv1[NOT Gate - Inversion Bubble]
    or1[OR Function Block - 2 Inputs]
    and1[AND Function Block - 2 Inputs]
    rsBlock[RS Bistable - Reset Dominant]
    timerBlock[TON Timer - 5 Second On-Delay]
    overloadInput[Input: Overload_Trip BOOL]
    outputCoil[Output: Motor_Contactor BOOL]
    feedbackLine[Feedback Line To OR]
    inpStop --> inv1
    inv1 --> or1
    inpStart --> or1
    or1 --> and1
    overloadInput --> and1
    and1 --> rsBlock
    rsBlock --> timerBlock
    timerBlock --> outputCoil
    outputCoil --> feedbackLine
    feedbackLine --> or1
    classDef inputStyle fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    classDef logicStyle fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00
    classDef outputStyle fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    class inpStart,inpStop,overloadInput inputStyle
    class inv1,or1,and1,rsBlock,timerBlock logicStyle
    class outputCoil,feedbackLine outputStyle
```

**Interpretation:**
- `Start_PB` OR `(feedback from output)` → `OR` block (latch logic).
- `Stop_PB` (inverted) joins the OR output → both signals must be TRUE to allow latching.
- The latched signal passes through an `AND` block that is **gated by the Overload_Trip signal** (must be FALSE to allow start).
- Output is then delayed by 5 s (inrush delay) before energizing the `Motor_Contactor`.
- The output feeds back into the OR, creating a **seal-in (latching) circuit** — exactly like a hard-wired motor starter.

## 4.3 RS-232 PLC-to-PC Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pcNode[Personal Computer - DTE]
    plcNode[Programmable Logic Controller - DCE]
    serialCable[RS-232 Serial Cable DB-9]
    txLine[Signal Line TXD Pin 3]
    rxLine[Signal Line RXD Pin 2]
    gndLine[Signal Line GND Pin 5]
    softwareVessel[Engineering Software Step 7 MicroWIN]
    memoryArea[PLC Program Memory Flash]
    dataExchange[Serial Data Frames 9600 8 N 1]
    pcNode --> softwareVessel
    softwareVessel --> serialCable
    serialCable --> txLine
    txLine --> plcNode
    plcNode --> memoryArea
    memoryArea --> rxLine
    rxLine --> pcNode
    plcNode --> dataExchange
    dataExchange --> pcNode
    serialCable --- gndLine
    classDef pcStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef plcStyle fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cableStyle fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px
    class pcNode,softwareVessel pcStyle
    class plcNode,memoryArea plcStyle
    class serialCable,txLine,rxLine,gndLine,dataExchange cableStyle
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **PC (DTE)** runs vendor software (e.g., STEP 7-Micro/WIN) and uses its **COM port** to send program data on **TXD (Pin 3)**.
- The **PLC (DCE)** receives data on its **RXD pin (Pin 2)** and stores it in **Flash memory**.
- **GND (Pin 5)** is the common signal reference between PC and PLC.
- Communication uses asynchronous frames at **9600 8-N-1** configuration.
- This is a **point-to-point, full-duplex** connection — the PC can simultaneously upload (program download) and download (status monitoring).

## 4.4 SFC Parallel Branching using Synchronization Bars

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mainStep[Main Step S0 - Initial]
    syncBar1[Synchronization Bar Split Point]
    syncBar2[Synchronization Bar Join Point]
    pStep1[Parallel Step P1 - Motor A Start]
    pStep2[Parallel Step P2 - Motor B Start]
    pStep3[Parallel Step P3 - Motor C Start]
    pTrans1{Transition T1: Enable All}
    pTrans2{Transition T2: All Sensors TRUE}
    postStep[Post-Sync Step S9 - System Ready]
    mainStep --> pTrans1
    pTrans1 --> syncBar1
    syncBar1 --> pStep1
    syncBar1 --> pStep2
    syncBar1 --> pStep3
    pStep1 --> pTrans2
    pStep2 --> pTrans2
    pStep3 --> pTrans2
    pTrans2 --> syncBar2
    syncBar2 --> postStep
    classDef syncStyle fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:3px
    classDef stepStyle fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    class syncBar1,syncBar2 syncStyle
    class mainStep,postStep,pStep1,pStep2,pStep3 stepStyle
```

**Interpretation:**
- The thick red **synchronization bar** at the top **splits** the flow into three parallel paths (P1, P2, P3).
- All three parallel steps execute **simultaneously**.
- The second synchronization bar at the bottom **waits** until **all** parallel steps are complete and the transition condition is TRUE, then proceeds to the next step.
- This is the **AND (parallel) branching** mechanism of SFC — distinct from OR (selection) branching.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Conceptual)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part A questions test **Remember (L1) and Understand (L2)** of Bloom's Taxonomy. Keep answers precise, definition-based, and within 30–40 words.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*List the five programming languages defined by IEC 61131-3 and classify them as graphical or textual.*

**Model Answer:**
The five IEC 61131-3 programming languages are:
1. **Ladder Diagram (LD)** – Graphical
2. **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** – Graphical
3. **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** – Graphical
4. **Structured Text (ST)** – Textual
5. **Instruction List (IL)** – Textual *(deprecated in 2013 revision)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
*What is the difference between an SFC Step and an SFC Transition? Can a transition be associated with an action? Justify.*

**Model Answer:**
A **Step** in SFC represents a stable **state** of the process in which specific actions are performed. A **Transition** represents a **boolean condition** that, when TRUE, causes the process to move from the preceding step to the succeeding step. **Actions are attached to Steps, not Transitions.** However, a transition may be guarded by a condition that itself is implemented using LD/FBD/ST logic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern (ESE / Module Test):** Part B carries **14 marks** with **choice** between two questions. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Solutions must be structured, equation-supported, and show derivations.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]**

#### (a) **With a neat diagram, explain the elements of a Sequential Function Chart (SFC) as per IEC 61131-3. List the various action qualifiers with their meaning. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** SFC is a graphical language defined in IEC 61131-3 for describing the sequential behavior of a PLC-controlled process. It decomposes a control program into **Steps, Transitions, and Actions** connected by directed links.

**Elements of SFC:**

1. **Steps (S):** Rectangular boxes representing the stable states of the process. Each step has a **flag variable** indicating its active/inactive status. The **initial step** is drawn with **double-line borders**.

2. **Transitions (T):** Small horizontal bars placed on the directed link between steps. Each contains a **boolean condition**. A transition is **cleared (TRUE)** when the condition is satisfied.

3. **Directed Links:** Arrows connecting steps and transitions, defining the flow direction of the process.

4. **Actions (A):** Statements (in LD/FBD/ST) executed while the step is active. Each action has an **action qualifier** defining its timing.

5. **Synchronization Bars (Parallel Branching):** Thick horizontal lines representing **divergence (split)** or **convergence (join)** of parallel sequences.

**Action Qualifiers Table:**

| Qualifier | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| (none) / N | Non-stored, active while step is active |
| L | Time-limited |
| D | Time-delayed |
| P / P0 | Pulse on activation / Pulse on deactivation |
| S / R | Set (latched) / Reset |
| SD / DS / SL | Combined stored + timed variations |

**SFC Block Diagram (Neat Sketch):**

```
   ║ Initial Step ║ ──T1──>  ┌──────┐ ──T2──>  ┌──────┐
   ║    (Idle)    ║          │ Step2│          │ Step3│
                            └──────┘          └──────┘
                            Action:           Action:
                            Motor ON (N)      Valve Open (L=5s)
```

**Enabling Rule:** A step is enabled when all preceding transitions are TRUE and all preceding steps are active.

---

#### (b) **Design an SFC for a bottle-filling plant with the following sequence: (i) Bottle detected on conveyor → (ii) Solenoid valve opens for 3 s to fill → (iii) Capping cylinder extends for 2 s → (iv) Cylinder retracts for 1 s → (v) Conveyor moves to next station. Use proper action qualifiers. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step Identification (5 Steps):**

$$
S_1 = \text{Wait}, \quad S_2 = \text{Fill}, \quad S_3 = \text{Cap\_Extend}, \quad S_4 = \text{Cap\_Retract}, \quad S_5 = \text{Conveyor\_Move}
$$

**Transition Identification:**

$$
T_1 = \text{Bottle\_Sensor} = \text{TRUE}, \quad T_2 = \text{Fill\_Timer3s}
$$

$$
T_3 = \text{Cap\_Timer2s}, \quad T_4 = \text{Retract\_Timer1s}, \quad T_5 = \text{Conveyor\_Move\_Done}
$$

**Action Table:**

| Step | Action | Qualifier | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| S1 | Conveyor Running | N | Run continuously while waiting |
| S2 | Fill Solenoid Open | L (3 s) | Open for 3 s only |
| S3 | Cap Cylinder Extend | L (2 s) | Extend for 2 s |
| S4 | Cap Cylinder Retract | L (1 s) | Retract for 1 s |
| S5 | Conveyor Move | P0 | Pulse to advance |

**SFC Diagram:**

```
   ║ S1 (Wait) ║ ──T1──>  S2 (Fill L=3s) ──T2──> S3 (CapExt L=2s) ──T3──> S4 (CapRet L=1s) ──T4──> S5 (Conv Move) ──T5──> ↺ S1
```

**Total Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{cycle}} &= 3 + 2 + 1 + t_{\text{conveyor}} \\
&= 6 + t_{\text{conveyor}}\,\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### (a) **Explain the Function Block Diagram (FBD) language of IEC 61131-3 with a neat diagram. List any 8 standard function blocks with their functions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** FBD is a graphical programming language in IEC 61131-3 in which the program is built from **interconnected rectangular blocks**. Signals (variables) flow along lines connecting block inputs to block outputs, much like in an electronic circuit diagram.

**Characteristics:**
- Graphical, easy to visualize signal flow.
- Built from **functions** (stateless) and **function block instances** (stateful, with retained memory).
- Supports **signal inversion** (bubbles), **fan-out** (one output to many inputs), and **EN/ENO** enable logic.

**FBD Diagram – PID Speed Control Loop:**

```
 SP ───┐
        │ ─[SUB]─> e ─┐
 PV ───┘              │
                      ├──[PID]──> MV ──[LIMIT 0-100]──> Speed_Output
                      │                                 │
                      └──────────── Feedback ───────────┘
```

**Eight Standard Function Blocks (KTU High-Yield):**

| # | Block | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `SR` | Set-dominant bistable (flip-flop) |
| 2 | `RS` | Reset-dominant bistable |
| 3 | `R_TRIG` | Rising-edge detector |
| 4 | `F_TRIG` | Falling-edge detector |
| 5 | `TON` | On-delay timer |
| 6 | `TOF` | Off-delay timer |
| 7 | `CTU` | Up-counter |
| 8 | `CTUD` | Up-Down counter |

**Rules of FBD:**
1. Signals flow left-to-right or top-to-bottom.
2. Two outputs cannot be connected directly.
3. Feedback loops must include at least one delay/latch element.

---

#### (b) **With a neat block diagram, explain the RS-232 serial communication standard used for PLC networking. State the DB-9 pin functions of TXD, RXD, and GND, and derive the transmission time for 1 character (8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit) at 9600 baud. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** RS-232 is a **single-ended, point-to-point, full-duplex serial communication standard** introduced by EIA in 1962. It is the simplest method to connect a PC to a PLC for programming and monitoring.

**Key Specifications:**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Max Cable Length | 15 m |
| Max Devices | 2 (point-to-point) |
| Voltage (Logic 1) | −3 V to −25 V (MARK) |
| Voltage (Logic 0) | +3 V to +25 V (SPACE) |
| Connector | DB-9 (or DB-25) |

**DB-9 Critical Pins:**

| Pin | Name | Direction (PC → PLC) | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2 | **RXD** | Input to PC | Data received by PC from PLC |
| 3 | **TXD** | Output from PC | Data transmitted by PC to PLC |
| 5 | **GND** | Common | Signal ground reference |

**Block Diagram – PC ↔ PLC over RS-232:**

```
 ┌──────────┐                ┌──────────┐
 │   PC     │  TXD (Pin 3) ──>│   PLC    │
 │  (DTE)   │  RXD (Pin 2) <──│  (DCE)   │
 │ COM Port │  GND (Pin 5) ────│ Serial   │
 └──────────┘                └──────────┘
```

**Derivation of Transmission Time:**

Total bits per frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 1 \text{ (start)} + 8 \text{ (data)} + 0 \text{ (parity)} + 1 \text{ (stop)} = \mathbf{10\ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Time per bit at 9600 baud:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{bit}} &= \frac{1}{9600} = 1.0417 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = \mathbf{104.17\,\mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total time per character:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{char}} &= N \times T_{\text{bit}} = 10 \times 104.17\,\mu\text{s} = \mathbf{1.0417\,ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective data throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{8\,\text{data bits}}{1.0417\,\text{ms}} = \mathbf{7680\,bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overhead percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead \%} &= \frac{10 - 8}{10} \times 100 = \mathbf{20\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 3):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Initial Step" double-line border** → loses **1 mark** in SFC diagrams.
> 2. **Confusing Action Qualifier `S` (Set) with `L` (Time-limited)** → these are NOT the same; `S` latches forever, `L` auto-clears.
> 3. **Drawing SFC parallel branching without synchronization bars** → answers become invalid.
> 4. **Wrong RS-232 voltage levels** (writing +12 V for Logic 1) → RS-232 is **inverted**: Logic 1 is **−ve** voltage.
> 5. **Not showing unit conversions** in numerical problems (e.g., baud to time) → loses partial marks.
> 6. **Forgetting GND pin (Pin 5)** in DB-9 questions → mandatory for any RS-232 link.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam):**

- [x] **IEC 61131-3** defines **5 standard PLC languages**: LD, FBD, SFC (graphical) + ST, IL (textual).
- [x] **SFC** is derived from **Grafcet (IEC 848)**; ideal for **sequential / batch processes**.
- [x] SFC has **3 main elements**: **Steps** (states), **Transitions** (conditions), **Actions** (outputs).
- [x] **Initial Step** → drawn with **double-line borders**; only one per SFC network.
- [x] **Action Qualifiers to memorize**: `N` (none), `L` (time-limited), `D` (time-delayed), `P` (pulse), `S` (set/latch), `R` (reset), `SD / DS / SL` (combined).
- [x] SFC supports **OR (selection)** and **AND (parallel, with sync bars)** branching.
- [x] **FBD** is built from **function blocks** (stateful) and **functions** (stateless).
- [x] FBD rules: signals flow **L→R or T→B**; **no direct output-output connection**; feedback must use a **delay/latch**.
- [x] Standard FBD blocks: `TON`, `TOF`, `TP`, `CTU`, `CTD`, `CTUD`, `R_TRIG`, `F_TRIG`, `RS`, `SR`, `PID`.
- [x] **RS-232** is **single-ended, point-to-point, full-duplex, asynchronous serial**.
- [x] **RS-232 max length = 15 m**; max practical speed = **20 kbps**; max devices = **2**.
- [x] RS-232 Logic 1 = **−3 to −25 V** (MARK); Logic 0 = **+3 to +25 V** (SPACE) — **inverted from TTL**.
- [x] Use a **MAX232 / MAX3232 level converter** when interfacing RS-232 to a 5 V TTL microcontroller pin.
- [x] DB-9 essentials: **Pin 2 = RXD**, **Pin 3 = TXD**, **Pin 5 = GND**.
- [x] Serial frame formula: **$T_{\text{frame}} = (\text{Start} + \text{Data} + \text{Parity} + \text{Stop}) / \text{Baud}$**.
- [x] RS-232 vs RS-485: RS-485 supports **32 devices, 1200 m, differential signaling** — much better for industrial multi-drop networks.
- [x] Vendor programming software using RS-232: STEP 7-Micro/WIN (Siemens S7-200), RSLogix (Allen-Bradley), EcoStruxure (Schneider).
- [x] For KTU 14-mark questions: **always draw a neat SFC / FBD diagram + add a truth table or timing diagram wherever possible**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RS485
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT412 - PLC & DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** RS485

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**RS-485** (Recommended Standard 485), also known as **EIA-485** or **TIA/EIA-485**, is a standard defining the **electrical characteristics of drivers and receivers** for use in **balanced digital multipoint systems**. It was introduced by the **Electronic Industries Alliance (EIA)** in **1983** and remains the most widely used physical layer for industrial serial communication networks.

The standard specifies a **differential signaling** scheme operating on a **twisted-pair cable**, where information is transmitted as the **voltage difference** between two wires (labeled **A** and **B**, or **D+** and **D-**), rather than as an absolute voltage level with respect to ground.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Syllabus Highlight
> Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT412 Module 3), RS-485 is examined as the **backbone physical layer of PLC-to-PLC, PLC-to-VFD, and DCS fieldbus networks**. Students must understand its differential topology, multi-drop capability, termination requirements, and superiority over RS-232 in noise-prone industrial environments.

## 1.2 Key Specification Snapshot

| Parameter | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Signaling Mode | Differential (Balanced) | Excellent Common-Mode Noise Rejection (CMRR) |
| Number of Drivers | 1 (Single) | Transmitters on bus |
| Number of Receivers | Up to 32 (Standard) | One unit load each |
| Maximum Data Rate | **10 Mbps** (at 12 m) | Speed inversely proportional to distance |
| Maximum Cable Length | **1200 m** (4000 ft) at 100 kbps | Distance inversely proportional to speed |
| Common-Mode Voltage Range | **-7 V to +12 V** | Tolerates ground potential differences |
| Differential Output Voltage | **±1.5 V minimum**, ±5 V typical | Logic 1 vs Logic 0 distinction |
| Termination Resistance | **120 Ω** | Matches twisted-pair characteristic impedance |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Bridge"

Imagine two people standing at opposite ends of a long, noisy factory floor filled with welding sparks, motor hum, and fluorescent lights. If one person shouts using a **single wire connected to ground** (like **RS-232**), the listener cannot tell the message from the noise — both signals arrive jumbled together.

Now, give each person **two ropes** (the twisted pair) and tell them: *“When you speak, pull one rope forward and the other backward by the same amount. The listener will measure only the **difference** between the two ropes.”* Since both ropes pick up **identical noise** from the environment, the **difference remains clean** — the message is preserved.

> [!NOTE] The Intuition
> - **RS-232** = Single-ended signal referenced to ground → vulnerable to noise.
> - **RS-485** = Differential signal on two wires → noise affects both equally, so the *difference* (the actual data) survives.

This is the **fundamental reason** RS-485 dominates industrial automation, motor control (VFDs), and DCS field networks where **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** is severe.

## 1.4 Where RS-485 Fits in the OSI Model

RS-485 defines **only the Physical Layer (Layer 1)** of the OSI model. It does **not** specify a communication protocol. Higher-layer protocols are layered on top, including:

- **Modbus RTU** (most common in PLC/DCS)
- **Profibus DP**
- **BACnet MS/TP** (building automation)
- **DMX512** (lighting control)
- **NMEA 0183** (marine electronics)

> [!TIP] Exam Tip
> If asked *"Is RS-485 a protocol?"* — the correct answer is **NO**. It is a **physical layer standard** that requires an upper-layer protocol (like Modbus RTU) to be functional.

## 1.5 Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex Configurations

RS-485 supports two wiring topologies:

1. **2-Wire (Half-Duplex) RS-485**: A single twisted pair carries data in **both directions, but only one direction at a time**. Requires driver/receiver switching (direction control line).
2. **4-Wire (Full-Duplex) RS-485**: Two twisted pairs allow **simultaneous** transmission and reception, used mainly in point-to-point master-slave configurations with full-duplex modems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: Differential Encoding
Data bits are encoded as the **polarity of the voltage difference** between the two lines:

$$V_{diff} = V_A - V_B$$

- If $V_A > V_B$ by at least **+200 mV** → Logic **MARK (1)**
- If $V_B > V_A$ by at least **+200 mV** → Logic **SPACE (0)**

The receiver only cares about this difference, not about absolute voltages.

### Step 2: Common-Mode Rejection
Industrial environments introduce **common-mode noise** — noise voltages that appear equally on both signal lines relative to ground. A differential receiver rejects this:

$$V_{out} = A_{diff}(V_A - V_B) + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$$

Since the Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) is extremely high, the term $A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$ becomes negligible, leaving only the desired signal.

### Step 3: Multi-Drop Bus Arbitration
Unlike RS-232 (point-to-point), RS-485 allows **multiple drivers and receivers on the same bus**. A common arrangement is **1 driver + up to 32 receivers** per segment, expandable to 256 nodes using high-impedance "1/8 unit load" transceivers.

### Step 4: Bus Idle State (Fail-Safe Biasing)
When no driver is active, the bus is in a **floating state**. External **pull-up and pull-down resistors** (typically 680 Ω to 4.7 kΩ) are connected to create a defined idle voltage, ensuring receivers do not interpret noise as data.

> [!IMPORTANT] Why Biasing is Required
> Without fail-safe biasing, floating bus lines can generate random bits during idle periods — a major source of CRC errors in Modbus RTU networks.

### Step 5: Signal Termination
At each **physical end** of the bus, a **120 Ω termination resistor** is placed in parallel across the two data lines. This resistor matches the **characteristic impedance** of the twisted pair, preventing **signal reflections** that would otherwise corrupt data at high speeds or long cable runs.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Purpose / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential Voltage | $V_{diff} = V_A - V_B$ | V | Determine logic state at receiver |
| Receiver Threshold | $V_{th} = \pm 200$ | mV | Minimum detectable signal swing |
| Common-Mode Voltage | $V_{cm} = (V_A + V_B)/2$ | V | Must lie in [-7, +12] V |
| Maximum Cable Length vs Baud | $L_{max} = 10^8 / (Baud \times Rate_{factor})$ | m | Trade-off between distance and speed |
| Number of Nodes (Standard) | $N = 32$ Unit Loads | count | With 1/8 UL transceivers: $N = 256$ |
| Termination Resistance | $R_t = 120$ | Ω | Match twisted-pair $Z_0$ |
| Reflection Coefficient | $\Gamma = (R_t - Z_0)/(R_t + Z_0)$ | unitless | Should approach 0 for matched line |
| Unit Load | $UL = 12$ kΩ input impedance | Ω | Reference load for 32-node limit |

> [!NOTE] Avoid Markdown Table Breaking
> Note: In the table above, the values inside cells use clean numeric notation. In complex derivations below, absolute-value and "such that" symbols are rendered as $\vert$ and $\mid$ to prevent breaking table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

RS-485 is the **unquestionable workhorse** of industrial communication because it:

1. **Survives Industrial EMI**: Welding machines, VFDs, and motor starters create massive noise that destroys single-ended standards like RS-232.
2. **Spans Long Distances**: Up to **1.2 km** between the master PLC and the most remote slave, covering entire factory floors.
3. **Supports Fieldbus Topologies**: Forms the physical layer of **Modbus RTU**, the world's most deployed industrial protocol.
4. **Enables DCS Architecture**: Allows multiple PLCs, RTUs, and HMIs to share a single twisted pair, reducing cabling cost dramatically.
5. **Galvanic Isolation Friendly**: Easily paired with **isolated transceivers** (e.g., ADM2582E, MAX3485) to break ground loops between field devices.

> [!TIP] KTU Industrial Context
> In a typical **Distributed Control System (DCS)**, the Engineering Station, Operator Station, and field PLCs communicate over **RS-485 + Modbus RTU** for cost-sensitive, moderate-speed data exchange (≤ 1 Mbps), while **Ethernet/IP** or **PROFINET** is used for high-speed data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Maximum Cable Length and Baud Rate

**Problem Statement (KTU-Standard):** *Show that for an RS-485 network, the maximum supported cable length is inversely proportional to the baud rate, and compute the maximum cable length for a 1 Mbps data rate given the standard empirical constant.*

### Given:
- Standard empirical relationship (from TIA/EIA-485-A and Maxim app notes):

$$L_{max} \times f_{baud} = 10^8 \text{ (m·Hz, empirical constant)}$$

### Step 1: Establish the proportionality
Rearranging the empirical law:

$$L_{max} = \frac{10^8}{f_{baud}}$$

This shows that as baud rate increases, the maximum cable length decreases **inversely**.

### Step 2: Verify with the standard reference points

For **100 kbps** ($f_{baud} = 10^5$ Hz):

$$L_{max} = \frac{10^8}{10^5} = 1000 \text{ m} \approx 1200 \text{ m (standard quoted value)}$$

The slight discrepancy is absorbed by the cable's **propagation delay budget** and the **rise-time** of the transceiver. The 1200 m figure assumes use of **24 AWG twisted pair** with a propagation velocity of ~0.66c.

For **10 Mbps** ($f_{baud} = 10^7$ Hz):

$$L_{max} = \frac{10^8}{10^7} = 10 \text{ m}$$

This matches the TIA/EIA-485 standard's upper data-rate limit (10 Mbps at 12 m or less).

### Step 3: Calculate for 1 Mbps

$$L_{max} = \frac{10^8}{1 \times 10^6} = 100 \text{ m}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** For a 1 Mbps Modbus RTU network, the maximum trunk length is **100 m**, which is more than sufficient for almost any DCS cabinet or PLC panel within a single control room.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Number of Nodes Using Unit Load Analysis

**Problem Statement:** *A 2-wire RS-485 bus uses standard 1/8 unit-load transceivers. Determine the maximum number of nodes that can be connected, and explain why the standard 32-node limit is exceeded.*

### Background — Unit Load Definition
The **Unit Load (UL)** is defined as the DC input resistance presented by a single receiver to the bus. For the original RS-485 standard:

$$R_{in,UL} = 12 \text{ kΩ (minimum)}$$

The maximum DC load current that the bus driver must sink is:

$$I_{load,max} = \frac{V_{diff}}{12 \text{ kΩ}}$$

For $V_{diff} = 5$ V:

$$I_{load,max} = \frac{5}{12000} = 0.417 \text{ mA per UL}$$

### Step 1: Standard 32-node calculation
A standard RS-485 driver can sink/source at least **60 mA** of bus current while maintaining the minimum 1.5 V differential output.

Maximum number of standard 1-UL receivers:

$$N_{max} = \frac{I_{driver,max}}{I_{UL}} = \frac{60 \text{ mA}}{1.875 \text{ mA}} \approx 32$$

(The factor 1.875 mA accounts for the 1.5 V minimum differential plus 5 V common-mode loss, yielding the standard **32 UL limit**.)

### Step 2: 1/8 Unit Load Expansion
Modern transceivers (e.g., **MAX3080**, **SN65HVD75**) present an input impedance of **96 kΩ minimum** (8 × 12 kΩ). Each is rated as **1/8 UL**.

Number of nodes:

$$N_{1/8} = 32 \times 8 = 256 \text{ nodes}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Using 1/8 UL transceivers, the bus can support up to **256 devices** without repeaters — ideal for large DCS installations with hundreds of I/O points.

## 3.3 Derivation: Termination Resistor Power Dissipation

**Problem Statement:** *Calculate the steady-state power dissipated in the 120 Ω termination resistor when the bus is driven to its maximum differential output of 5 V.*

### Step 1: Apply Ohm's Law
The current through the termination resistor at the end of the bus:

$$I_t = \frac{V_{diff}}{R_t} = \frac{5}{120} = 0.0417 \text{ A} = 41.7 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Power

$$P_t = I_t^2 \cdot R_t = (0.0417)^2 \times 120 = 0.208 \text{ W}$$

Alternatively:

$$P_t = \frac{V_{diff}^2}{R_t} = \frac{5^2}{120} = 0.208 \text{ W}$$

### Step 3: Resistor Selection
A standard **1/4 W (0.25 W)** resistor provides a comfortable safety margin. Engineers often select **1/2 W** resistors for industrial environments with elevated ambient temperatures.

> [!TIP] Practical Consideration
> Termination is only required at the **two physical ends** of the bus. Adding termination in the middle creates reflections and should be avoided.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — RS-485 vs RS-422 vs RS-232

This is one of the **most frequently asked comparison questions** in KTU examinations.

| Feature | **RS-232** | **RS-422** | **RS-485** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signaling | Single-ended | Differential | Differential |
| Drivers per Bus | 1 | 1 | 32 |
| Receivers per Bus | 1 | 10 | 32 (up to 256 with 1/8 UL) |
| Mode | Full-duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex (2-wire) or Full-duplex (4-wire) |
| Max Cable Length | 15 m | 1200 m | **1200 m** |
| Max Data Rate | 20 kbps | 10 Mbps | **10 Mbps** |
| Voltage Levels | ±3 to ±25 V | ±2 to ±6 V (diff) | **±1.5 to ±5 V (diff)** |
| Topology | Point-to-point | Multi-drop (1 driver) | **True multi-point** |
| Common-Mode Range | ±25 V | ±7 V | **-7 to +12 V** |
| Typical Use | PC COM ports | Industrial legacy | **PLC / DCS / Modbus** |

## 3.5 Cable Selection and Characteristic Impedance Derivation

For a **twisted pair**, the characteristic impedance depends on the **ratio of conductor diameter (d)** to **conductor spacing (D)**:

$$Z_0 = \frac{120}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2D}{d}\right) \text{ Ω}$$

For typical PVC-insulated twisted pair, $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.3$:

$$Z_0 \approx \frac{120}{1.517} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2D}{d}\right) \approx 79.1 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2D}{d}\right) \text{ Ω}$$

To match the standard **120 Ω termination**, cables are manufactured with $D/d$ chosen such that $Z_0 \approx 120 \text{ Ω}$.

> [!WARNING] Common Student Error
> Using **50 Ω coaxial cable** for RS-485 is a common mistake. The 120 Ω termination requires a cable with $Z_0 \approx 120 \text{ Ω}$, which is the basis of **120 Ω twisted pair** specification (e.g., Belden 3106A, Lapp ETHERLINE).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RS-485 Multi-Drop Bus Topology (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeM["MASTER PLC\nCPU with RS485 port"]
    nodeS1["SLAVE 1\nVFD Drive"]
    nodeS2["SLAVE 2\nRemote I/O Block"]
    nodeS3["SLAVE 3\nTemperature Sensor"]
    nodeS4["SLAVE 4\nEnergy Meter"]
    nodeS5["SLAVE 5\nHMI Panel"]
    termA["TERMINATION 120 OHM\nLeft End"]
    termB["TERMINATION 120 OHM\nRight End"]
    biasR["FAIL SAFE BIAS\nPull up to 5V\nPull down to GND"]
    twist["TWISTED PAIR CABLE\nBelden 3106A"]

    nodeM --- termA
    termA --- twist
    twist --- nodeS1
    nodeS1 --- nodeS2
    nodeS2 --- nodeS3
    nodeS3 --- nodeS4
    nodeS4 --- nodeS5
    nodeS5 --- termB
    biasR --- twist
```

> [!NOTE] Diagram Interpretation
> This block diagram shows the **classic Modbus RTU over RS-485 topology** used in DCS and PLC networks:
> - **Master PLC** sits at one end with **120 Ω termination**
> - **Slaves** are daisy-chained (not star-wired) along the trunk
> - **120 Ω termination** at the far end absorbs reflections
> - **Fail-safe bias** network prevents floating-line errors

## 4.2 RS-485 2-Wire Half-Duplex Signal Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    direction LR
    subgraph TX_DRIVER["TRANSMITTER STAGE"]
        inputA["Logic Input\nDE High"]
        driverA["Differential\nDriver IC\nMAX485"]
        pwmA["Generate VA and VB"]
    end
    subgraph BUS_LINE["TWISTED PAIR BUS"]
        lineA["Line A\nNon Inverting"]
        lineB["Line B\nInverting"]
    end
    subgraph RX_RECEIVER["RECEIVER STAGE"]
        senseA["Differential\nComparator"]
        outputA["Logic Output\nRO pin"]
    end

    inputA --> driverA --> pwmA
    pwmA --> lineA
    pwmA --> lineB
    lineA --> senseA
    lineB --> senseA
    senseA --> outputA
```

## 4.3 RS-485 vs RS-232 Signal Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph RS232["RS 232 SINGLE ENDED"]
        tx1["TX Driver"]
        gnd1["Ground Reference"]
        sig1["Signal 1 to 5V\non TX line"]
        tx1 --> sig1
        sig1 --> gnd1
    end

    subgraph RS485["RS 485 DIFFERENTIAL"]
        tx2["Differential Driver"]
        va["Line A plus 2.5V"]
        vb["Line B minus 2.5V"]
        diff["Difference VA minus VB equals 5V"]
        tx2 --> va
        tx2 --> vb
        va --> diff
        vb --> diff
    end
```

> [!TIP] Visual Insight
> In **RS-232**, the signal is referenced to ground. In **RS-485**, the signal is the **difference** of two lines around a common-mode offset (typically 2.5 V), which is why both lines carry "live" voltages that swing in opposite directions.

## 4.4 Industrial DCS Network Using RS-485 Backbone

```mermaid
graph TB
    controlRoom["CONTROL ROOM\nOperator Station and Engineering Workstation"]
    masterPLC["MASTER PLC\nSiemens S7 1200 with CM 1241 module"]
    busA["RS 485 Trunk 1\nModbus RTU 115200 bps"]
    busB["RS 485 Trunk 2\nModbus RTU 9600 bps"]
    vfd1["VFD Drive 1\nMotor M1"]
    vfd2["VFD Drive 2\nMotor M2"]
    rio1["Remote I/O Block 1\n16 DI 16 DO"]
    rio2["Remote I/O Block 2\n8 AI 4 AO"]
    meter1["Energy Meter\nThree Phase"]
    hmi1["HMI Panel\n7 inch Touch"]

    controlRoom --- masterPLC
    masterPLC --- busA
    masterPLC --- busB
    busA --- vfd1
    busA --- vfd2
    busA --- rio1
    busB --- rio2
    busB --- meter1
    busB --- hmi1
```

> [!IMPORTANT] Real DCS Architecture
> This diagram represents a **realistic Distributed Control System** layout where a master PLC communicates over **two separate RS-485 trunks** with different baud rates — the high-speed trunk for VFDs and the low-speed trunk for metering and HMI.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*List any **three** advantages of RS-485 over RS-232 in industrial communication.*

**Model Answer (3 key points):**

1. **Differential signaling** provides superior noise immunity through Common-Mode Rejection, making RS-485 suitable for electrically noisy industrial environments (motors, VFDs, welding), whereas RS-232's single-ended signaling is highly susceptible to such noise.

2. **Multi-drop capability** allows up to **32 transmitters and 32 receivers** (or 256 with 1/8 UL transceivers) on a single bus, while RS-232 supports only point-to-point communication between two devices.

3. **Long-distance communication** up to **1200 m at 100 kbps**, compared to RS-232's maximum of **15 m**, enabling plant-wide and field-level network deployment from a single PLC master.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*What is the function of the 120 Ω resistor in an RS-485 network, and where should it be placed?*

**Model Answer:**

The **120 Ω resistor** is a **termination resistor** that matches the **characteristic impedance** of the twisted-pair cable (typically 120 Ω for Belden-type cables). Its functions are:

- **Absorbs high-frequency signal reflections** that occur at the ends of an unterminated transmission line, which would otherwise corrupt data bits.
- **Maintains signal integrity** by ensuring the differential waveform settles cleanly before the next bit period.

It must be placed **only at the two physical ends** of the RS-485 bus — never in the middle. Each end of the trunk requires exactly **one** 120 Ω resistor connected between the **A and B lines**. Placing it in the middle creates secondary reflection points and degrades signal quality.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Option 1]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the differential signaling scheme used in RS-485. With a neat diagram, describe how a logic '1' (MARK) and logic '0' (SPACE) are represented on the bus. Also explain why differential signaling is preferred over single-ended signaling in industrial environments.*

**(b)** [7 Marks] *A Modbus RTU network uses RS-485 as the physical layer. The network has a master PLC and **20 slave devices** distributed over a cable length of **800 m**. The data rate is **115200 bps**. Determine:*
- *(i) Whether the cable length is within the RS-485 specification.*
- *(ii) The recommended termination scheme.*
- *(iii) Whether the bus can be extended using a repeater if a 21st device is needed 400 m away.*

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Differential Signaling in RS-485 [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** RS-485 uses **differential (balanced) signaling**, where data is transmitted as the **voltage difference** between two wires (Line A and Line B) rather than as a voltage measured with respect to ground.

**Logic Representation (2 marks):**
- **MARK (Logic 1)**: $V_A - V_B \geq +200$ mV (typically +1.5 V to +5 V)
- **SPACE (Logic 0)**: $V_B - V_A \geq +200$ mV (typically -1.5 V to -5 V)
- **Idle**: Bus is biased to a defined MARK state via fail-safe pull-up/pull-down resistors.

**Waveform (1 mark):**

During transmission, Line A and Line B swing in **opposite directions** around a **2.5 V common-mode offset**. For example, for a 5 V differential swing:
- MARK: $V_A = +5$ V, $V_B = 0$ V → $V_{diff} = +5$ V
- SPACE: $V_A = 0$ V, $V_B = +5$ V → $V_{diff} = -5$ V

**Why Differential is Preferred in Industrial Environments (2 marks):**
Industrial environments generate **common-mode noise** (from VFDs, motors, welding equipment) that appears equally on both signal lines. Since the receiver measures only the **difference**, this noise cancels out, providing **Common-Mode Rejection Ratios (CMRR) of 60–80 dB**. Single-ended RS-232, in contrast, cannot distinguish noise from the actual signal, leading to data corruption in such environments.

#### Part (b) — Modbus RTU Network Analysis [7 Marks]

**(i) Cable length verification (3 marks):**
Using the empirical relationship:

$$L_{max} = \frac{10^8}{f_{baud}} = \frac{10^8}{115200} = 868 \text{ m}$$

Since 800 m < 868 m, **the cable length is within specification** at 115200 bps. The network will operate reliably.

**(ii) Termination scheme (2 marks):**
- Install **one 120 Ω resistor** at the **master PLC end** of the trunk.
- Install **another 120 Ω resistor** at the **last (20th) slave end** of the trunk.
- Do **NOT** place terminations at any of the 18 intermediate slave devices.
- Add **fail-safe bias resistors** (typically 680 Ω pull-up to 5 V on Line A and 680 Ω pull-down to GND on Line B) at the master end.

**(iii) Extension using a repeater (2 marks):**
Yes, the bus can be extended using a **RS-485 repeater** (also called a "bus extender" or "repeater hub"). Adding the 21st device 400 m away would push the total length to **1200 m**, which marginally exceeds the limit at 115200 bps. A repeater:
- **Regenerates** the signal at full voltage swing.
- **Isolates** the two bus segments electrically.
- **Doubles the effective cable length** to 2400 m, with up to 32 devices per segment.
- Industrial examples: **B&B Electronics BB-485OP**, **Phoenix Contact PSI-REP-RS485**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Option 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Compare RS-232, RS-422, and RS-485 standards with respect to signaling mode, number of drivers, number of receivers, maximum cable length, maximum data rate, and typical applications. Use a comparative table.*

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Explain the function and calculation of **Unit Load** in RS-485. A network uses **1/8 unit load** transceivers. Calculate the maximum number of devices that can be connected to a single RS-485 bus. Why is this expansion important for large DCS installations?*

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparative Study [7 Marks]

| Parameter | **RS-232** | **RS-422** | **RS-485** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Signaling Mode | Single-ended (unbalanced) | Differential (balanced) | Differential (balanced) |
| Drivers per Bus | 1 | 1 | Up to 32 (1/8 UL: 256) |
| Receivers per Bus | 1 | Up to 10 | Up to 32 (1/8 UL: 256) |
| Maximum Cable Length | 15 m (50 ft) | 1200 m at 100 kbps | **1200 m at 100 kbps** |
| Maximum Data Rate | 20 kbps | 10 Mbps | **10 Mbps** |
| Duplex Mode | Full-duplex | Full-duplex | Half/Full-duplex |
| Voltage Levels | ±3 to ±25 V | ±2 to ±6 V (diff) | **±1.5 to ±5 V (diff)** |
| Topology | Point-to-point | Multi-drop (1 driver) | **True multi-point** |
| Common-Mode Range | ±25 V | ±7 V | **-7 to +12 V** |
| Typical Use | PC COM port, lab instruments | Industrial legacy systems | **PLC, DCS, Modbus, VFDs** |

*(Full marks for complete table + 1-2 line explanation of why RS-485 is the most suitable for industrial use — 7 marks total distributed across table entries and concluding remark.)*

#### Part (b) — Unit Load Calculation [7 Marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** A **Unit Load (UL)** in RS-485 is the **DC load** presented by a single standard receiver to the bus, defined as a minimum input impedance of **12 kΩ**. The standard bus driver can reliably drive up to **32 such loads**, which forms the origin of the well-known "32-node limit" of RS-485.

**Calculation (3 marks):**
With **1/8 UL transceivers**, each device presents an input impedance of:

$$R_{in} = 12 \text{ kΩ} \times 8 = 96 \text{ kΩ}$$

Each device consumes only 1/8th of the current of a standard UL receiver. Therefore, the maximum number of devices is:

$$N_{max} = 32 \times 8 = 256 \text{ devices}$$

**Importance for DCS (2 marks):**
Large **Distributed Control Systems** often contain **hundreds of I/O points** spread across multiple cabinets, MCCs, and field junction boxes. The original 32-node limit would require many separate RS-485 trunks, increasing cabling cost, gateway complexity, and maintenance burden. By using **1/8 UL transceivers**, a single trunk can support up to **256 devices**, dramatically simplifying network architecture, reducing cost, and improving reliability in large-scale plants such as refineries, power stations, and water-treatment facilities.

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Confusing "Unit Load" with "Device"**: A standard UL receiver counts as 1 UL. 1/8 UL receivers count as 1/8 UL each, so 256 of them = 32 UL total. Students often incorrectly state "256 devices = 256 UL".
> 2. **Ignoring stub length**: RS-485 is a **daisy-chain bus**. Stubs from the main trunk to each device should be **< 1 meter at 1 Mbps** to prevent reflections. Students commonly sketch star topologies, which is incorrect.
> 3. **Forgetting fail-safe bias**: When all drivers are disabled, the bus floats. Without pull-up/pull-down bias, the receiver sees random noise and may output garbage data — a frequent source of "spurious CRC errors" complaints.
> 4. **Wrong cable impedance**: Using 50 Ω coax instead of 120 Ω twisted pair leads to severe reflections even with 120 Ω termination.
> 5. **Exceeding 32 UL total**: Mixing 1 UL and 1/8 UL devices is allowed, but the **sum must not exceed 32 UL**. For example, 16 standard UL + 128 of 1/8 UL = 16 + 16 = 32 UL (✓ valid).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RS-485 is a physical layer standard** (Layer 1 of OSI), not a protocol. It requires a higher-layer protocol such as **Modbus RTU** to be functional.
- **Differential signaling** (voltage difference between A and B) provides superior noise immunity in industrial environments.
- **Maximum 32 unit loads** per bus, expandable to **256 with 1/8 UL transceivers**.
- **Maximum cable length 1200 m at 100 kbps**, decreasing to ~10 m at 10 Mbps. The product $L \times f$ remains approximately $10^8$ m·Hz.
- **120 Ω termination resistors** must be installed at **both physical ends** of the bus, never in the middle.
- **Fail-safe biasing** (pull-up on A, pull-down on B) is mandatory to prevent floating-line errors during idle periods.
- **Topology is daisy-chain**, not star. Stubs must be kept **< 1 m** at high baud rates.
- **Common-mode voltage range** is -7 V to +12 V, allowing operation across ground-potential differences.
- **Twisted pair with 120 Ω characteristic impedance** is the correct cable (e.g., Belden 3106A, Lapp UNITRONIC).
- **Half-duplex (2-wire)** is the most common configuration; **full-duplex (4-wire)** is used for dedicated master-slave modems.
- RS-485 forms the **physical backbone of Modbus RTU**, the most widely deployed industrial protocol in DCS and PLC networks worldwide.
- **Repeaters** double the effective bus length (to 2400 m) and isolate segments electrically.
- **Galvanic isolation** (using isolated transceivers) is recommended for long runs to break ground loops between field cabinets.
- Always compute $L_{max} = 10^8 / f_{baud}$ to verify network feasibility **before installation**.
- For KTU exams, remember the comparison triad: **RS-232 (short, simple) vs RS-422 (1 driver, multi-drop) vs RS-485 (multi-point, true bus)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocols - Ethernet - Modbus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT412 - PLC & Distributed Control SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protocols - Ethernet - Modbus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Communication Protocols in DCS — The Linguistic Backbone of Automation

In any Distributed Control System (DCS), hundreds of field devices — sensors, actuators, controllers, valves, and HMIs — must "talk" to each other in a language both sides understand. That universal agreement on **how data is formatted, transmitted, addressed, and interpreted** is defined by a **Communication Protocol**. Without protocols, a pressure transmitter from Vendor A could not communicate with a PLC from Vendor B; industrial automation would devolve into a Babel of incompatible signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Communication Protocol** is a standardized set of rules, message formats, timing conventions, and error-handling procedures that govern the exchange of data between two or more devices in an industrial automation hierarchy. In a DCS, protocols operate across multiple layers of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model**, typically spanning the **Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, and Application layers**.

### 1.2 Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) — The Industrial Nervous System

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Ethernet** is a family of wired networking technologies defined by the **IEEE 802.3 standard** that specifies the physical layer (cabling, signaling, voltage levels) and the MAC (Media Access Control) sub-layer of the data link layer for local area networks (LANs). In modern DCS and Industry 4.0 architectures, industrial Ethernet has become the de-facto fieldbus and backbone communication medium.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Office Postal System":**
Imagine a large multinational company where every department (Production, Quality, Maintenance) needs to send letters to every other department daily.

- **Ethernet is the pneumatic tube / internal postal network** running through the walls of the building.
- Every desk (device) has a unique **mailbox number (MAC address)**.
- Every letter is sealed in a **standard envelope (Ethernet Frame)**.
- A central sorter (the **switch**) reads the destination address and routes the letter to the correct desk.
- The "postal code" is the **IP address**, while the "specific desk" within a department is identified by the **port number**.

In this analogy, the physical tubes (twisted-pair copper, fiber optic, or wireless RF) are the **Physical Layer**, the sorter logic (CSMA/CD or full-duplex switching) is the **MAC Sub-Layer**, and the addressing scheme (IPv4/IPv6) is the **Network Layer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industrial Variants of Ethernet Relevant to DCS:**
> - **Standard Ethernet (10/100/1000 Mbps)** — Office-grade, used in control room LANs.
> - **Industrial Ethernet (IE)** — Ruggedized, deterministic, real-time variants: **EtherNet/IP, PROFINET, EtherCAT, Modbus TCP, Sercos III**.
> - **PoE (Power over Ethernet, IEEE 802.3af/at)** — Delivers both data and **48 V DC** power over a single twisted pair, ideal for IP cameras and wireless APs in the field.

### 1.3 Modbus — The Lingua Franca of Industrial Communication

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Modbus** is an open, royalty-free, master-slave (client-server) application-layer communication protocol originally published by **Modicon (now Schneider Electric)** in **1979** for use with its PLCs. It defines a simple Protocol Data Unit (PDU) — function codes and data payloads — that is transported over various physical layers such as RS-232, RS-485, and TCP/IP.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Order System":**
Picture a busy restaurant kitchen:

- The **Waiter (Master / Client)** walks between tables taking orders.
- The **Chef at each station (Slave / Server)** prepares only the dishes on his menu.
- The order slip has two parts: a **dish number (Function Code)** and **modifiers (Data/Registers)**.
- Each station has a unique **Station ID (Slave Address)** between 1 and 247.
- The waiter can either *ask* for a dish status (**Read**) or *tell* the chef to start a new dish (**Write**).
- If the station does not recognize the dish number, it returns a **"Sorry, not on my menu" error (Exception Response)**.

This perfectly captures Modbus: a strictly **polled, request-response protocol** with no spontaneous device-initiated messages (a limitation modernized in **Modbus TCP with event-driven reporting**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modbus Protocol Family — Three Principal Flavors:**
> | Variant | Physical Layer | Frame Format | Typical Use |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Modbus RTU** | RS-232 / RS-485 | Binary, compact | Factory floor, legacy PLCs |
> | **Modbus ASCII** | RS-232 / RS-485 | Hex ASCII, human-readable | Slow lines, debugging |
> | **Modbus TCP / Modbus RTU over TCP** | Ethernet (TCP/IP) | MBAP header + PDU | Modern Ethernet DCS backbones |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The OSI Model Mapping of Ethernet and Modbus

To understand how Ethernet and Modbus cooperate, we map them onto the **7-layer OSI model**. This is a **favourite KTU question** — examiners often present a block diagram with blank layers and ask students to map protocols.

| OSI Layer | Function | Ethernet Role | Modbus Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **7. Application** | User-level data services | HTTP, FTP, SNMP | **Modbus Application Protocol (PDU)** |
| **6. Presentation** | Data formatting, encryption | TLS/SSL (optional) | Encodes 16-bit register values |
| **5. Session** | Dialog control | TCP sockets | Connection management |
| **4. Transport** | End-to-end reliability, ports | **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** | **TCP Port 502** (default) |
| **3. Network** | Logical addressing, routing | **IP (Internet Protocol v4/v6)** | Optional IP addressing |
| **2. Data Link** | Framing, MAC addressing | **Ethernet MAC (IEEE 802.3)** | RTU: RS-485 framing |
| **1. Physical** | Bits on the wire | Twisted pair, fiber, RJ45 | RS-485 differential signaling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Insight:** Classic Modbus (RTU/ASCII) is a **layer 1+2+7** protocol — it defines its own framing on RS-485 and its own application message. Modbus TCP, however, becomes a **layer 4+7** protocol riding on top of the standard TCP/IP + Ethernet stack.

### 2.2 Ethernet Frame Structure (IEEE 802.3)

The fundamental unit of data exchange on an Ethernet network is the **frame**. Understanding its anatomy is essential for any DCS network engineer.

A standard IEEE 802.3 frame (without 802.1Q VLAN tag) contains:

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Preamble | 7 | Synchronization bit pattern (10101010…) |
| SFD (Start Frame Delimiter) | 1 | Marks the start of the frame (10101011) |
| Destination MAC Address | 6 | Hardware address of the receiver |
| Source MAC Address | 6 | Hardware address of the sender |
| EtherType / Length | 2 | 0x0800 for IPv4, 0x86DD for IPv6, 0x88A4 for EtherCAT, 0x88CC for LLDP |
| Payload (Data) | 46 – 1500 | Higher-layer protocol data (e.g., Modbus TCP ADU) |
| FCS (Frame Check Sequence) | 4 | **CRC-32** error-detection code |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Ethernet Frame Size = 64 bytes** (header 14 + payload 46 + FCS 4).
> **Maximum Ethernet Frame Size = 1518 bytes** (without Jumbo Frames / VLAN tag).
> This fixed maximum is why **Modbus TCP limits a single PDU to 253 bytes** of payload (260 - 7 MBAP bytes).

### 2.3 Modbus Protocol Data Unit (PDU) Anatomy

The **Modbus PDU** is the heart of the protocol — it is **identical for all Modbus variants**.

$$\text{PDU} = \underbrace{FC}_{\text{Function Code (1 byte)}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Data}}{\text{Variable length (0–252 bytes)}}$$

The **ADU (Application Data Unit)** is the variant-specific wrapper that transports the PDU:

- **Modbus RTU ADU** = Slave Address (1 byte) + PDU + CRC-16 (2 bytes)
- **Modbus ASCII ADU** = `:` + Slave Address (2 ASCII) + PDU + LRC (2 ASCII) + CRLF
- **Modbus TCP ADU** = **MBAP Header (7 bytes)** + PDU

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the most-tested facts in KTU ESE.**

| Parameter / Formula | Expression | Value / Unit | Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus RTU frame silence gap** | $T_{3.5} = 3.5 \times t_{byte}$ | ≥ **3.5 character times** | Marks end of RTU frame |
| **Modbus RTU inter-character gap** | $T_{1.5} = 1.5 \times t_{byte}$ | ≤ **1.5 character times** | Maximum allowed within a frame |
| **Time per byte** | $t_{byte} = \dfrac{11 \text{ bits}}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | seconds | 11 bits = 1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop |
| **Modbus TCP default port** | $P_{MB}$ | **502 / TCP** | IANA-registered port |
| **MBAP Header length** | $L_{MBAP}$ | **7 bytes** | Transaction ID (2) + Protocol ID (2) + Length (2) + Unit ID (1) |
| **Max Modbus TCP PDU data** | $D_{max} = 260 - L_{MBAP} - 1_{FC}$ | **253 bytes** | ADU capped at 260 bytes by RFC |
| **Max slaves per RS-485 segment** | $N_{slaves}$ | **32** (or **247** logical) | Electrical loading of RS-485 drivers |
| **RS-485 max cable length** | $L_{cable} = \dfrac{10^7}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ (approx) | Up to **1200 m** at 9600 baud | Rule of thumb |
| **Ethernet max nodes (logical)** | $N_{nodes}$ | $2^{48} - 2$ | 48-bit MAC address space |
| **Max PDU length** | $L_{PDU}$ | **253 bytes** | One FC + 252 data bytes |
| **Cyclic Redundancy Check (RTU)** | $CRC_{16}$ | **16-bit polynomial** | Polynomial: $x^{16} + x^{15} + x^{2} + 1$ |
| **Longitudinal Redundancy (ASCII)** | $LRC$ | **8-bit two's complement** | Simpler than CRC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on escape sequences:** The pipes used here for absolute-style quantities (e.g. $\vert x \vert$) have been replaced with their LaTeX-safe `$\vert$` rendering to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. Use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` in your own answer scripts.

### 2.5 Function Codes — The Modbus Vocabulary

Function codes (FC) are the **verbs** of Modbus. Each code tells the slave **what operation to perform** and **on which data area**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Four" Function Codes — Memorize These:**
> | FC (Decimal) | FC (Hex) | Name | Data Type Accessed |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **01** | 0x01 | Read Coils | Discrete Outputs (1-bit, RW) |
> | **02** | 0x02 | Read Discrete Inputs | Discrete Inputs (1-bit, RO) |
> | **03** | 0x03 | Read Holding Registers | 16-bit RW registers |
> | **04** | 0x04 | Read Input Registers | 16-bit RO registers |
> | **05** | 0x05 | Write Single Coil | Set 1 bit |
> | **06** | 0x06 | Write Single Register | Set 16-bit value |
> | **15** | 0x0F | Write Multiple Coils | Set N bits |
> | **16** | 0x10 | Write Multiple Registers | Set N 16-bit registers |

The four data areas in a Modbus slave are referenced by their **address spaces**:

- **Coils (0xxxx)** — 1-bit, read/write, outputs.
- **Discrete Inputs (1xxxx)** — 1-bit, read-only, status flags.
- **Input Registers (3xxxx)** — 16-bit, read-only, measured values.
- **Holding Registers (4xxxx)** — 16-bit, read/write, setpoints and configuration.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Industries:** Refineries and petrochemical plants deploy **Modbus TCP over industrial Ethernet** as the backbone linking DCS controllers (e.g., Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV) to RTUs and third-party PLCs.
- **Power Systems:** **IEC 61850** and **DNP3** dominate substation automation, but Modbus remains ubiquitous in **renewable inverters, battery management systems (BMS), and SCADA gateways**.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** **OPC UA over TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking)** is the future, but **Modbus TCP remains the lingua franca** of brownfield integration — a Modbus sensor can speak to a cloud platform via an MQTT-Modbus gateway.
- **Building Management:** HVAC, lighting, and access control systems use Modbus RTU over RS-485 to this day because of its simplicity, robustness, and **1200 m cable reach**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Modbus RTU Inter-Frame Timing

**Problem Statement:** A Modbus RTU network operates at **19,200 baud** with **11 bits per character** (1 start + 8 data + 1 even parity + 1 stop). Compute the **inter-frame silence interval $T_{3.5}$** that marks the end of a valid RTU message, and determine the **maximum allowable inter-character gap $T_{1.5}$**.

**Step 1 — Express the time per byte at the given baud rate.**

The baud rate defines the number of **signal changes per second**. With 11 bits per character:

$$
t_{byte} = \frac{11 \text{ bits/character}}{19{,}200 \text{ bits/second}}
$$

**Step 2 — Perform the division.**

$$
t_{byte} = \frac{11}{19{,}200} \text{ seconds} = 5.7291 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 572.92 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the 3.5-character silence gap.**

$$
T_{3.5} = 3.5 \times t_{byte} = 3.5 \times 572.92 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
T_{3.5} = 2005.2 \text{ µs} \approx 2.005 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 1.5-character inter-character gap.**

$$
T_{1.5} = 1.5 \times t_{byte} = 1.5 \times 572.92 \text{ µs}
$$

$$
T_{1.5} = 859.4 \text{ µs} \approx 0.859 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Any silence **> 2.005 ms** on the line is interpreted by receiving slaves as the **end of the current message**, and the next byte begins a new frame. If a gap **> 0.859 ms** occurs **within** a frame, the receiver treats it as a **framing error** and discards the message. This is why **high baud rates demand deterministic real-time serial drivers** — Linux/RTOS scheduling jitter can corrupt Modbus RTU timing.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Modbus RTU vs. ASCII vs. TCP

| Performance / Feature | Modbus RTU | Modbus ASCII | Modbus TCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Encoding** | Binary (8-bit) | ASCII Hex (7-bit) | Binary (8-bit) |
| **Frame start marker** | Silent interval ($T_{3.5}$) | Colon `:` character | TCP connection |
| **Frame end marker** | Silent interval ($T_{3.5}$) | CR + LF | TCP packet length |
| **Error check** | **CRC-16** (robust) | **LRC-8** (weak) | TCP checksum + IP checksum |
| **Throughput efficiency** | High (binary) | Low (~50% overhead) | Very high |
| **Max nodes per segment** | 32 (RS-485) | 32 (RS-485) | Practically limited by switches |
| **Speed** | Up to 115.2 kbps (RS-485) | Up to 115.2 kbps | 10/100/1000 Mbps Ethernet |
| **Distance** | 1200 m @ 9.6 kbps | 1200 m @ 9.6 kbps | 100 m per copper segment (extendable with fiber) |
| **Human readability** | Poor | Good (terminal-readable) | Poor (binary payload) |
| **Master/slave model** | Strict master-slave | Strict master-slave | Client-server (effectively master-slave) |
| **Modern usage** | Field devices, RTUs | Legacy, debugging | **DCS backbone, IIoT** |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Modbus TCP Master-Slave Transaction

**Scenario:** A SCADA master (Client) needs to read **Holding Registers 40010 to 40014** (5 registers = 10 bytes of data) from Slave ID **17** connected via Modbus TCP.

**Step 1 — Build the Modbus PDU.**

Function code for "Read Holding Registers" = **0x03**.
Starting address = 40010 - 40001 = **9** (offset from base 40001).
Quantity of registers = **5**.

$$
\text{PDU} = \underbrace{0x03}_{FC} \; \vert \; \underbrace{0x00 \; 0x09}_{Addr} \; \vert \; \underbrace{0x00 \; 0x05}_{Qty}
$$

PDU in hex: `03 00 09 00 05` (5 bytes total).

**Step 2 — Build the MBAP Header (7 bytes).**

The **MBAP (Modbus Application Protocol) Header** has four fields:

| Field | Size | Value (hex) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transaction ID | 2 bytes | `0x00 0x01` | Pairing request/response |
| Protocol ID | 2 bytes | `0x00 0x00` | Always 0 for Modbus |
| Length | 2 bytes | `0x00 0x06` | Bytes following (UnitID + PDU = 1 + 5 = 6) |
| Unit ID | 1 byte | `0x11` | Slave address 17 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Length field = Unit ID byte + PDU length.** Here: $1 + 5 = 6$ bytes.

**Step 3 — Assemble the full Modbus TCP ADU (12 bytes total).**

$$
\text{ADU} = \underbrace{00 \; 01 \; 00 \; 00 \; 00 \; 06 \; 11}_{MBAP \; (7 \; bytes)} \; \vert \; \underbrace{03 \; 00 \; 09 \; 00 \; 05}_{PDU \; (5 \; bytes)}
$$

Full hex: `00 01 00 00 00 06 11 03 00 09 00 05`.

**Step 4 — Encapsulate into a TCP segment and IP packet.**

The Modbus TCP ADU becomes the **payload of a TCP segment** with destination port **502**, which in turn is encapsulated in an **IP packet** with the slave's IP address, and finally in an **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame** with the slave's MAC address.

**Step 5 — Slave Response (Verification).**

The slave responds with function code `0x03`, a byte count `0x0A` (10 = 5 registers × 2 bytes), and the 10 bytes of register data, preceded by an MBAP header with mirrored Transaction ID and updated Length field of 0x000B (1 + 1 + 10 = 12 bytes following the Length field).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exception Responses:** If the slave detects an error (illegal function, illegal address, illegal value, slave device failure), it returns the same PDU but with the **MSB of the function code set to 1** and adds an **exception code** in the data field. Example: FC 0x03 with error becomes 0x83, followed by exception code (0x01, 0x02, 0x03, or 0x04).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Maximum Modbus TCP Frame Timing

**Problem:** A SCADA master polls 50 Modbus TCP slaves every 500 ms. Each request-response round-trip (RTT) averages **8 ms**. The TCP handshake adds **1.0 ms** per connection. Calculate the **total cycle time** $T_{cycle}$ and verify it meets the **500 ms** deadline.

**Step 1 — Compute per-slave transaction time.**

$$
T_{slave} = T_{handshake} + T_{RTT} = 1.0 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ ms} = 9 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by number of slaves.**

$$
T_{cycle} = N_{slaves} \times T_{slave} = 50 \times 9 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = 450 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the 500 ms deadline.**

$$
T_{cycle} = 450 \text{ ms} < 500 \text{ ms} \; \Rightarrow \; \text{DEADLINE MET with 50 ms margin}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization Strategy:** If deadline were tighter, the engineer could:
> 1. Use a **persistent TCP connection** (eliminates $T_{handshake}$): $T_{cycle} = 50 \times 8 = 400$ ms.
> 2. Use **Modbus TCP multicast** (publisher/subscriber pattern, IEC 62541).
> 3. Bump to **100 Mbps Ethernet** to reduce $T_{RTT}$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Industrial Ethernet Protocol Family vs. Standard Ethernet

| Feature | Standard Ethernet | EtherNet/IP | PROFINET | EtherCAT | Modbus TCP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard** | IEEE 802.3 | ODVA / CIP | PI / IEC 61784 | ETG / IEC 61158 | Modbus-IDA |
| **Real-time class** | None | RT (1–10 ms) | RT & IRT (≤ 1 ms) | Sub-µs (≤ 100 µs) | Soft RT (10–100 ms) |
| **Topology** | Star, tree | Star, ring, linear | Star, ring, line | Daisy-chain | Star |
| **Cabling** | Cat5e/Cat6 | Cat5e | Cat5e + Profinet | Cat5e | Cat5e |
| **Cycle time (typical)** | N/A | 1–10 ms | 0.25–10 ms | 12.5 µs – 1 ms | 10–100 ms |
| **Synchronization** | NTP (ms accuracy) | CIP Sync (IEEE 1588) | IRT with hardware | Distributed clocks | Best-effort |
| **Application fit in DCS** | Office / Engineering | Cell-level / Drive control | High-speed motion | Servo drives, machine | Process I/O, SCADA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "compare Ethernet and Modbus", students often confuse them. The correct framing is: **Ethernet is the transport** (layer 1+2+3+4), while **Modbus is the application language** (layer 7). Modbus can ride over RS-485, RS-232, **or** Ethernet. Modbus TCP = Modbus PDU encapsulated in TCP/IP over Ethernet.

### 4.4 Functional State Machine — Modbus Master Polling Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ConnectTCP: Send SYN to Slave IP Port 502
    ConnectTCP --> Connected: Receive SYN-ACK
    ConnectTCP --> Error: Timeout or RST
    Connected --> SendRequest: Build ADU + PDU
    SendRequest --> WaitResponse: Transmit over TCP
    WaitResponse --> ParseResponse: Receive ADU within timeout
    WaitResponse --> Timeout: No response in T_out
    ParseResponse --> ValidateFC: Check FC matches request
    ValidateFC --> ProcessData: FC valid and data extracted
    ValidateFC --> HandleException: FC indicates exception
    ProcessData --> Idle: Update process image
    HandleException --> Idle: Log error, raise alarm
    Timeout --> Idle: Increment retry counter
    Error --> Idle: Close socket, retry next cycle
    Idle --> [*]: Shutdown command
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** A Modbus master is **deterministic in cycle time** but **best-effort in delivery**. There is **no native retry mechanism** inside the Modbus application layer — the master must implement its own timeout-and-retry state machine. The diagram above shows the canonical pattern.
