# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Availability Formula from First Principles

> **Problem Statement:** A DCS controller is observed for $N$ cycles. It is operational for a total of $T_{up}$ hours and down for $T_{down}$ hours. Derive the availability expression.

**Step 1 — Define Instantaneous Availability.**

$$A(t) = 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if system is UP at instant } t \\
0 & \text{if system is DOWN at instant } t
\end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Time-averaged (Steady-State) Availability.**

$$A = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} A(t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the observation window.**

Let $T = T_{up} + T_{down}$. Then:

$$A = \frac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of reliability metrics.**

In a repairable system, the long-run fraction of uptime equals the ratio of mean uptime to total mean cycle time:

$$A = \frac{E[T_{up}]}{E[T_{up}] + E[T_{down}]} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using limits.**

- If MTTR $\to 0$ (instant repair): $A \to 1$ (perfect availability). ✓
- If MTTR $\to \infty$ (never repaired): $A \to 0$. ✓
- If MTBF $\to \infty$ (never fails): $A \to 1$. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary check passes** — formula is dimensionally and behaviourally consistent.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reliability of a Redundant (1oo2) Pair

> **Problem Statement:** Two identical DCS controller cards each have reliability $R$. They operate in **1-out-of-2** configuration (1oo2) — system is UP if *at least one* works. Derive the parallel reliability.

**Step 1 — Probability that *both* fail.**

$$P(\text{both fail}) = (1 - R)(1 - R) = (1 - R)^2$$

**Step 2 — Probability that *at least one* works (1oo2 / parallel).**

$$R_{1oo2} = 1 - (1 - R)^2$$

**Step 3 — Expand for clarity.**

$$R_{1oo2} = 1 - (1 - 2R + R^2) = 2R - R^2$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

For $R = 0.9$ per card:

$$R_{1oo2} = 2(0.9) - (0.9)^2 = 1.8 - 0.81 = 0.99$$

So a single 90 %-reliable card becomes **99 %** in 1oo2 redundancy. ✓

**Step 5 — General parallel formula for $n$ identical modules.**

$$R_{nooN} = 1 - (1 - R)^n$$

For 2oo3 (any 2 of 3 must work):

$$R_{2oo3} = \binom{3}{2}R^2(1-R) + \binom{3}{3}R^3 = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Loop Execution Time Decomposition

> **Problem Statement:** A DCS executes a PID loop. Show how the total loop time is decomposed.

$$T_{loop} = T_{in} + T_{calc} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$$

- $T_{in}$ = I/O scan time (analog input conditioning, A/D)
- $T_{calc}$ = PID algorithm execution
- $T_{out}$ = Output update to final control element
- $T_{comm}$ = Network propagation (fieldbus, control network)

**Numerical Worked Example:**

- $T_{in} = 50$ ms, $T_{calc} = 20$ ms, $T_{out} = 30$ ms, $T_{comm} = 100$ ms  
- $T_{loop} = 50 + 20 + 30 + 100 = 200$ ms

**Performance Verdict:** This is *acceptable* for thermal process loops (which have time constants of minutes), but **marginal** for compressor surge control (where $\le 20$ ms is required).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Combined Plant Availability (Series Reliability of Subsystems)

> **Problem Statement:** A plant consists of three subsystems in series from a control-availability viewpoint: DCS controller (A = 0.999), Sensor network (A = 0.995), Final control element / valve (A = 0.99). Find overall plant availability.

**Step 1 — Series availability rule (independent failures).**

$$A_{plant} = A_{DCS} \cdot A_{sensor} \cdot A_{valve}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$A_{plant} = 0.999 \times 0.995 \times 0.990$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$A_{plant} = 0.999 \times 0.98505 = 0.98406 \approx 98.41\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

Even if the DCS itself is 99.9 % available, the *overall* plant availability drops to **98.41 %** because the weakest link (valves) drags it down. This is the **domo effect** of reliability engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU exam:** *The weakest subsystem dominates overall plant availability.* If you can only afford to upgrade one component, upgrade the **lowest-reliability** one — not the most expensive one.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — SIL and PFDavg Relationship

> **Problem Statement:** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is required to have SIL-2. Calculate the maximum allowable PFDavg.

**Step 1 — From SIL-2 band.**

$$10^{-3} \leq PFD_{avg} \leq 10^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Take the worst-case (most stringent, i.e., the lower PFD).**

$$PFD_{avg} = 10^{-3} \; \text{(boundary, conservative)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute RRF.**

$$RRF = \frac{1}{PFD_{avg}} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The safety system must reduce risk by a factor of **1000** — i.e., without the SIF a hazardous event is expected ~1000 times more often.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — DCS vs PLC vs SCADA Performance

> **Five-axis comparison** (axes marked in the order KTU 2024 examiner commonly tests):

| Performance Axis | DCS | PLC | SCADA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability (typical)** | 99.99 % – 99.999 % | 99.9 % – 99.99 % | 99.5 % – 99.9 % |
| **Loop Scan Time** | 100 ms – 1 s | 1 – 50 ms | 1 s – 60 s |
| **I/O Density per Controller** | 100 – 10 000 | 16 – 2048 | Limited (acquired via RTU) |
| **Geographic Spread** | Plant-wide | Local / machine | Wide-area (km) |
| **Programming Standard** | IEC 61131-3 + proprietary function blocks | IEC 61131-3 (LD, FBD, ST) | Vendor-specific, SCADA tags |
| **Hot Redundancy** | Built-in (1:1, 2:3) | Optional (rare) | Server-pair redundant |
| **Data Historian** | Integrated | External (rarely) | Integrated |
| **Process Safety Integration** | Native SIL support | Limited (safety PLC separate) | None |
| **Cost per I/O Point** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Best For** | Continuous processes | Discrete / motion | Telemetry / utilities |

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are framed strictly per the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech assessment pattern**: Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice). The "Choice" in Part B is provided as **Question A (14 M)** and **Question B (14 M)** — student answers any ONE.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**  
Define **Availability** of a DCS. State the formula and indicate what values of MTBF and MTTR are required to achieve "Five Nines" availability.

**Model Answer:**

> **Availability** is the fraction of time a system is in a functional state, expressed as:
> $$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$
> For "Five Nines" (99.999 %), the *ratio* MTTR / MTBF must be $\leq 10^{-5}$. Example: MTBF = 100 000 h, MTTR = 1 h gives $A = 0.99999$. This is achieved practically via hot-redundant controllers, hot-swappable I/O, and predictive maintenance.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**  
Differentiate between **MTTF** and **MTBF**. In which category does a DCS controller fall?

**Model Answer:**

> - **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** For *non-repairable* items (e.g., bulbs, sensors that are replaced on failure).
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** For *repairable* items (e.g., DCS controllers, I/O cards, network switches).
> - A DCS controller is a **repairable** system because faulty cards are *swapped* with spares and the unit is restored. Hence, **MTBF** is the applicable metric.
> Formula: $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`**  
**(a)** Explain the **six major performance criteria** used to evaluate a Distributed Control System. *(7 Marks)*  
**(b)** A DCS controller has MTBF = 40 000 hours and MTTR = 6 hours. Compute the **availability, unavailability, equivalent downtime per year**, and the **reliability at 1 000 hours** assuming a constant failure rate. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

1. **Availability** — fraction of time the system is functional. Achieved through redundancy, hot-swap, self-diagnostics. Formula: $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.

2. **Reliability** — probability of continuous correct operation for time $t$. For exponential failure: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. High-reliability components (MTBF $\gg$ mission time).

3. **Maintainability** — quantified by MTTR; minimized by modular design, hot-swap, built-in diagnostics. $MTTR = T_{detect} + T_{diagnose} + T_{replace} + T_{verify}$.

4. **Integrity / Safety** — compliance with **IEC 61508/61511 SIL** levels. Modern DCS supports SIL-2/3 for emergency shutdown systems.

5. **Speed of Response** — loop scan time, I/O update, network latency. Targets range from 100 ms (thermal) to 10 ms (compressor surge).

6. **Flexibility / Interoperability** — supports open protocols (OPC UA, Modbus, Foundation Fieldbus, Profinet). Allows modular expansion and multi-vendor integration.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** MTBF = 40 000 h, MTTR = 6 h, $t = 1000$ h, constant failure rate.

**Step 1 — Failure rate.**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{40000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{failures/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Availability.**

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{40000}{40000 + 6} = \frac{40000}{40006} = 0.99985 = 99.985\%$$

**Step 3 — Unavailability.**

$$1 - A = 1 - 0.99985 = 0.00015 = 0.015\%$$

**Step 4 — Downtime per year (8760 hours).**

$$T_{down} = (1 - A) \times 8760 = 0.00015 \times 8760 = 1.314 \; \text{hours/year}$$

**Step 5 — Reliability at 1000 hours.**

$$R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000} = e^{-0.025} = 0.9753 \approx 97.53\%$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Failure rate $\lambda$ | $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ / h |
| Availability $A$ | **99.985 %** |
| Unavailability | 0.015 % |
| Downtime per year | ~1.31 h |
| Reliability at 1000 h | 97.53 % |

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]`**  
**(a)** Compare **DCS, PLC, and SCADA** under the following performance criteria: availability, response time, I/O handling, geographic spread, programming standard, and typical applications. *(7 Marks)*  
**(b)** Two redundant DCS controller cards each have reliability $R = 0.92$. Compute the **1oo2** and **2oo2** parallel reliabilities. Comment on the trade-off. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

| Performance Criterion | **DCS** | **PLC** | **SCADA** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | 99.99 – 99.999 % (built-in redundancy) | 99.9 – 99.99 % (optional redundancy) | 99.5 – 99.9 % (server-pair redundant) |
| **Response Time** | 100 ms – 1 s | 1 – 50 ms | 1 s – 60 s |
| **I/O Handling** | 100 – 10 000 per controller | 16 – 2048 per rack | Limited (acquired via RTUs) |
| **Geographic Spread** | Plant-wide | Local / machine | Wide-area (km) |
| **Programming** | IEC 61131-3 + proprietary function-block libraries | IEC 61131-3 (LD, FBD, ST) | Vendor-specific tag-based configuration |
| **Typical Applications** | Refineries, power plants, chemical processes | Assembly lines, packaging, motion control | Pipelines, power T&D, water distribution |

**Conclusion:**  
- **DCS** is the natural choice for **continuous, plant-wide, high-availability** processes.  
- **PLC** is optimal for **fast, discrete, machine-level** control.  
- **SCADA** is suited for **wide-area supervisory monitoring** where direct real-time control is not the primary function.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — 1oo2 (One-out-of-Two) parallel reliability.**

$$R_{1oo2} = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (0.08)^2 = 1 - 0.0064 = 0.9936$$

So $R_{1oo2} = 99.36\%$.

**Step 2 — 2oo2 (Two-out-of-Two) — both must work.**

$$R_{2oo2} = R \times R = R^2 = (0.92)^2 = 0.8464$$

So $R_{2oo2} = 84.64\%$.

**Step 3 — Comparison and Trade-off.**

- **1oo2:** System is UP if at least one card works → **higher availability**, but if a faulty card outputs a "bad" signal, it could mislead. Used where fault tolerance is paramount and diagnostics are reliable.
- **2oo2:** Both must agree → **higher integrity / safety** (erroneous single signal is rejected), but lower availability. Used in **safety instrumented systems** (SIL-2 / SIL-3) where spurious trips are less costly than hazardous outputs.

**Trade-off Summary:** 1oo2 favours *availability*; 2oo2 favours *integrity*. A **2oo3** configuration is the optimal compromise in critical loops, voting on the majority of three identical cards.

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks (Per KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF** — Examiners *deduct 1 mark* if a student applies MTTF to a repairable DCS subsystem.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert "Five Nines" to numeric form** — Always state $99.999\% = 0.99999$ explicitly before plugging in.
> 3. **Wrong parallel/series formula application** — Series: multiply $R_i$. Parallel: $1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$. Examiners specifically test the **distinction**.
> 4. **Skipping units in final answer** — Always write "hours", "%", or "per year" alongside the number.
> 5. **Ignoring the role of redundancy** — Even if you compute a low $A$ for a single card, mention that **1:1 redundancy** boosts it; otherwise your answer is incomplete.
> 6. **In Q on SCADA vs DCS, missing the "supervisory vs direct control" distinction** — SCADA is a *monitoring* layer; DCS *directly controls* loops.
> 7. **In safety/SIL questions, not citing the IEC standard** — Always write "per IEC 61508/61511" to score the method mark.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Density Summary**

- **Availability** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ is the *single most-tested* performance metric in KTU papers.
- **Reliability** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ uses **constant failure rate** assumption (memoryless property).
- **MTTF** applies to *non-repairable* items; **MTBF** applies to *repairable* items (DCS controllers).
- **"Five Nines"** = 99.999 % availability → downtime ~5.26 min/year.
- **SIL** is governed by **IEC 61508/61511**; SIL-2 is the typical DCS safety target.
- **Loop execution time** $T_{loop} = T_{in} + T_{calc} + T_{out} + T_{comm}$ must be **less than one-tenth** of the process time constant for stable control.
- **Series reliability** of plant = product of subsystem availabilities; the **weakest link dominates**.
- **1oo2 redundancy** boosts availability; **2oo2 redundancy** boosts safety/integrity; **2oo3** is the engineering compromise.
- **DCS** = continuous process, plant-wide, high redundancy.  
  **PLC** = discrete, machine-level, fast.  
  **SCADA** = wide-area, supervisory, telemetry.
- **Purdue / ISA-95** model maps tools to **Levels 0–4**.
- **Open protocols** (OPC UA, Modbus, Foundation Fieldbus, Profinet, Ethernet/IP) define **interoperability**.
- **Hot-swap I/O**, **self-diagnostics**, and **watchdog timers** are the three engineering pillars that minimise MTTR.
- Always **cite the IEC standard** in safety/integrity answers for full marks.
- **Final answer units and assumption statements** are compulsory; examiners *expect* to see them.

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — PERFORMANCE CRITERIA FOR DCS AND OTHER AUTOMATION TOOLS**
